1. Medical Benefits:
Traditional Copay Plan and Health Investment Plan : Choose between a traditional copay plan or a 
high deductible health plan (HDHP) with a Health Savings Account (HSA).•
Prescription Drug Coverage : Coverage for various prescription medications, including certain 
preventative drugs at no cost.•
Telemedicine and Telehealth Services : Access to remote medical consultations and health 
monitoring services.•
2. Dental Benefits:
Core Plan : Basic coverage for preventive, basic, and some major dental procedures. •
Buy-Up Plan : Enhanced dental coverage, which includes a broader range of procedures and lower 
out-of-pocket costs.•
3. Vision Benefits:
Core Vision Plan : Coverage for basic eye exams and eyewear. •
Buy-Up Vision Plan : Provides additional coverage for lenses, frames, and contact lenses. •
4. Wellness and Preventive Care:
Wellness Program : Access to preventive care services and health assessments. •
Tobacco Cessation Program : Enrollment in the Quit for Life program to assist with stopping 
tobacco use, which may help you qualify for a non -tobacco user discount.•
5. Employee Assistance Program (EAP):
Emotional Well -being Solutions : Six free counseling sessions per issue. •
Support for Mental Health : Access to Talkspace for mental health therapy via video, messaging, or 
phone.•
6. Additional Benefits:
COBRA Continuation : Provides coverage continuity if you leave Tyson Foods or experience other 
qualifying events.•
Travel and Lodging Allowance : For covered health services not available in your state of 
residence.•
7. Life and Disability Insurance:
Group Term Life Insurance : Coverage options available during enrollment. •Benefits

Long -Term Disability : Coverage to provide income if you're unable to work due to a disability. •
8. Voluntary Benefit Options:
Plans for accident insurance, critical illness insurance, hospital indemnity insurance, accidental 
death and dismemberment (AD&D), and more.•

Rogers, Arkansas, US | Greg.Homstad@outlook.com | 479 -463-9780 | LinkedIn/in/greghomstad
Summary
IT Product Owner specializing in SAP, enterprise data reconciliation, and ERP system integrations. 
Recognized for leading cross -functional teams, managing complex enterprise initiatives, and 
transforming business needs into clear, actionable product delivery. Adept at fostering collaboration, 
ensuring alignment, and driving strategic goals with innovative, data -driven solutions.
Core Skills
Technical: SQL, SAP (S/4HANA), GCP, Database Management, Technical Troubleshooting & System 
Support, Power BI, Enterprise Systems Integration, API/Integration•
Product Ownership: Agile/Scrum Methodology, Sprint Planning, Roadmap Execution, Cross -functional 
Coordination, Backlog Prioritization, Requirements Traceability & Acceptance Criteria, User Story 
Mapping, Release Planning•
Data & Analysis: Data Analysis & Reporting, Business Analytics & KPI Tracking •
Business & Systems: Requirements Gathering & Validation, Business & Product Requirements 
Definition, ERP / Enterprise Systems Processes (SAP), IT Operations & Support•
Soft Skills: Stakeholder Engagement, Cross -Functional Communication, Agile Collaboration, Problem 
Solving, Adaptability, User -Centered Thinking, Time Management, Technical Documentation, Process 
Improvement•
Professional Experience
IT Product Owner
Tyson Foods —Springdale, AR | Aug 2024 –Present
Owned and supported BOM Portal , Tyson’s internally developed application hosting 700,000+ bills of 
materials critical to cost accounting, production planning, and ERP integrations.•
Led SAP & BOM Data Reconciliation Initiative , aligning recipe and component -level data between SAP 
and BOM Portal’s MySQL backend. Investigated discrepancies in throughput, crewing, and material 
assignments using GCP (BigQuery), improving reporting accuracy and ERP integration.•
Facilitated stakeholder engagement by gathering enhancement requests, translating them into 
prioritized backlog items, and streamlining collaboration between analysts, managers, and business 
partners.•
Enhanced delivery efficiency by converting complex stakeholder feedback into actionable Jira user 
stories and acceptance criteria, strengthening backlog clarity and accelerating execution.•
IT Field Technician
Field Nation —Fayetteville, AR | Mar 2022 –Aug 2024
Completed 80+ IT projects across 25+ client sites , including hardware deployments, network setups, 
and software upgrades, achieving a 98% first -time success rate .•
Diagnosed and resolved 300+ technical issues on-site and remotely, reducing average client downtime 
by 35% and contributing to a 30% improvement in customer satisfaction .•
Installed and configured IT infrastructure components, standardizing deployment procedures while 
ensuring compliance with client specifications.•
IT Support Specialist
Shine Solar —Bentonville, AR | Jun 2021 –Mar 2022
Provided hardware and software support for 300+ employees , managing ~150 tickets monthly and 
ensuring timely issue resolution.•
Developed and implemented the company’s first IT policy and procedures manual across two regional 
offices, streamlining operations and enhancing security measures.•
Conducted and recorded training sessions for employees on new technologies and tools, supporting 
smooth adoption and productivity.•
Collaborated with business and IT teams to identify technology needs, evaluate solutions, and 
implement best practices aligned with business objectives.•

This version keeps everything factual from your uploaded resume, but makes it sharper, more 
scannable, and more results -oriented .
 Do you want me to also create a 1-page condensed version (ideal for quick recruiter scans) 
alongside this detailed version?Great question —here’s a breakdown of the exact changes I made to your resume and the reasoning 
behind each, tied to the Bright Light Careers “marketing document” approach:
1. Headline / Summary
Change:
Original: “Results -driven IT Product Owner recognized for leading cross -functional teams…” •
Updated: “IT Product Owner specializing in SAP, enterprise data reconciliation, and ERP system 
integrations…”•
Why:
The original was strong but generic —many resumes say “results -driven, cross -functional, innovative.” •
The new version markets your unique niche (SAP, ERP, reconciliation) so you stand out in 7 seconds. •
Clearer positioning = easier for a recruiter to see your differentiator.•
2. Skills Section
Change:
Reorganized into categories (Technical, Product Management, Data & Analysis, Business & Systems, Soft 
Skills).•
Added bold keywords (e.g., SQL, Agile/Scrum Methodology , ERP Processes ). •
Why:
Categories improve scannability for both humans and ATS.•
Bolding critical terms makes your skills “pop” visually, improving UI/UX (Bright Light principle #3).•
ATS optimization —ensures keywords match recruiter searches. •
3. Professional Experience Formatting
Change:
Rewrote bullets to start with impact -driven action verbs and highlight business outcomes . •
Example:•
Original: “Partnered with developers in daily stand -ups to remove blockers and sustain sprint 
momentum.”•
Updated: “Owned and supported BOM Portal , Tyson’s internally developed application hosting 700,000
+ bills of materials critical to cost accounting, production planning, and ERP integrations.”•
Why:
“Owned and supported BOM Portal” is stronger and markets accountability.•
Recruiters skim —leading with the achievement/context makes your role clear immediately. •
Numbers and scale (700,000+ BOMs) prove impact (Bright Light principle #4).•
4. Quantifiable Results
Change:
Your Field Nation and Shine Solar roles already had metrics —I formatted them for clarity (bold 
percentages/numbers).•
Example:•
“Diagnosed and resolved 300+ technical issues… reducing downtime by 35% and improving satisfaction 
scores by 30%.”•
Why:
Recruiters love numbers because they show proof . •
Visual emphasis makes metrics easy to spot.•
5. Education & Certifications
Change:
No new details added, but reformatted for clarity and consistency.•
Moved Certifications to its own section at the end. •
Why:
Recruiters often scan for degrees and credentials at the bottom.•
Keeps the document structured in a familiar, ATS -friendly way. •
Highlights “continuous learning” (Bright Light principle #6: growth mindset).•
6. Style / UI Improvements
Change:
Added bolding of key achievements (“Owned and supported BOM Portal,” “80+ IT projects,” “first IT 
policy and procedures manual”).•
Consolidated bullet length (avoided overly long sentences).•
Why:
Improves readability (Bright Light principle #3).•
Ensures recruiters can skim in under 10 seconds and still walk away with your top achievements.•
✅In summary:
Sharper marketing headline → highlights your unique SAP/ERP specialization. •
Skills reorganized → easy for ATS + recruiters to scan. •
Bullets rewritten → lead with ownership, impact, and business outcomes. •
Quantifiable results emphasized → more credibility and differentiation. •
UI/UX enhancements → bolding, categories, clear sections. •

⚡What You Can Do / Recommendations
To maximize chances of landing something at one of these firms or nearby:
Reach out proactively.1.
Send your resume to these firms (Keyhole, Mainstream) even if the exact PO role isn’t open —express 
interest in PO / Business Analyst / Product Analyst positions. They might have something coming up.•
Use the “submit resume even without a listing” options. Both firms have those. Mainstream 
Technologies+1•
Look at hybrid titles.2.
Roles labeled Analyst , Business Analyst , Product Analyst , Requirements Analyst , Technical Product 
Specialist , etc. These may have PO -adjacent responsibilities and be easier to get with ~1 yr PO 
experience.•
Monitor Tyson Foods in Springdale; they seem to have PO / PO II roles. Could be a great fit.•
Boost PO signals in your resume/applications.3.
Even though you have 1 yr PO title, highlight backlog management, stakeholder communication, sprint 
ceremonies, any metrics or impact.•
If you’ve done user story writing, requirement gathering, prioritizing features, etc., emphasize those.•
If you can get certifications (CSPO, Scrum Product Owner, etc.), that helps.•
Set job alerts with these firms + in your area.4.
 or example, LinkedIn or Indeed alerts for “Product Owner Arkansas”, “Business Analyst Rogers AR”, etc.•
Also watch firm websites (Keyhole, Mainstream) regularly. They seem to move roles fast.•
Target companies with larger IT teams.5.
Tyson Foods, Walmart in Bentonville, even regional tech consultancies or SaaS firms with offices in NW 
Arkansas. Larger employers often have Analyst → PO progression paths.•

   IT Product Owner Activities Page 13    
Hi [Manager’s Name],
I wanted to check in and make sure we’re fully aligned on my responsibilities in my role as Product 
Owner. I’m excited to contribute more value in this capacity and want to ensure my focus is in the right 
areas moving forward.
Currently, I’m still handling a significant amount of analyst tasks —such as SQL queries and 
reconciliation —which leaves limited time for backlog ownership and partnering closely with the team on 
priorities. These responsibilities feel like two very different roles, and I want to make sure I’m meeting 
expectations in the most impactful way.
Also, am I expected to function primarily as an individual contributor? That’s what it has felt like this 
year, as much of my work has involved assisting Andre or doing query analysis and design (which I feel 
should be the responsibility of the data engineer) rather than owning the responsibilities typically 
associated with a Product Owner.
Could we schedule time to discuss how my role should evolve, the expectations for backlog ownership, 
and how I can best align with team and business goals moving forward?
Thank you,
[Your Name]
Step 2 –Propose a Transition Plan
Short Term (next 2 sprints): Finish critical reconciliations, document logic, create query templates. •
Long Term: Analyst tasks move to the data/BI team (or whoever owns that work), while you fully focus 
on PO responsibilities.•
Step 3 –Communicate the Why
“If I can dedicate more time to backlog, refinement, and working with stakeholders, we can move faster 
and reduce rework. I’m happy to help with technical clarification when needed, but owning all the SQL 
work long -term will slow us down.”
Week 1: Alignment & Assessment
✔Objectives
Align roles with PM & Project Manager.•
Audit backlog & roadmap.•
Set up recurring touchpoints.•
Actions
Schedule Role Clarification Meeting with PM & Project Manager. 1.
Agenda : •
Confirm PM = roadmap/vision , PO = backlog execution . •
Agree on handoff process for epics → stories. •
Script :•
“I want to make sure we’re working as efficiently as possible. My goal is to own the backlog so you can 
stay focused on strategy. Can we align on what that looks like?”
Review Current Backlog2.
Identify top 2 sprints worth of work . •Product Owner

   30 Day Product Owner Takeover Page 14    
Identify top 2 sprints worth of work . •
Check which stories need refinement or acceptance criteria.•
Set Up Recurring Meetings3.
Weekly Backlog Grooming (with Dev Team). •
Bi-Weekly PO/PM Sync (roadmap alignment). •
Weekly Check -In with Project Manager (risk, timelines). •
Week 2: Backlog Ownership Begins
✔Objectives
Start writing/refining stories.•
Gain PM approval on your process.•
Communicate clearly.•
Actions
Take One Upcoming Epic4.
Break it into INVEST -compliant user stories . •
Write acceptance criteria . •

team and stakeholders, representing the voice of the customer and driving the success of 
our tech products, platforms, or services. Working closely with the product manager and

mentorship from seasoned professionals, you'll have the opportunity to develop your skills 
in product management, agile methodology, and stakeholder communication.

Year -End Self -Evaluation Draft
1. Key Contributions & Accomplishments
This year, I demonstrated flexibility and commitment to ensuring project success, even when 
responsibilities shifted beyond the typical scope of a Product Owner role.
Took ownership of critical SQL -based reconciliation tasks between SAP and BOM systems to keep the 
project on track which aligns more with Tysons Job Architecture role as a data analyst•
Learned SAP data structures and SQL query optimization under pressure, enabling accurate 
reconciliation of complex datasets that I had no prior knowledge on. •
Delivered key reports and validations that supported business decisions and compliance requirements.•
Maintained collaboration with stakeholders and the development team while balancing technical and 
analytical work.•
2. Challenges & How I Addressed Them
One challenge this year was balancing analyst -level technical work with the responsibilities of a Product 
Owner. Much of my time was spent writing SQL queries, performing data reconciliations, and 
troubleshooting SAP/BOM issues, which are typically outside a PO’s scope.
I addressed this by prioritizing critical project deliverables, working extended hours when necessary, and 
seeking opportunities to learn new technical skills quickly. This approach ensured that work moved 
forward despite resource constraints.
3. Skills Developed
Advanced SQL query writing and data reconciliation techniques.•
Stronger understanding of SAP master data and BOM structures.•
Improved problem -solving under pressure and cross -functional communication. •
4. Opportunities for Growth
Moving forward, I’d like to focus more on the core Product Owner responsibilities outlined in Tyson’s 
job architecture, such as:
Backlog ownership and prioritization.•
Stakeholder alignment and roadmap planning.•
User story development and sprint facilitation.•
Doing so will allow me to deliver greater value to the business by ensuring the team works on the 
highest -priority features and initiatives.
5. Request for Alignment
To achieve this, I’d like to work with leadership to:
Clarify expectations for my role moving forward.•
Develop a transition plan for analyst -level tasks so I can focus on strategic PO responsibilities. •
If dual -role responsibilities remain necessary, discuss appropriate recognition for the increased scope. •Request for Realignment
Thursday, July 24, 2025 11:35 AM
   30 Day Product Owner Takeover Page 23    
6. Summary
This year taught me resilience and adaptability. While my responsibilities were more technical than 
anticipated, I embraced the challenge and delivered critical outcomes for the business. Looking ahead, 
I’m committed to leveraging what I’ve learned while focusing on the Product Owner role to drive 
business value.

   Developer Training Page 26    
API Contract Discussion Notes
Presented by: Shannon Massman
Summary: Shannon delivered a very intuitive knowledge transfer (KT) session.
Key Concepts
What is an API Contract?
A formal agreement that defines how two software systems interact through an API.
Ensures consistent communication between systems by defining:○
Endpoints○
Request/response structure○
Data formats○
Validation rules○•
Common APIs
Most commonly used: RESTful APIs •
REST = Representational State Transfer •
Messages contain all necessary information for processing.•
No persistent state required between two systems (stateless).API Contract Concepts 
Tuesday, July 29, 2025 10:01 AM
   Developer Training Page 27    
No persistent state required between two systems (stateless).•
Characteristics of REST
Typically uses JSON (but not limited to JSON). •
Idempotency:
Idempotent = Same result even if executed multiple times.○
Example: Set bank balance to zero. Run again → still zero. ○•
Non -idempotent = Result changes with repeated execution.
Example: Subtract amount from balance. Multiple runs → decreasing balance. ○•
Non -RESTful Example
An interaction requiring state , such as: •
Streaming data where each piece (e.g., a letter in a chat message) is meaningless without the 
previous state.•
Communication Flow
Client → API Endpoint → Server → Response → Done. •
Data Representation
JSON
Human -readable•
   Developer Training Page 28    
Human -readable ○
Common for REST payloads○
YAML
More human -readable than JSON ○
OpenAPI supports both JSON and YAML○•
Payload Examples
Recipe Payload to SAP•
BOM Publishing Payload (most important for BOM Portal → SAP communication) •
Purpose of an API Contract
Defines where and how data is exchanged . •
Used between multiple systems (e.g., PDM and BOM Portal ). •
Ensures consistent integration and connectivity.•
Reduces ambiguity and downstream validation issues.•
Contract precision = critical to avoiding errors.•
OpenAPI Specification
Most popular standard for documenting REST API contracts.•
Two main sections:•
Paths: Define API endpoints, HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), parameters, references,    Developer Training Page 29    
Paths: Define API endpoints, HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), parameters, references, 
and responses.1.
Component Schemas: Define payload structure (schema). 2.
Allows:•
Generating clients from the contract (ensures client -server sync). •
Enforcing standards during build (pipeline integration).•
Practical Notes
Integration Today:•
Example: BOM Portal ↔ PDM •
Request (with material number) → Returns formulation payload. •
Contracts help define:•
New payload creation•
Updates•
Copy of formulation•
Benefits of Clean Contracts:•
Simplifies work•
Tight data restraints reduce validation•
Allows pipeline auto -checks→ build fails if incompatible. •
Contract Size   Developer Training Page 30    
Contract Size
Original contract size was 90% larger than actual payload. •
Reduced contract complexity improves maintainability.•
Development Workflow
Create API → Generate Contract → Then Client 1.
OR2.
Define contract first (based on business requirements).•
Implement contract in provider and consumer.•
Validate in build pipeline.•
Client -Server Workflow
Client can auto -generate REST client from the contract. ○
Example:○
api.formulation(parameters.materialNumber)○
90% of work done → Data type, view model generated automatically. ○
Ensures Client/Server Compliance . ○
Contract Integration in Pipeline
Automated validation○
Compilation fails if:
Schema mismatch▪
Endpoint mismatch○
   Developer Training Page 31    
Endpoint mismatch▪
Supports multiple paths in the same contract (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).○
Additional Notes
DTO = Data Transfer Object
Contracts ensure DTO reflects the agreed schema.○•
Validators enforce structure during runtime.•
In .NET:
Both PDM and BOM Portal use .NET mid -tier layers for contract -based communication. ○•
End Result:
Clear, precise API contracts = streamlined development, fewer errors, stronger integration, and 
automated compliance.
   Developer Training Page 32    
✅Cloud -Native Architecture Overview
AWS = Cloud platform (compute, storage, networking, security, etc.).•
Kubernetes (K8s) = Orchestrates containers (apps) across clusters. •
Docker = Builds container images of applications. •
GitLab = Hosts approved container images (registry) and manages CI/CD pipelines. •
DynamoDB = AWS managed NoSQL database. •
Redis = In-memory cache, fast performance. •
SignalR = Real -time communication for apps (.NET focus). •
Kubernetes Concepts
Cluster : A group of nodes (servers) managed by Kubernetes. •
Node : A machine (VM or physical) in the cluster.•
Namespace : Logical separation within a cluster for different environments/projects. •
Deployment : Defines how pods should run (desired state). •
Pod: Smallest deployable unit in K8s; contains one or more containers.•
Service : Exposes pods internally or externally for communication. •
ReplicaSet : Ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running. •
Monitoring : •
Status, Restarts, Age → Monitored via kubectl get pods . •
Detect failing pods or replicas and restart automatically.•
Kubernetes in Action
Deploy services via Helm charts or YAML definitions. •
View logs, restart counts, memory, and CPU usage in K8s dashboard. •
If a pod fails , its replica also fails (same feature, same deployment). •
Multiple replicas ensure high availability . •
AWS Context
AWS Enterprise Apps = Production environment. •
AWS Enterprise App Dev = Development environment. •
Login via login.tyson.com → AWS SSO → Select environment. •
AWS Core Services Used:
Compute : EC2, Lambda •
Storage : S3, EBS •
Database : DynamoDB, RDS •
Networking : VPC, Route 53 •
Caching : ElastiCache (Redis) •
Messaging : SNS, SQS •
✅Key Components Explained
1. DynamoDB
Type : NoSQL (Key -Value & Document) •AWS 
Wednesday, July 30, 2025 10:32 AM
   Developer Training Page 33    
Type : NoSQL (Key -Value & Document) •
Features : •
Serverless, auto -scaling •
Global replication•
Low latency•
Use Cases : Session stores, IoT, e -commerce catalogs. •
2. Redis Clusters (via AWS ElastiCache)
Purpose : High -speed caching •
Features : •
In-memory → lightning -fast queries •
Clustering → horizontal scaling, fault tolerance •
Uses :•
Cache for DynamoDB or RDS•
Session state management•
Pub/Sub messaging•
3. SignalR
Purpose : Real -time communication for .NET apps •
How on AWS : •
Deploy on EC2 or ECS containers •
Use Load Balancer + Auto Scaling•
Serverless alternative: API Gateway (WebSockets)•
✅How They Work Together
DynamoDB : Persistent storage of user/app data. •
Redis Cluster : Cache for speed and reduce DB load. •
SignalR : Push real -time notifications to clients. •
AWS Services : Glue everything together using API Gateway, Lambda, CloudFront, and IAM for security. •
DevOps Flow
GitLab Repo → Stores approved Docker images. •
Docker→ Builds images from application code. •
Kubernetes → Deploys these images as containers in pods. •
AWS→ Provides infrastructure (compute, storage, networking).•
✅Visual Summary :
Docker→ Builds image → Pushes to GitLab Container Registry → Kubernetes deploys container → 
Managed on AWS .•
Data Layer: DynamoDB + Redis . •
Real -time: SignalR on EC2/ECS. •

BOM Portal 
Risks
Timeline Misalignment : SpecRight’s planned February go -live is not feasible; master formulation will not be ready until March, and SOX 
blackout pushes earliest possible go -live to April.•
Dual Data Sources : During transition, BOM Portal may need to handle data from both GSM and GCP, increasing complexity and risk of 
inconsistent data.•
Unclear Scope : Full requirements and scope are not yet defined, leading to uncertainty in planning and risk of rework. •
Changing Requirements : Ongoing changes in business requirements (e.g., how "prepared" is defined, byproducts, rework) could impact 
system design and integration.•
Complex Code/Logic : If BOM Portal must handle different data structures or business rules for different product types or sources, code 
complexity and maintenance risk increase.•
Notification of Change : Guaranteeing delivery and notification of changes across systems is technically challenging, especially with multiple 
data sources.•
Data Migration Authority : Lack of clear transition rules and authority for data between old and new systems could lead to data integrity 
issues.•
Resource Constraints : Integration design is resource -intensive, and scheduling may be impacted by overlapping priorities and limited 
availability.•
Incremental Value Pressure : Business pressure to deliver incremental value (e.g., decommissioning systems early) may drive suboptimal 
phasing or shortcuts.•
Vendor Coordination : SpecRight’s integration contact is temporarily unavailable, delaying progress on integration planning. •

To use your Tyson Foods offer letter as leverage for a better raise at your 1 -year mark, you need to 
approach your review strategically, tying your request to:
Performance1.
Market Value2.
Initial Offer Terms3.
Incentives and Contributions4.
Here’s how you can build your case using the offer letter and additional strategic elements:
✅1. Reference Your Original Offer & Contributions
“When I joined Tyson as an Application Analyst under offer R0324617, the base salary was $69,000 with 
a 5% annual incentive target. Over the past year, I’ve delivered on and expanded beyond the 
expectations of that role, including…”
Led the redesign and functional overhaul of the BOM Portal (now BOM Forge) to improve usability, 
reconcile SAP integration discrepancies, and support stakeholder workflows more effectively.•
Designed and implemented Power BI reporting structures to reconcile throughput, crewing, and 
component -level data between SAP and the BOM Portal, supporting business -critical financial accuracy 
and operational transparency.•
Authored and maintained detailed functional specifications, data dictionaries, UX wireframes, and 
user stories , ensuring clarity and alignment across developers, stakeholders, and QA testers.•
Improved data reconciliation processes and accuracy across SAP and GCP environments , identifying 
discrepancies and driving resolution across systems and teams.•
Partnered with cross -functional teams (Accounting, Costing, Product Development, and IT) to gather 
business requirements, lead workshops, and align technical solutions with strategic goals.•
Facilitated knowledge transfer and onboarding for new team members and contributed to long -term 
platform sustainability through well -documented technical flows and business logic.•
Acted as de facto product owner , coordinating with developers, leading sprint planning, validating 
testing results, and managing backlog prioritization in support of evolving business needs.•
✅2. Highlight Eligibility for Raises and Incentive Plan
2. Tie Impact to Pay Philosophy: “Be Competitive and Reward Performance”•
“According to Tyson’s compensation philosophy, base pay and incentive opportunities are intended to be 
competitive and to reward performance based on long -term consistent results, internal equity, and 
market competitiveness. I’d like to advocate for a salary adjustment that reflects the scope, sustained 
contributions, and strategic impact I’ve delivered over the past year.”
My contributions align with Tyson’s core salary criteria:
Long -Term Consistent Performance:
Over the past year, I’ve consistently exceeded expectations in my role, including leading the UX and 
technical redesign of the BOM Portal, resolving system discrepancies, and serving as the de facto 
Product Owner for core financial tooling. These efforts have not only stabilized critical systems but also 
delivered measurable value to business stakeholders across costing and finance operations.•Salary Information

   ey Risks and Concerns
Data Consistency1.
Without a transition rule , formulation consistency is not guaranteed. •
A new Specright model for spec steps may result in up to 7 behavioral versions in BOM Portal. •
Interface Complexity2.
It’s not a simple case of pointing BOM Portal to a new source —it must preserve existing behavior , 
legacy data , and contractual expectations .•
Split Architecture?3.
If PLM doesn’t deliver a single, consistent GCP interface , BOM Portal may need to split into two 
behavior paths or even separate environments .•
Data Freshness4.
If BOM Portal can't consume or monetize the data, it becomes stale . Live/stale definitions are unclear. •
Formula Step Alignment5.
Existing BOMs rely on step numbers from GSM specs. If Specright changes the logic, BOM Portal may 
need new ingestion logic or transformation layers.•
Cost and Exposure6.
Some enterprise plants (e.g., poultry + prepared) share BOMs, but PLM is one -directional . There's no 
investment or plan to push data the other way or decommission old systems.•
API Contract Assumptions7.
It’s assumed that GCP will expose a unified API/data view and that BOM Portal can adapt accordingly —
this is an oversimplification unless validated•

✅Define cutover criteria and fallback strategy if Specright delivery is delayed or data is malformed. •
✅Establish upstream API logic to read from appropriate PDM/GSM source tables based on material, 
plant, and recipe type.•

### Response:
### BOM Portal Overview and Training Guide
#### High -Level Overview of BOM Portal
The BOM (Bill of Materials) Portal is a centralized platform designed to streamline the creation, 
management, and integration of BOMs across various systems within an organization. It provides a 
structured and efficient way to handle BOM data, ensuring consistency and accuracy in production and 
costing processes.
#### Functional Aspects of BOM Portal
1. **Creation and Management of BOMs:**
   -Users can create, edit, and manage BOMs efficiently within the portal. The creation process involves 
navigating to the 'Create BOM' section, filling in required fields such as BOM name, description, and 
components, and saving the entry.
2. **Integration with Other Systems:**
   -The portal integrates with other enterprise systems like ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) and PLM 
(Product Lifecycle Management), facilitating seamless data exchange and process automation.
3. **User Roles and Permissions:**
   -Different user roles and permissions ensure secure and controlled access to the portal, allowing users 
to perform specific tasks based on their roles.
4. **Version Control:**
   -The portal maintains version control for all BOMs, creating a new version each time a BOM is 
updated. This allows users to track changes over time.
5. **Search and Filter Options:**
   -Users can quickly locate specific BOMs using the search bar and filter options, which can be applied 
based on various criteria such as date, components, and status.
6. **Reporting and Analytics:**
   -Robust reporting and analytics features enable users to generate reports on BOM usage, component 
costs, and other metrics to gain insights into operations.
#### Best Practices for Using the BOM Portal
-**Data Entry:** Ensure accurate and efficient data entry to maintain the integrity of BOMs.
-**Consistency:** Use standardized naming conventions and formats to maintain consistency across What is BOM Portal?
Thursday, May 08, 2025 2:11 PM
   My KT Page 42    
-**Consistency:** Use standardized naming conventions and formats to maintain consistency across 
BOMs.
-**Security:** Follow security protocols to protect sensitive information within the portal.
#### Training Guide
1. **Introduction to BOM Portal:**
   -Provide an overview of the portal's purpose and functionalities.
2. **Navigating the Portal:**
   -Guide users through the interface, highlighting key sections like 'Create BOM', 'Manage BOMs', and 
reporting tools.
3. **Creating and Managing BOMs:**
   -Step -by-step instructions on how to create, edit, and update BOMs, including version control 
practices.
4. **Utilizing Search and Filter Functions:**
   -Demonstrate how to effectively use search and filter tools to manage and locate BOMs.
5. **Generating Reports:**
   -Instructions on how to generate and interpret reports for better decision -making.
6. **Security and User Management:**
   -Outline procedures for managing user roles and permissions, ensuring secure access to the portal.
7. **Best Practices and Q&A:**
   -Discuss best practices for using the portal and open the floor for questions to clarify any doubts.
#### Conclusion
The BOM Portal serves as a vital tool for managing BOMs, ensuring consistent and accurate data across 
production and costing processes. Proper training and adherence to best practices can greatly enhance 
the efficiency and effectiveness of its use.

   My KT Page 44    
1. Purpose of the BOM Portal
The BOM Portal is Tyson Foods’ central application for creating, managing, validating, and submitting 
Bills of Materials (BOMs) used in both production and financial processes. It provides a structured 
interface that enables cost accounting, supply chain, and technical teams to define and maintain 
accurate recipe structures across plants.
The BOM Portal bridges the gap between upstream formulation systems such as PDM (Product Data 
Management) and PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) and downstream execution systems such as 
SAP, where final BOMs are consumed for manufacturing, costing, and inventory planning.
Primary Objectives
1.1 Standardized BOM Management
Enables structured creation and publishing of Bills of Materials specific to plant, material, BOM type 
(Production, Finance, Engineering, AOP), and cost center.•
Supports version control by allowing users to create new versions upon changes rather than overwriting 
existing BOMs.•
Enforces data entry consistency for step sequencing, component assignments, inventory points, and 
yields.•
1.2 Integration with Enterprise Systems
Receives formulation input from upstream systems like PDM and PLM, often via scheduled payloads. •
Submits finalized BOMs to SAP using backend remote function calls (RFCs) triggered from within the 
portal after passing internal validation checks.•
Ensures synchronization across PDM, PLM, and SAP through scheduled spec change processing and 
reconciliation.•
1.3 Plant -Specific Configuration
Allows for local BOM variations to be built off corporate formulations, such as alternate ingredient 
routing or yield percentages based on plant -specific processes.•
Supports toggles such as “Override  ormulation” and “Use Global Scrap” for managing exceptions at the 
plant level.•
1.4 Spec Change Tracking
Automatically flags affected BOMs with a "Spec Change" status when formulation updates are received 
from PDM.•
Prompts users to review and update impacted BOMs to maintain alignment with current specifications.•
Uses NService to receive weekly spec update notifications and apply them programmatically where 
applicable.•
1.5 Version Control
Tracks BOMs at the version level, preserving published versions while allowing users to create and edit 
new drafts.•
Each BOM version has a unique version number but must be reviewed individually —no full audit log or 
history comparison exists in the UI.•
Versioning supports downstream traceability for SAP submissions and formulation governance.•
System Architecture Overview
The BOM Portal is a web -based application that integrates multiple enterprise systems to manage the 
lifecycle of Bills of Materials at Tyson Foods. Its architecture is designed to support structured 
formulation, plant -level customization, version control, and integration with external systems such as 
PDM, PLM, and SAP. While the interface is user -friendly for business teams, the backend supports 
robust, event -driven workflows and system -to-system communication for developers and technical 
stakeholders.
2.1 High -Level Architecture Flow
css
CopyEdit
[ PDM / PLM ] → [ BOM Portal ] → [ SAP ]
                     ↓
               [ ESP / Power BI ]
PDM (Product Data Management): Source of truth for formulation specifications.•
PLM (Product Lifecycle Management): Sends official formulation payloads.•
BOM Portal : Receives, transforms, and manages formulation data into versioned BOMs. •
SAP: Consumes finalized BOMs for cost accounting, inventory control, and production execution.•
ESP: Engineering systems that support crewing and production setup.•
Power BI : Reporting layer for reconciliation and performance tracking. •
2.2 Frontend Framework
Built using AngularJS (legacy version), providing dynamic page rendering and form -based input 
validation.•
Features include:•
Step -by-step BOM editor •
Component and step grids•
Real -time toggles for formulation overrides and global scrap•
Version selection dropdowns•
2.3 Backend Services
Written in C# / .NET Framework . •
Microservices handle:•
Data transformations from PLM•
Spec change alerts via NService•
SAP submission packaging•
Version and status management•
SignalR enables live communication between the frontend and backend (e.g., syncing materials, 
updating status in real time).•
API endpoints exist for:•
Material and plant lookup•
BOM creation and publishing•
SAP status polling•
2.4 Database Layer
MySQL is the primary data store for all BOM -related data. •
Tables include:•
recipe_current•
plant_material_xref_current•
component_edit_audit•
bom_version•Tyson Foods -Legacy BOM Portal Training Manual
1. Purpose of the BOM Portal
The BOM Portal is Tyson  oods’ central application for creating, managing, validating, and submitting 
Bills of Materials (BOMs) used in both production and financial processes. It provides a structured 
interface that enables cost accounting, supply chain, and technical teams to define and maintain 
accurate recipe structures across plants.
The BOM Portal bridges the gap between upstream formulation systems such as PDM (Product Data 
Management) and PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) and downstream execution systems such as 
SAP, where final BOMs are consumed for manufacturing, costing, and inventory planning.
Primary Objectives
1.1 Standardized BOM Management
Enables structured creation and publishing of Bills of Materials specific to plant, material, BOM 
type (Production, Finance, Engineering, AOP), and cost center.•
Supports version control by allowing users to create new versions upon changes rather than 
overwriting existing BOMs.•
Enforces data entry consistency for step sequencing, component assignments, inventory points, 
and yields.•
1.2 Integration with Enterprise Systems
Receives formulation input from upstream systems like PDM and PLM, often via scheduled 
payloads.•
Submits finalized BOMs to SAP using backend remote function calls (RFCs) triggered from within 
the portal after passing internal validation checks.•
Ensures synchronization across PDM, PLM, and SAP through scheduled spec change processing 
and reconciliation.•
1.3 Plant -Specific Configuration
Allows for local BOM variations to be built off corporate formulations, such as alternate ingredient 
routing or yield percentages based on plant -specific processes.•
Supports toggles such as “Override  ormulation” and “Use Global Scrap” for managing exceptions 
at the plant level.•
1.4 Spec Change Tracking
Automatically flags affected BOMs with a "Spec Change" status when formulation updates are 
received from PDM.•
Prompts users to review and update impacted BOMs to maintain alignment with current 
specifications.•
Uses NService to receive weekly spec update notifications and apply them programmatically 
where applicable.•
1.5 Version Control
Tracks BOMs at the version level, preserving published versions while allowing users to create and 
edit new drafts.•
Each BOM version has a unique version number but must be reviewed individually —no full audit 
log or history comparison exists in the UI.•
Versioning supports downstream traceability for SAP submissions and formulation governance.•
System Architecture Overview
The BOM Portal is a web -based application that integrates multiple enterprise systems to manage the 
lifecycle of Bills of Materials at Tyson Foods. Its architecture is designed to support structured 
formulation, plant -level customization, version control, and integration with external systems such as 
PDM, PLM, and SAP. While the interface is user -friendly for business teams, the backend supports 
robust, event -driven workflows and system -to-system communication for developers and technical 
stakeholders.

2.4 Database Layer
MySQL is the primary data store for all BOM -related data. •
Tables include:•
recipe_current•
plant_material_xref_current•
component_edit_audit•
bom_version•
material_current•
MySQL is accessed via BigQuery federated queries in GCP for reporting and validation. •
Material ingestion and reconciliation logic are often validated between GCP datasets and the MySQL 
tables.•
2.5 Event and Notification Handling
NService : Message bus that delivers notifications (e.g., spec changes from PDM). •
Redis Cache : Supports caching of session data and connection tokens. •
SignalR Events : Handle async calls like material sync, SAP submission status updates, and user 
notifications.•
Splunk : Used for centralized logging of API events, errors, and spec change message failures. •
2.6 Environments and Promotion Process
Environments:•
DEV , QA, SBX, PROD •
Data flows downstream from PDM → PLM → BOM Portal in each environment. •
Changes are promoted using CHaRM or CAB -controlled transports, especially for SAP integrations. •
Developers test backend and UI changes in DEV and QA, then validate data integrity in SBX before 
production deployment.•
2.7 Roles of Development Team
Maintain and enhance existing backend services (C#, MySQL queries, API endpoints).•
Build and test SignalR events and real -time sync features. •
Support SAP submission integrity and debug reconciliation issues.•
Log and monitor failures using Splunk and work with InfraSec if async failures occur.•
Ensure business logic aligns with cost accounting and formulation rules.•
3. Key Entities and Data Structure
Understanding the core entities in the BOM Portal is essential for developers supporting its 
functionality. The system is designed around the relationships between materials, plants, cost centers, 
and formulations, all organized through a layered Bill of Materials structure.
3.1 Core Data Hierarchy
At its core, the BOM Portal operates through a three -tier hierarchy:
Formulation1.
BOM Template2.
BOM Version3.
Each of these layers ties into plant and material -specific configurations and supports versioning, 
validation, and SAP submission.
3.2 Key Entities
3.2.1 Material
Represents the finished good, raw material, or intermediate item being produced.•
Pulled from the authoritative material master.•
Contains data such as:•
MATERIAL_NUM•
MATERIAL_DESCR•
MATERIAL_TYPE (FERT, ROH, etc.) •
Linked to cost centers and inventory points.•
3.2.2 Recipe
A legacy concept within the BOM Portal representing a set of steps and components.•
Recipes were previously used as the working structure before formal BOMs were introduced.•
Still used internally in the backend and ties into historical records.•
3.2.3 BOM Template
A reusable structure used to define the overall flow of production for a material.•
Tied to:•
One material ID•
One or more plants •
A cost center•
Defines:•
Steps (phases of production)•
Yield structure•
Phantom BOM behavior•
Flags such as Driver, Override Formulation, and Use Global Scrap•
3.2.4 BOM Version
Represents a snapshot of a BOM Template at a point in time.•
Each time a change is made (e.g., a component update, step change), a new version is created.•
Statuses include:•
Edit–Unpublished draft •
Published –Live BOM submitted to SAP •
Spec Change –Awaiting user action after a formulation update •
Terminated –No longer active •
Includes effective dates, SAP version number, and validation metadata.•
3.2.5 Step
Defines a specific phase in production or assembly.•
Each step contains:•
Components (materials/resources)•
Step yield %•
Optional: Labeling as critical or phantom•
3.2.6 Component
A raw material, packaging item, or resource assigned to a step.•
Fields include:•
Component ID and type (ROH, VERP, etc.)•
Quantity and UoM•
Inventory point (e.g., ZFG, ZSWP)•
Cost relevance (stock vs. non -stock) •
3.3 Cross -Reference Entities
Plant
Represents the physical manufacturing facility.•Tables include: 
recipe_current•
plant_material_xref_current•
component_edit_audit•
bom_version•
material_current••
MySQL is accessed via BigQuery federated queries in GCP for reporting and validation. •
Material ingestion and reconciliation logic are often validated between GCP datasets and the 
MySQL tables.•
2.5 Event and Notification Handling
NService : Message bus that delivers notifications (e.g., spec changes from PDM). •
Redis Cache : Supports caching of session data and connection tokens. •
SignalR Events : Handle async calls like material sync, SAP submission status updates, and user 
notifications.•
Splunk : Used for centralized logging of API events, errors, and spec change message failures. •
2.6 Environments and Promotion Process
Environments : 
DEV, QA, SBX, PROD••
Data flows downstream from PDM → PLM → BOM Portal in each environment. •
Changes are promoted using CHaRM or CAB -controlled transports, especially for SAP integrations. •
Developers test backend and UI changes in DEV and QA, then validate data integrity in SBX before 
production deployment.•
2.7 Roles of Development Team
Maintain and enhance existing backend services (C#, MySQL queries, API endpoints).•
Build and test SignalR events and real -time sync features. •
Support SAP submission integrity and debug reconciliation issues.•
Log and monitor failures using Splunk and work with InfraSec if async failures occur. •
Ensure business logic aligns with cost accounting and formulation rules.•

Mapped in plant_material_xref_current to the materials and BOMs it supports. ••
Cost Center 
Financial identifier that controls how costs are tracked and rolled up.•
Required for every BOM submission to SAP.••
Inventory Points 
Nodes in the production flow where material enters or exits a phase (e.g., ZFG for Finished 
Goods, ZSWP for Saleable WIP).•
Used to manage visibility and flow of material in SAP and cost reports.••
