for Saleable WIP).•
Used to manage visibility and flow of material in SAP and cost reports.•
3.4 Data Sources and Tables (MySQL)
Table Name Purpose
recipe_current Stores current recipe records (precursor to BOMs)
plant_material_xref_current Links materials to plants
material_current Stores master data for materials
component_edit_audit Tracks changes to components in steps
recipe_formulation_xref_current Maps formulation to recipe entities
component_step_current Defines component placement in steps
bom_version (internal table) Tracks version metadata for published BOMs
4. BOM Creation and Management (Legacy BOM Portal)
In the legacy BOM Portal , the process of creating and managing BOMs is structured around templates 
and version control. BOMs are configured at the plant -material level, tied to a cost center and BOM 
type, and represent the formal structure used for production or financial costing in SAP.
4.1 Accessing BOM Creation
There is no standalone “Create BOM” button in the legacy interface.
Instead, users navigate to the Manage BOMs or Manage Versions section from the main menu or 
landing screen. BOM creation typically starts by:
Selecting a plant and material •
Choosing a BOM Type (Production, Finance, Engineering, AOP) •
Selecting or creating a cost center mapping •
Using the “Create Version” button (if visible) or initiating from an existing template •
4.2 Defining the BOM Template
The template defines the structural framework of the BOM. When creating a new BOM version:
The system may prefill data if a formulation exists in PDM/PLM for that material•
Users define:•
Steps (each representing a phase in production)•
Components for each step (raw materials, packaging, labor, etc.) •
Formulation overrides can be enabled if needed per plant •
Global scrap can be toggled on/off based on material group rules •
The template does not support cloning in bulk but can be manually duplicated by selecting similar 
existing BOMs and copying their setup.
4.3 Step and Component Configuration
Each step includes:
A label or sequence number•
Yield percentage (must total to 100%)•
Option to assign a phantom flag (if used in multi -level BOMs) •
Each component within a step includes:
Material number (selected from material master)•
Quantity and unit of measure•
Inventory point (e.g., ZFG, ZSWP)•
Type: stock, non -stock, packaging, or resource •
Component grids are editable in the UI and validated at save.
4.4 Versioning and Status Flow
In the legacy BOM Portal, each change to a BOM results in a new version . Versions do not overwrite 
previous ones.
Version statuses include:
Edit–Draft or in -progress BOM •
Published –Active version used for SAP submission •
Spec Change –Triggered when upstream formulation changes are detected •
Terminated –Inactive version, manually deactivated by user •
There is no side -by-side version comparison or visual change tracking within the UI.
4.5 Publishing the BOM
When a BOM version is ready:
The user clicks “Publish” •
The system performs internal validation:•
Step yields = 100%•
Required fields (plant, cost center, material) are present•
Component entries are valid•
Upon success, the BOM is marked Published and can be submitted to SAP manually or via the 
submission queue•
4.6 Managing Existing BOMs
From the Manage Versions page, users can:
Filter BOMs by plant, material, cost center, status, or BOM type•
View and edit individual versions (only one version at a time)•Financial identifier that controls how costs are tracked and rolled up.•
Required for every BOM submission to SAP.•
Inventory Points 
Nodes in the production flow where material enters or exits a phase (e.g., ZFG for Finished 
Goods, ZSWP for Saleable WIP).•
Used to manage visibility and flow of material in SAP and cost reports.••
3.4 Data Sources and Tables (MySQL)
Table Name Purpose
recipe_current Stores current recipe records (precursor to BOMs)
plant_material_xref_current Links materials to plants
material_current Stores master data for materials
component_edit_audit Tracks changes to components in steps
recipe_formulation_xref_current Maps formulation to recipe entities
component_step_current Defines component placement in steps
bom_version (internal table) Tracks version metadata for published BOMs
Export to Sheets
4. BOM Creation and Management (Legacy BOM Portal)
In the legacy BOM Portal , the process of creating and managing BOMs is structured around templates 
and version control. BOMs are configured at the plant -material level, tied to a cost center and BOM 
type, and represent the formal structure used for production or financial costing in SAP.
4.1 Accessing BOM Creation
There is no standalone “Create BOM” button in the legacy interface. •
Instead, users navigate to the Manage BOMs or Manage Versions section from the main menu or 
landing screen. BOM creation typically starts by: 
Selecting a plant and material •
Choosing a BOM Type (Production, Finance, Engineering, AOP) •
Selecting or creating a cost center mapping •
Using the “Create Version” button (if visible) or initiating from an existing template ••
4.2 Defining the BOM Template
The template defines the structural framework of the BOM. When creating a new BOM version:
The system may prefill data if a formulation exists in PDM/PLM for that material•
Users define: 
Steps (each representing a phase in production)•
Components for each step (raw materials, packaging, labor, etc.) •
Formulation overrides can be enabled if needed per plant •
Global scrap can be toggled on/off based on material group rules ••
The template does not support cloning in bulk but can be manually duplicated by selecting similar 
existing BOMs and copying their setup.•
4.3 Step and Component Configuration
Each step includes:
A label or sequence number•
Yield percentage (must total to 100%)•
Option to assign a phantom flag (if used in multi -level BOMs) •
Each component within a step includes:
Material number (selected from material master)•
Quantity and unit of measure•
Inventory point (e.g., ZFG, ZSWP)•
Type: stock, non -stock, packaging, or resource •
Component grids are editable in the UI and validated at save.•
4.4 Versioning and Status Flow
In the legacy BOM Portal, each change to a BOM results in a new version . Versions do not overwrite 
previous ones.
Version statuses include: 
Edit–Draft or in -progress BOM •
Published –Active version used for SAP submission •
Spec Change –Triggered when upstream formulation changes are detected •
Terminated –Inactive version, manually deactivated by user ••
There is no side -by-side version comparison or visual change tracking within the UI. •
4.5 Publishing the BOM
When a BOM version is ready:
The user clicks “Publish” •
The system performs internal validation: 
Step yields = 100%•
Required fields (plant, cost center, material) are present•
Component entries are valid••
Upon success, the BOM is marked Published and can be submitted to SAP manually or via the 
submission queue•
4.6 Managing Existing BOMs
From the Manage Versions page, users can:
Filter BOMs by plant, material, cost center, status, or BOM type•
View and edit individual versions (only one version at a time)•
Terminate versions no longer in use•
Create a new version based on a prior one by using the version dropdown or manual recreation•
There is no audit log or centralized history tab —users must check version numbers and 
timestamps individually.•
4.7 Developer Considerations
BOM creation and edits update the following MySQL tables: 
recipe_current•
component_step_current•
plant_material_xref_current••
Publishing triggers backend logic to assign version numbers and flag readiness for SAP submission•
SAP submission status is tracked in the SAP submission table or status queue•
Version data is not stored as diffs —each version is a full data snapshot •
5. Spec Change Handling (Legacy BOM Portal)
Specification changes (spec changes) are a critical function in the BOM Portal. They ensure that the Bills 
of Materials remain synchronized with official formulation data maintained in PDM and PLM . The legacy 
BOM Portal includes automated and manual workflows to detect, flag, and address formulation changes 
that impact existing BOMs.
5.1 Overview of Spec Changes
Spec changes originate from PDM (Product Data Management), where formulation updates are 
maintained.•
These changes are pushed to the BOM Portal through PLM as structured data payloads. •
   My KT Page 47    
4.6 Managing Existing BOMs
From the Manage Versions page, users can:
Filter BOMs by plant, material, cost center, status, or BOM type•
View and edit individual versions (only one version at a time)•
Terminate versions no longer in use•
Create a new version based on a prior one by using the version dropdown or manual recreation•
There is no audit log or centralized history tab —users must check version numbers and timestamps 
individually.
4.7 Developer Considerations
BOM creation and edits update the following MySQL tables:•
recipe_current•
component_step_current•
plant_material_xref_current•
Publishing triggers backend logic to assign version numbers and flag readiness for SAP submission•
SAP submission status is tracked in the SAP submission table or status queue•
Version data is not stored as diffs —each version is a full data snapshot •
5. Spec Change Handling (Legacy BOM Portal)
Specification changes (spec changes) are a critical function in the BOM Portal. They ensure that the Bills 
of Materials remain synchronized with official formulation data maintained in PDM and PLM . The legacy 
BOM Portal includes automated and manual workflows to detect, flag, and address formulation changes 
that impact existing BOMs.
5.1 Overview of Spec Changes
Spec changes originate from PDM (Product Data Management), where formulation updates are 
maintained.•
These changes are pushed to the BOM Portal through PLM as structured data payloads. •
A messaging service called NService delivers weekly updates notifying the BOM Portal of formulation 
updates.•
If a BOM in the system is impacted by a formulation update, it is automatically flagged with the status 
"Spec Change."•
5.2 Status: Spec Change
When a BOM version is impacted by an upstream formulation change:
Its status transitions from Published to Spec Change •
This status indicates that the BOM is no longer aligned with the current formulation and requires user 
review•
Users cannot re -submit the BOM to SAP until the spec change is resolved •
This ensures that outdated or misaligned BOMs do not propagate to SAP and cause data integrity issues.
5.3 How Users Resolve Spec Changes
In the legacy UI:
Navigate to the Manage Versions section 4.
Locate BOMs with status “Spec Change” 5.
Open the affected BOM version6.
Review the formulation updates (users often must refer to PLM or external documentation to identify 
exact changes)7.
Apply any required changes manually:8.
Adjust component quantities•
Add or remove steps or materials•
Save and re -publish the BOM to return it to Published status 9.
Note: There is no built -in comparison tool in the legacy portal to highlight what specifically changed. 
Users must perform this analysis manually.
5.4 System Behavior and Developer Triggers
From a backend/system perspective:
The spec change notification is delivered through NService •
The BOM Portal service reads the message and identifies the impacted BOMs•
A flag is set at the version level to mark the BOM as "Spec Change"•
This change may trigger updates in the recipe_current or component_step_current tables •
If NService fails to deliver or process a message, developers may troubleshoot the issue using:
Splunk logs•
SAP submission queues•
Manual review of mismatched data between BOM Portal and PLM•
5.5 Monitoring and Reconciliation
To ensure all spec changes are addressed:
A weekly reconciliation process compares BOM Portal data to formulation data in PLM/PDM •
Power BI reports are sometimes used to assist business users in identifying unmatched records•
Developers may be called on to assist with discrepancies, especially where payload mismatches or failed 
syncs are suspected•
5.6 Known Limitations in Legacy System
No visual diff tool for before/after comparisons•
No mass spec change approval or bulk update functionality•
Users must review each impacted BOM manually•
Delays in NService delivery may cause inconsistent spec change triggers•
6. SAP Integration (Legacy BOM Portal)
One of the core functions of the legacy BOM Portal is to submit finalized and validated BOMs to SAP, 
Tyson’s enterprise system for production planning, inventory management, and cost accounting. The 
submission to SAP is a controlled process that ensures each BOM is compliant, complete, and tied to a 
specific plant, material, and cost center.
6.1 Purpose of SAP SubmissionBOM Portal includes automated and manual workflows to detect, flag, and address formulation changes 
that impact existing BOMs.
5.1 Overview of Spec Changes
Spec changes originate from PDM (Product Data Management), where formulation updates are 
maintained.•
These changes are pushed to the BOM Portal through PLM as structured data payloads. •
A messaging service called NService delivers weekly updates notifying the BOM Portal of 
formulation updates.•
If a BOM in the system is impacted by a formulation update, it is automatically flagged with the 
status "Spec Change."•
5.2 Status: Spec Change
When a BOM version is impacted by an upstream formulation change:
Its status transitions from Published to Spec Change •
This status indicates that the BOM is no longer aligned with the current formulation and requires 
user review•
Users cannot re -submit the BOM to SAP until the spec change is resolved •
This ensures that outdated or misaligned BOMs do not propagate to SAP and cause data integrity 
issues.•
5.3 How Users Resolve Spec Changes
In the legacy UI:
Navigate to the Manage Versions section •
Locate BOMs with status “Spec Change” •
Open the affected BOM version•
Review the formulation updates (users often must refer to PLM or external documentation to 
identify exact changes)•
Apply any required changes manually: 
Adjust component quantities•
Add or remove steps or materials••
Save and re -publish the BOM to return it to Published status •
Note: There is no built -in comparison tool in the legacy portal to highlight what specifically 
changed. Users must perform this analysis manually.•
5.4 System Behavior and Developer Triggers
From a backend/system perspective:
The spec change notification is delivered through NService •
The BOM Portal service reads the message and identifies the impacted BOMs•
A flag is set at the version level to mark the BOM as "Spec Change"•
This change may trigger updates in the recipe_current or component_step_current tables •
If NService fails to deliver or process a message, developers may troubleshoot the issue using: 
Splunk logs•
SAP submission queues•
Manual review of mismatched data between BOM Portal and PLM••
5.5 Monitoring and Reconciliation
To ensure all spec changes are addressed:
A weekly reconciliation process compares BOM Portal data to formulation data in PLM/PDM •
Power BI reports are sometimes used to assist business users in identifying unmatched records•
Developers may be called on to assist with discrepancies, especially where payload mismatches or 
failed syncs are suspected•
5.6 Known Limitations in Legacy System
No visual diff tool for before/after comparisons•
•
   My KT Page 48    
6.1 Purpose of SAP Submission
BOMs submitted from the BOM Portal to SAP enable:
Production scheduling using SAP PP -PI (Process Industries) •
Costing and pricing using SAP Actual Costing (CO -PC) •
Material consumption and inventory visibility in SAP systems•
Only Published BOMs are eligible for SAP submission. BOMs in Edit, Spec Change, or Terminated status 
cannot be submitted.
6.2 SAP Submission Process
User Action 10.
A user selects a Published BOM Version from the Manage Versions view •
Clicks “Submit to SAP” (button available only if version passes internal validations) •
Validation Checks 11.
The system validates:•
Required fields (material number, plant, cost center, BOM type)•
Effective date is current or future -dated •
Component data is complete and valid•
Step yield equals 100%•
If validation fails, submission is blocked and the user receives an error message•
RFC Trigger 12.
Upon passing validation, a Remote Function Call (RFC) is executed to SAP •
BOM data is sent to SAP using a structured payload format•
SAP assigns a BOM number ( STLNR ) and confirms submission •
Response Handling 13.
The response from SAP is recorded in the system (success or failure)•
If successful, the SAP BOM Number is stored•
If failed, the error message is logged and displayed to the user•
6.3 Submission Interface
The submission interface in the legacy BOM Portal includes:
Submit to SAP button (visible only for valid Published versions) •
SAP Status Field (displays submitted, failed, or pending) •
Manual Retry Option if submission fails and is corrected •
There is no batch submission or queue view —each version must be submitted individually.
6.4 SAP Data Mapping
BOM Portal Field SAP Field (Mapped To)
Material Number MATNR
Plant WERKS
BOM Type STLAN
Version Number STLAL
Header Data STKO Table
Component Data STPO Table
SAP BOM Number STLNR
Cost Center Internal ZPARM configuration
6.5 Developer Notes
SAP submission logic is typically handled via C# backend service methods (e.g., SubmitBOMToSAP ) •
Submissions are logged through internal tracking tables (may include sap_submission_log , 
depending on implementation)•
Failures are often related to:•
Missing plant -cost center mappings •
Duplicate component entries•
Date formatting or null fields•
ZPARM programs in SAP control how incoming BOMs are interpreted and where values are stored •
Splunk logs can be used to trace failed submissions or connection issues with SAP •
6.6 Known Limitations
No mass submission capability (each BOM must be submitted individually)•
No built -in retry scheduler for failed submissions •
Minimal user feedback beyond error messages•
Delay between submission and SAP confirmation if system is under load•
Here is Section 7: User Roles and Access Controls , tailored specifically to the legacy BOM Portal :
7. User Roles and Access Controls (Legacy BOM Portal)
Access to the BOM Portal is tightly controlled to ensure that only authorized users can view, modify, or 
publish BOMs. The legacy system uses role-based access control (RBAC) with security group mappings 
and plant -based restrictions to manage user permissions across functions.
7.1 Authentication
The legacy BOM Portal uses Okta Single Sign -On (SSO) for user authentication. •
Upon login, the user's roles and permissions are evaluated against configured Okta groups or 
application -specific roles.•
Users must be onboarded and assigned a role before gaining access to the portal.•
7.2 Role Types and Permissions
Role Description
Viewer Can view published BOMs, spec change statuses, and submission results.
Editor Can create new BOMs, edit drafts, apply formulation changes, and save versions.
Approver (e.g., Cost Accounting) Can review and publish BOMs but not edit them.
Admin Can do all the above, terminate versions, assign cost centers, and submit to SAP.
Note: Roles are cumulative —higher roles inherit the capabilities of lower roles.
   My KT Page 49    
7.3 Plant -Based Access Restrictions
Users are granted access only to specific plants . •
They can only create, view, or edit BOMs for materials associated with those plants.•
If a user attempts to access a BOM outside of their assigned plant list, the system blocks access with a 
permission error.•
This structure ensures separation of duties between regions, product lines, or operational divisions.
7.4 UI Behavior Based on Role
Feature Viewer Editor Approver Admin
View Published BOMs ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅
Create/Edit Draft BOMs ❌ ✅ ❌ ✅
Publish BOMs ❌ ❌ ✅ ✅
Terminate BOM Versions ❌ ❌ ❌ ✅
Submit BOM to SAP ❌ ❌ ❌ ✅
Modify Cost Center Assignments ❌ ❌ ❌ ✅
7.5 Role Assignment and Support
Role assignment is typically managed by the BOM Portal administrator or IT support team.•
Requests for role changes or plant access are handled via internal ticketing (e.g., ServiceNow).•
Roles may also be stored and validated against internal user tables within the BOM Portal database, 
depending on the version.•
7.6 Developer Considerations
Roles and permissions are enforced both in the frontend UI and the backend API layer . •
Backend services include checks for:•
Role validation before allowing POST or PUT actions•
Plant access validation to prevent cross -site data exposure •
Developers should never bypass role checks, even for admin -level scripts. •
Would you like to move on to Section 8: Reconciliation and Data Validation next?
Here is Section 8: Reconciliation and Data Validation , specifically for the legacy BOM Portal :
8. Reconciliation and Data Validation (Legacy BOM Portal)
The BOM Portal must ensure that the Bills of Materials it maintains are accurate, current, and aligned 
with upstream formulation data (from PDM/PLM) and downstream systems (SAP). This is achieved 
through a combination of automated data validations and manual reconciliation processes involving 
reports, backend queries, and error logging.
8.1 Purpose of Reconciliation
Reconciliation ensures:
BOMs in the portal reflect the latest formulation specs from PDM •
Submissions to SAP are complete, valid, and traceable •
Materials, cost centers, and step yields are correctly aligned •
No critical gaps exist between systems that could lead to manufacturing or costing errors•
8.2 Types of Reconciliation
1. Upstream (PDM → BOM Portal)
Checks whether formulations received from PLM match the BOM structures built in the Portal.•
Weekly spec change syncs help detect mismatches. •
Material and component -level differences must be resolved by business users manually. •
2. Downstream (BOM Portal → SAP)
Ensures that submitted BOMs:•
Match what's stored in SAP tables ( STKO , STPO , MAST ) •
Include valid STLNR (SAP BOM number)•
Are accepted by SAP with no errors•
SAP submission logs and version status are the primary feedback mechanisms.•
8.3 Validation Rules in the Portal
The legacy BOM Portal enforces several validations at time of save and publish :
Validation Rule Triggered When
Step yield must total 100% On publish
Each component must have valid material ID On save or component entry
Plant and cost center must be assigned On create
No duplicate components in same step On save
Effective date must be today or future -dated On publish or submit to SAP
Required inventory points must be assigned On component entry
If validations fail, the system blocks publish or submission actions and presents error messages to the 
user.
8.4 Manual Reconciliation Reports
Though no automated comparison screen exists in the UI, teams use Power BI reports or GCP queries to 
manually reconcile BOM data:
Compare BOM component lists with formulation data•
Identify BOMs flagged for Spec Change that haven’t been updated •
Review mismatches in material descriptions or quantities•
Validate cost center assignments across BOM versions•
These reports are typically maintained outside the BOM Portal and rely on MySQL/GCP data extracts.
8.5 Developer Support in Reconciliation
   My KT Page 50    
8.5 Developer Support in Reconciliation
Developers may assist with:
Writing or debugging reconciliation queries (e.g., join between recipe_current , 
material_current , and formulation source)•
Investigating mismatches between BOM Portal and PLM payloads•
Identifying failures in the NService delivery or spec sync logic•
Supporting business teams with one -off material audits via backend SQL •
8.6 Known Gaps in Legacy Portal
No in -app report or dashboard for full BOM comparison•
No mass update or auto -resolution for spec change mismatches •
No side -by-side version diff tool •
Manual data validation is time -consuming and requires support from analysts and developers •
Would you like to continue with Section 9: Testing and ALM Integration (Legacy Only) next?
Here is Section 9: Testing and ALM Integration , specifically for the legacy BOM Portal :
9. Testing and ALM Integration (Legacy BOM Portal)
Testing and quality assurance are critical for maintaining the stability and integrity of the BOM Portal, 
especially when deploying updates to logic tied to SAP integration, formulation structure, or cost -
sensitive processes. Tyson uses HP ALM (Application Lifecycle Management) as its primary test case 
repository and validation environment for all functional changes to the BOM Portal.
9.1 Role of ALM in BOM Portal Testing
ALM supports:
Test case authoring and execution•
Traceability between functional requirements and test coverage•
Regression suite tracking for code changes and production issues•
Formal sign -off and documentation for CAB review or SOX compliance •
9.2 Functional Testing Scope
The following areas are covered by functional test cases in ALM:
Area Tested In
BOM creation and publishing DEV, QA
SAP submission validation QA, SBX, PROD
Versioning rules DEV
Spec change flag behavior QA
Material/component validations DEV, QA
Role -based access controls DEV
Tests are typically mapped by functionality, not individual UI components.
9.3 Testing Environments
Environment Purpose
DEV Developer unit testing, early functional testing
QA Functional, integration, and regression testing
SBX (Sandbox) UAT -style validation with near -production data
PROD Production environment, only used for validation after release
Each environment has mirrored data tables, but only QAand SBX typically receive fully synced 
formulation payloads and SAP connectivity for end -to-end testing.
9.4 Developer Responsibilities
Maintain automated and manual test case coverage for any new feature or logic change.•
Collaborate with QA analysts to define test inputs, expected outputs, and validation rules.•
Ensure backend API changes or submission logic have testable criteria and clean rollback behavior.•
Write regression test scripts for recurring logic like spec change syncing or SAP error handling.•
Developers are expected to test in DEV before handing off to QA, and to assist during UAT in SBX when 
needed.
9.5 Regression Testing
A core set of regression tests is maintained for:
SAP submission logic•
Spec change detection and status updates•
Material -component assignment rules •
Yield and inventory point validations•
Regression testing is required before any deployment to PROD, especially for changes related to cost 
center logic or backend processing tied to financial reporting.
9.6 Known Testing Limitations
Some validations rely on external systems (e.g., SAP, PDM) and require manual test data staging•
No test automation framework is in place for UI testing (legacy AngularJS limits this)•
Tests for SignalR -based async updates are limited and often require manual validation •
Would you like to proceed with Section 10: Logging and Monitoring (Legacy Only) next?
Here is Section 10: Logging and Monitoring , written for the legacy BOM Portal :
10. Logging and Monitoring (Legacy BOM Portal)
Reliable logging and system monitoring are essential for ensuring uptime, diagnosing issues, and 
   My KT Page 51    
Reliable logging and system monitoring are essential for ensuring uptime, diagnosing issues, and 
maintaining traceability in the legacy BOM Portal. While the UI offers limited visibility into internal 
processes, developers and support teams rely on centralized logging tools and service -level monitors to 
detect and resolve system issues.
10.1 Logging Architecture Overview
The legacy BOM Portal uses a combination of backend application logging , real-time event tracking , 
and external log aggregation to support monitoring:
Application Logs : Captured via .NET backend logging (e.g., log4net or custom logging) •
SignalR Events : Used to monitor asynchronous operations like spec syncs or SAP submissions •
Splunk : Centralized log aggregation and search for tracking API errors, payload issues, submission 
failures, and service interruptions•
10.2 What Is Logged
Event Log Location
SAP submission request & response Splunk, backend logs
Spec change detection or failure Splunk, backend logs
Material sync issues SignalR, Splunk
Role -based access denials Application logs
Backend service failures (timeouts, 500s) Splunk
NService delivery & processing NService logs, Splunk
Logs often include:
Timestamp•
User ID•
Environment (DEV, QA, PROD)•
Material or BOM ID•
Error message or stack trace (if applicable)•
10.3 Splunk Monitoring
Splunk is the primary tool used by developers and support teams to:
Search logs by keyword, user, or date•
Correlate submission errors with backend failures•
Monitor real -time failures across environments •
Investigate discrepancies reported by business users•
Dashboards or saved searches may be maintained by infrastructure or application teams for recurring 
issues (e.g., SAP submission timeouts).
10.4 SignalR Monitoring
The legacy portal uses SignalR to power real -time notifications and status updates in the UI.
Typical SignalR events include:
“Material Sync Successful”•
“BOM Submitted to SAP”•
“Spec Change Detected”•
SignalR issues (e.g., stale connections or failure to push status) are often not logged unless backend API 
endpoints fail, making real -time validation important during testing.
10.5 Monitoring Best Practices
Always review Splunk logs when:•
A BOM fails to submit to SAP•
A spec change is expected but not flagged•
Users report missing materials or inventory points•
Use environment filters (e.g., env=QA , env=PROD ) to avoid confusion during triage •
Validate that all NService messages have been processed if spec changes are not appearing•
10.6 Known Gaps in Monitoring
No dedicated monitoring dashboard within the BOM Portal UI•
Limited in -app feedback for background failures •
SignalR failures can silently break async updates (requires manual browser refresh or backend check)•
Error handling on some backend services lacks descriptive output, requiring Splunk inspection for root 
cause•
Would you like to continue with Section 11: Development Process and DevOps (Legacy Only) next?
Here is Section 11: Development Process and DevOps , focused specifically on the legacy BOM Portal :
11. Development Process and DevOps (Legacy BOM Portal)
Maintaining and enhancing the legacy BOM Portal requires developers to work within a structured 
DevOps process that supports code quality, stable deployment, and controlled environment promotion. 
Given the portal’s integration with critical systems like SAP and PDM, all changes are thoroughly tested 
and promoted through a gated release process.
11.1 Codebase Overview
The legacy BOM Portal is built using:•
Frontend : AngularJS (legacy version) •
Backend : C# (.NET Framework) •
Database : MySQL, accessed via ADO.NET and Entity Framework •
Messaging : NService for async event handling (spec changes) •
Real -time Communication : SignalR •
Code is stored in GitLab , with repository folders typically organized by: •
UI/•
Services/•
Models/•
Controllers/•
SAPIntegration/•
Most of the critical submission logic resides in Services/ and SAPIntegration/ .
   My KT Page 52    
Most of the critical submission logic resides in Services/ and SAPIntegration/ .
11.2 Local Development and Testing
Developers are expected to:
Pull the latest code from the dev branch •
Spin up the application locally using IIS Express or Docker (where supported)•
Test features against stubbed data or QA environment APIs•
Use logging breakpoints to trace submission failures, spec processing, or material sync issues•
11.3 Environment Promotion Process
Environment Purpose
DEV Active development and unit testing
QA Full integration and functional testing
SBX UAT -style validation with near -prod data
PROD Live production environment
Code is promoted from DEV → QA → SBX → PROD •
Approval for promotion may require CAB sign -off or ALM test case completion •
Promotion Tools:
GitLab CI/CD Pipelines or internal scripts (depending on legacy setup)•
Manual deployment steps may include:•
Publishing backend services•
Deploying frontend bundles•
Syncing versioned DLLs•
Restarting IIS App Pools•
11.4 Change Control
All production -impacting changes must follow a structured process:
Requirements definition 14.
Code development in DEV 15.
Functional testing in QA 16.
Test case validation in ALM 17.
CAB approval (if needed) 18.
Deployment to PROD 19.
Any SAP -related logic or ZPARM adjustments may require:
Coordination with SAP BASIS team•
Creation of transport requests•
11.5 Developer Responsibilities
Ensure business logic follows formulation and costing rules•
Validate material and plant data access aligns with role -based controls •
Assist with QA troubleshooting and test case support•
Review and maintain SignalR updates, NService handlers, and error logging•
Contribute to reconciliation query improvements and backend validation logic•
11.6 Known Challenges in Legacy DevOps
AngularJS frontend is aging and fragile; testing is manual•
No automated unit or UI test framework•
Splunk logs may be incomplete without context from SignalR or middleware•
Environment configuration drift may cause inconsistencies in plant/material mappings•
Would you like to proceed with Section 12: Troubleshooting Common Issues (Legacy Only) ?
Here is Section 12: Troubleshooting Common Issues , specifically for the legacy BOM Portal :
12. Troubleshooting Common Issues (Legacy BOM Portal)
Due to the complexity of integrations and the aging frontend, users and developers of the legacy BOM 
Portal may encounter a variety of issues ranging from validation failures to SAP submission errors. This 
section outlines the most common problems, how they present in the system, and how developers can 
support their resolution.
12.1 SAP Submission Failures
 Symptoms
User receives an error after clicking “Submit to SAP”•
SAP BOM Number ( STLNR ) is not returned or remains blank •
Status remains “Published” but never moves to “Submitted”•
  Root Causes
Missing or invalid plant -cost center -material mapping •
Duplicate component entries in the same step•
Invalid effective date (must be today or future)•
RFC failure or SAP connection timeout•
 Troubleshooting Steps
Check required fields (plant, cost center, material number) 20.
Review version for duplicate or incomplete components 21.
Search Splunk logs for SubmitBOMToSAP or similar error entries 22.
Manually retry submission after corrections 23.
12.2 Spec Change Not Triggering
 Symptoms
User knows a spec has changed in PDM, but the BOM is still “Published”•
Expected “Spec Change” status never appears•
  Root Causes
NService message failed to deliver or process•
Material not properly mapped in formulation payload•
Backend logic failed to flag the impacted BOM•
 Troubleshooting Steps
Confirm PDM spec update exists for the material 24.
Check backend table recipe_current for timestamp changes 25.
Review Splunk logs for NService -related failures 26.
   My KT Page 53    
Review Splunk logs for NService -related failures 26.
Trigger a manual sync or escalate to integration team 27.
12.3 Version Stuck in Edit
 Symptoms
User cannot publish or submit a BOM version•
Version remains in “Edit” status despite being complete•
  Root Causes
Incomplete validation: step yield ≠100%, missing components •
Cost center or inventory point not assigned•
User lacks permissions to publish for selected plant•
 Troubleshooting Steps
Validate all required fields and that step yields total 100% 28.
Confirm cost center is selected and valid for the plant 29.
Check user’s assigned role and plant access 30.
Use backend queries to confirm version metadata 31.
12.4 Material Not Appearing in Search
 Symptoms
User cannot find a material in dropdowns or search fields•
Existing materials appear blank or filtered out•
  Root Causes
Material not synced to BOM Portal from PDM•
Plant -material linkage missing in plant_material_xref_current •
User doesn’t have access to the plant or BOM type•
 Troubleshooting Steps
Check material_current and plant_material_xref_current tables 32.
Verify plant assignment via backend or user config 33.
Confirm with business team that the material is ready for BOM creation 34.
12.5 General UI Failures or Freezing
 Symptoms
Pages freeze or crash during component entry•
Long load times on version dropdown or spec change screen•
  Root Causes
AngularJS performance limitations•
Large component lists or improperly cached data•
SignalR disconnect or timeout•
 Troubleshooting Steps
Refresh the browser or clear session cache 35.
Monitor for any failing SignalR messages in developer console 36.
Review backend logs for page load performance 37.
Escalate to frontend support team if UI is unresponsive 38.
12.6 Logging and Debugging Tools Summary
Tool Use
Splunk View backend logs, errors, SAP submission failures
SignalR Logs Detect async update failures or stale syncs
MySQL Queries Validate material/plant linkage and BOM components
UI Console Spot frontend errors, missing events, or slow loads

CopyEdit

 Look for Duplicated Components
sql
CopyEdit
SELECT RECIPE_ID, COMPONENT_ID, COUNT(*) 
FROM component_step_current 
GROUP BY RECIPE_ID, COMPONENT_ID 
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

1. Purpose of the BOM Portal
The BOM Portal is Tyson Foods’ central application for creating, managing, validating, and submitting 
Bills of Materials (BOMs) used in both production and financial processes. It provides a structured 
interface that enables cost accounting, supply chain, and technical teams to define and maintain 
accurate recipe structures across plants.
The BOM Portal bridges the gap between upstream formulation systems such as PDM (Product Data 
Management) and PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) and downstream execution systems such as 
SAP, where final BOMs are consumed for manufacturing, costing, and inventory planning.
Primary Objectives
1.1 Standardized BOM Management
Enables structured creation and publishing of Bills of Materials specific to plant, material, BOM type 
(Production, Finance, Engineering, AOP), and cost center.•
Supports version control by allowing users to create new versions upon changes rather than overwriting 
existing BOMs.•
Enforces data entry consistency for step sequencing, component assignments, inventory points, and 
yields.•
Provides a single source of truth for BOM data, reducing discrepancies and errors across departments.•
Streamlines the BOM creation process, making it more efficient and less time -consuming. •
1.2 Integration with Enterprise Systems
Receives formulation input from upstream systems like PDM and PLM, often via scheduled payloads.•
Submits finalized BOMs to SAP using backend remote function calls (RFCs) triggered from within the 
portal after passing internal validation checks.•
Ensures synchronization across PDM, PLM, and SAP through scheduled spec change processing and 
reconciliation.•
Facilitates seamless data exchange between different systems, eliminating manual data entry and 
reducing the risk of errors.•
Improves data accuracy and consistency across the enterprise.•
1.3 Plant -Specific Configuration
Allows for local BOM variations to be built off corporate formulations, such as alternate ingredient 
routing or yield percentages based on plant -specific processes.•
Supports toggles such as “Override  ormulation” and “Use Global Scrap” for managing exceptions at the 
plant level.•
Enables plants to adapt BOMs to their specific needs and optimize production processes.•
Provides flexibility to handle variations in raw materials, equipment, and production environments.•
1.4 Spec Change Tracking
Automatically flags affected BOMs with a "Spec Change" status when formulation updates are received 
from PDM.•
Prompts users to review and update impacted BOMs to maintain alignment with current specifications.•
Uses NService to receive weekly spec update notifications and apply them programmatically where 
applicable.•
Ensures that BOMs are always up -to-date with the latest specifications, preventing production errors 
and quality issues.•
Provides a clear audit trail of changes, improving accountability and traceability.•
1.5 Version ControlThursday, May 08, 2025 3:06 PM
   My KT Page 56    
1.5 Version Control
Tracks BOMs at the version level, preserving published versions while allowing users to create and edit 
new drafts.•
Each BOM version has a unique version number but must be reviewed individually —no full audit log or 
history comparison exists in the UI.•
Versioning supports downstream traceability for SAP submissions and formulation governance.•
Allows users to easily revert to previous BOM versions if needed, minimizing disruption to production.•
Supports the management of BOM changes over time, providing a historical record for analysis and 
reporting.•

   My Shout Outs Page 62    
  
Hi Tricia,
Thank you for your email and for the opportunity to align before our meeting when I return. I 
agree maybe this wasn't best place to include Andre and Jerry however the insights that I have 
pertain to their roles as well. This will be outlined a detailed in our upcoming meeting. I have 
completed my sections of the Performance Assessment with detailed ratings and comments.
As I reviewed the template, I noticed that several duties and skills listed align more closely with 
a Product Manager or Team Manager role rather than a Product Owner role as defined in Agile 
practices. While I welcome the opportunity for visibility and training in these areas to support 
future growth, the way we have operated as a team over the past year has placed many of 
these responsibilities outside my current scope.
Below is my response to the points you requested:
1. Fundamental disconnect in role definition, responsibilities, and support
I would like to gain a clearer understanding of the distinctions between Product Owner I, 
Product Owner II, and Manager roles within Tyson’s job architecture, as well as what is 
specifically needed from me to progress to a PO II.
Based on Tyson’s IT Job Architecture, my understanding of the core PO I responsibilities 
includes:
Collaborating with the Product Manager and cross -functional teams to understand 
the voice of the customer.•
Translating requirements into actionable user stories and acceptance criteria.•
Participating in sprint ceremonies.•
Assisting in guiding the team in delivering high -quality, customer -focused 
solutions.•
Driving continuous improvement.•
Over the past year, there has been overlap between roles. In some cases, responsibilities such 
as backlog creation, acceptance criteria definition, and roadmap planning have primarily been 
handled by others. I have offered to participate in or take ownership of these activities but was 
redirected or told they would be addressed later (which is what I’m now using this email for).
While I acknowledge I could have advocated more strongly from the get go , I am still 
continuing to learn the most effective ways to communicate within the team structure and 
adapt to corporate processes. My goal is to ensure alignment so I can fully deliver on the 
expectations for my role.
2. Requests for realignment
I have previously requested a clear delineation of my responsibilities versus those of the 
Product Manager and Team Manager. I believe it would be valuable to have a documented 
agreement so both Andre and I are fully aligned on:
My direct responsibilities.•
His direct responsibilities.•
Where review, visibility, and feedback are most appropriate.•
As Agile best practices outline:
Product Manager: Owns whyand what big things are built (roadmap, initiative -
level prioritization).•
Product Owner: Owns what exactly and when in sprints (backlog management, 
story writing, acceptance criteria).•
Team Manager: Owns who and how we staff and enable the team (resourcing, 
performance management).•
Clear boundaries here would help reduce role overlap, prevent duplication of effort, and ensure 
performance is measured against my core responsibilities.
3. Additional work and responsibilities
Over the past year, I have taken on responsibilities outside the PO I profile, including:
SAP/BOM Portal reconciliation SQL query development.•
Power BI design specifications, data model reviews, and dashboard•
These contributions were made in good faith to support the team, but they 
represent off -scope work that I believe should be recognized separately from my 
core PO performance.•
4. What I need to be successful
Role clarity: A documented agreement on PO I vs. Product Manager vs. Team 
Manager responsibilities.•
Scope boundaries: Confirmation of which additional tasks are permanent versus 
temporary or evolving.•
Training plan: Identification of skills to develop for career progression without 
impacting current performance metrics.•
Consistent involvement: Inclusion in all relevant stakeholder and UX/UI meetings 
where my input is valuable.•
Feedback cadence: Regular check -ins to review progress against role expectations 
and overall team alignment.•
5. Why this alignment matters now
The lack of clearly defined boundaries has, at times, impacted deliverables. For example, when 
dependent work items (such as a card remaining in validation after a sprint without clear next 
steps) are delayed, it directly affects my ability to close related tasks, impacts reporting 
timelines, and can create downstream rework. Documented ownership and role definitions 
would ensure accountability, streamline handoffs, and protect delivery commitments.
I am committed to improving where needed and to fully aligning with expectations once they 
are clearly defined. I look forward to discussing this with you next week so we can finalize role 
alignment and ensure performance measurement is based on agreed -upon responsibilities.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Best regards,
Greg Homstad

   accomodations Page 65    
Standardization to GitLab
Transition to GitLab : •
All projects and corresponding blocks for generating services and web apps are moving to GitLab. 
Azure will no longer be used for deployments and will be decommissioned.
Pipeline Changes : •
Two different pipelines need to be established in GitLab for services and web applications.
Material and Recipe Management
BOM Portal and Recipe Management :
Material BOM (CS03) : SAP transaction code used to display Material BOM. •
Recipe BOM (C203) : SAP transaction code used to display Recipe BOM. •
BOM Portal : Focuses only on production line recipes. •
Recipe Management : Ensures production recipes are updated and synchronized with the 
BOM Portal.••
Enterprise Change Management (ECM)
ECM Lifecycle :
Manages changes in how the BOM is manufactured from a cost accounting perspective 
rather than formulation.•
Changes involve both stock and non -stock components that are sent to SAP. ••
Handling of Seconds Material
Seconds Material : •
Represents downgraded material. Discussions are needed on managing and justifying exceptions 
for seconds all the way back to PDM.
Communication Between Systems
PDM to BOM Portal : •
PDM sends notifications to the BOM Portal about specification changes. BOM Portal acts as the 
authoritative container for master formulation data.
Synchronization :
Weekly synchronization checks between PDM, PLM, and BOM.•
PLM creates and decentralizes an official product payload containing material data and 
converts it into a BOM -readable format.•
Regular reconciliation ensures synchronization between BOM and PDM.•
NService Notifications : ••
NService is used for receiving notifications about specification changes, which are integrated into 
a weekly reconciliation process.
Reconciliation and Data Integrity
Weekly and Daily Reconciliation :
Daily comparisons check BOM and PDM data for around 1,000 recipes.•
Weekly reports reconcile BOM and PDM data, cross -referencing with PLM requests to 
ensure they are in sync.••
Splunk Dashboards and Logging
Dashboard Updates : •
Splunk dashboards are updated to enhance logging capabilities, using Redis and SignalR web 
sockets for quick reconnections and IP address assignments.
Ensuring Data Flow and Specification Changes
Data Flow :
PDM sends formulation data to BOM Portal and ESP (Engineering Service Portal).•
SAP receives recipe -level data from the BOM Portal. •
Regular checks confirm that specification changes are applied correctly.•

What are the main reasons for moving away from the "Master -Formulation" label, 
and what alternative terminology will be adopted?
Master formulation results vaguely define and doesn’t provide the correct 
description on the efforts defined within the project’s scope; it was loosely 
labeled as Master Formulation since the previous project was named Official 
Master Data. In essence both Official Master Data and Master Formulation refer 
to the same portion, which is implement an authoritative layer of date 
representing true Formulations, replicating an isolated entity within BOM 
Portal.1.1.
What challenges are anticipated during the "soft" migration process, and how will 2.•
   Andre KT Page 67    
What challenges are anticipated during the "soft" migration process, and how will 
they be mitigated? Additionally, what distinguishes a "soft" migration from a "hard" 
migration?
A soft migration was chosen since it would allow the internal skillset to 
accommodate per the ETL process. Basically a soft migration will allow to isolate 
plants to have new BOMs (vs LEGACY Recipe entities) and others to maintain 
the Legacy recipe design. With this we keep a duality , but also represents 
challenges between data transition and data integrity.1.2.
How will new software platforms, such as Microsoft Blazor, be integrated and utilized 
in the migration process?
Blazor is an equivalent to .NET CORE, in which case this will hold all mid -tier and 
front -end level features, a first draft to demonstrate that functionalities can be 
moved into blazor is the integration of the “Formulation Viewer” which is an 
angular -based API that represents the state of transition of that formulation 
replication level. The actual plan needs to be reviewed with Development team; 
you can see a first draft of that on the corresponding user stories for Blazor 
integration.1.3.

How will the system validate that only accurate and compliant BOMs are included in 
the BOM Portal? What criteria will be used to determine their validity?
BOMs are a unique combination of material:cost -center (since cost centers are 
plant specific), and BOM Templates can only represent a unique Formulation.1.
Normalization reduces discrepancies and integrity violations, of any sort, either 
entity integrity violation, transactional integrity violation etc.2.
Modeling schema prior to actual definition allows the visualization of multiple 
constraints that can be implemented between joins and relations (assume 
relation as an abstraction of table)3.
Follow business rules that dictate the correct integration of schema to reduce 
integrity violations (refer to previous attachment).4.
Implement software engineering best practices.5.10.

versions of their associated formulations during testing?
Schema design constraints:
Formulation ID is a FK on BOM Template, this tuple represents the 
uniqueness of the formulation gathered in a 
suitable/engineerable/consumable BOM Template, that is the central 
entity of BOM versioning. In this way we ensure that BOM Templates will 
only point at a unique Formulation ID and that a Formulation must 
uniquely represent a Material ID.1.1.
What strategy will be employed to manage different inventory points (such as ZFG 
and ZSWP) and their relationships across multiple plants?
Hard question to answer right now, I need to give you more details on what 
inventory points are from a costing perspective and what bad practices have 
been merged into them because of BOM deficiency. Inventory points will be 
exclusively part of BOM Versions and they will not be dependent on any 
formulation level data, as they will be isolated from any transaction 
corresponding to formulation/bom template levels. 1.12.
   Andre KT Page 69    
A bill of materials (BOM) is a comprehensive list of all the materials, 
components, and subassemblies required to manufacture a product. A 
BOM essentially provides a structure for making a product repeatably 
every time, thereby introducing a basis for standardization in the 
production process.
A bill of materials will contain the quantity or volume of each component 
used but it may also include additional information such as:
production routings•
production cycle times•
production costs•
waste factors•
other work -center data required to produce the finished item. •

PLM creates and decentralizes an official product payload containing material data and

NService is used for receiving notifications about specification changes, which are integrated into

ECM Lifecycle :
Manages changes in how the BOM is manufactured from a cost accounting perspective 
rather than formulation.•
Changes involve both stock and non -stock components that are sent to SAP. ••
Legacy System Integration
PDM (Product Data Management): Serves as the source of record for material formulations. •
Updated PDM Data Handling: PDM data is now translated into BOM format by an interceptor in •Thursday, May 08, 2025 10:43 AM
   Andre KT Page 70    
Updated PDM Data Handling: PDM data is now translated into BOM format by an interceptor in 
the BOM portal.•
PLM (Product Lifecycle Management): Acts as an intermediary, receiving the latest version of 
formulations in XML format. The BOM portal translates this data without relying on the 
destination.•
Legacy Structure:
Internally, there is no "BOM" in the current implementation; only recipes exist.•
Recipes span BOMs, and BOMs span recipes, but recipes may exist without BOMs. Recipes contain 
non-authoritative copies of corporate formulations, regularly edited due to various structural and 
quantitative inputs.•
Goal Structure:
The revised implementation will store data from external systems separately from BOMs 
and update only with changes propagated from the source of record.•
An intermediate "Template" layer corresponding to plant cost centers will provide 
additional data de -duplication and consistency guarantees.•
Versions will only include version -specific data. •••
Project Overview:
Recipe -to-BOM Migration: Focuses on migrating the existing BOM Portal's structure from 
"Recipes" to "Bill of Materials" (BOM). The current structure is inaccurate, and the goal is to 
redesign it to be fundamentally accurate and align with business requirements.•
Master Formulation: The project revolves around the concept of a "Master Data -Formulation 
Replica," which should not be referred to as "Master -Formulation."•1.
Migration Plan:
Software Update and Data Migration: Implement new software platforms and design a new 
schema that aligns with business requirements. Migrate data from the old schema to the new 
functional schema.•
User -Case Migration: Transition from "Soft" migration to a more manual user -case migration 
approach.•2.
Plant -Specific Migration: The approach will involve selecting specific plants to isolate and migrate 
materials.•
Objectives:
Core Redesign: Redefine the core functionality of the BOM Portal, transitioning from Recipes to 
BOMs.•
Data Structure Consistency: Enforce a single approved schema for BOMs and ensure all BOM 
entities have consistent data relationships (one driver, one root step node, etc.).•3.
BOM Templates and Versions:
Unique BOM Structures: Each combination of plant, material, and cost center should have a 
unique BOM structure.•
BOM Templates as Drivers: BOM Templates serve as the "driver" models that define the 
reference for BOM versions.•4.
Validation and Consistency:5.
Formulation and BOM Validation: The document outlines rules for BOM consistency, validation of 
material types, SAP version numbers, date ranges, etc.•
Inventory Points and Material Requirements: There are specific rules around inventory points, such as 
ZCWPs (Costing Work in Process) and other inventory point types.•
Creation and Editing Rules:
Templates and Versions: Procedures for creating and editing BOM Templates and Versions are 
specified, including rules for data retention, step yields, material data, etc.•
Submission to SAP: The document details conditions and requirements for submitting BOMs to 
SAP.•6.
Local Change Management:
Enterprise Change Management: Describes how local changes to BOMs should be managed, with 
a focus on maintaining consistency and avoiding overlapping changes.•7.

Questions for Clarification
Migration Approach:
What are the specific reasons for transitioning from a "Soft" migration to a manual user -case 
migration approach? Are there particular challenges with the "Soft" migration that the manual

preserving references to formulations and formulation -associated attributes. How does this 
relationship work in practice? Will formulations be directly linked to BOMs or just referenced?•2.
Template and Version Definitions:
Could you provide more details on what constitutes a "driver" BOM, particularly in the context of 
Finance and Production BOM types? How do these differ in terms of structure and purpose?•
What specific conditions dictate whether a BOM is a Finance BOM or a Production BOM in Co -
Packer Plants?•3.
Validation and Consistency Requirements:
The rules around BOM validation are extensive. Are there existing tools or software that will 
automate these checks, or will these validations require manual oversight?•
Could you elaborate on how the "Consistency Scan" for BOMs will be implemented, and what 
technologies or methodologies will be used for this?•4.
Data Handling and Submission:

plant location. Can you clarify if this will be an automated process, or if manual intervention will 
be necessary? What criteria determine this?•5.

you explain how these changes differ, and how they will be managed to avoid conflicts or 
overlaps?•6.

functionality of the BOM Portal?•7.

Knowledge Transfer Session: Functional Aspects of BOM Portal
Introduction
Host: Good [morning/afternoon], everyone. Thank you for joining today's Knowledge Transfer 
Session. The purpose of this session is to provide a comprehensive understanding of the functional 
aspects of the BOM Portal. By the end of this session, you should have a clear grasp of how to 
effectively use the portal for managing Bill of Materials.
Host: Let's start with a brief overview of the BOM Portal. The BOM Portal is a centralized platform 
designed to streamline the creation, management, and integration of Bill of Materials across various 
systems within our organization.
Functional Overview
Host: The BOM Portal offers several key features:
Creation and Management of BOMs: Users can create, edit, and manage BOMs efficiently. 1.
Integration with Other Systems: The portal integrates seamlessly with other enterprise 
systems such as ERP and PLM. SAP and PDM2.
User Roles and Permissions: Different user roles and permissions ensure secure and 
controlled access to the portal.3.
Detailed Functionalities
Host: Let's dive deeper into the functionalities of the BOM Portal.
Host: BOM Creation Process:
To create a BOM, navigate to the 'Create BOM' section. Fill in the required fields such as BOM 
name, description, and components. Once completed, click 'Save' to create the BOM.•
Host: Editing and Updating BOMs:
To edit an existing BOM, go to the 'Manage BOMs' section. Select the BOM you wish to edit, 
make the necessary changes, and click 'Update' to save the changes.•
Host: Version Control:
The portal maintains version control for all BOMs. Each time a BOM is updated, a new version 
is created, allowing users to track changes over time.•
Host: Search and Filter Options:
Use the search bar and filter options to quickly locate specific BOMs. Filters can be applied 
based on various criteria such as date, components, and status.•
Host: Reporting and Analytics:
The portal offers robust reporting and analytics features. Users can generate reports on BOM 
usage, component costs, and other metrics to gain insights into their operations.•
Best PracticesThursday, May 08, 2025 1:23 PM
   Andre KT Page 73    
Best Practices
Host: Here are some best practices to keep in mind:
Data Entry: Ensure accurate and efficient data entry to maintain the integrity of BOMs. 1.
Maintaining Consistency: Use standardized naming conventions and formats to maintain 
consistency across BOMs.2.
Security Measures: Follow security protocols to protect sensitive information within the portal. 3.
Q&A Session
Host: Now, let's open the floor for questions. Feel free to ask any questions or clarify any doubts you 
may have about the BOM Portal.
Conclusion
Host: To wrap up, let's summarize the key points we covered today:
Overview of BOM Portal functionalities•
Detailed guide on creating, editing, and managing BOMs•
Best practices for using the portal•
Host: Thank you for your participation. If you have any further questions or need additional 
resources, please don't hesitate to reach out. Have a great day!

Scope of Programs in ZParm:
ZParm : In SAP, ZParm typically refers to a customized or user -defined set of configuration parameters or 
programs. These parameters control the behavior of specific SAP functions or processes, ensuring 
efficient data transportation and smooth business operations.•
Programs in ZParm include:
EIM: Enterprise Information Management programs, focusing on data integration, quality, and 
governance.1.
Disassembly : Processes that involve breaking down materials or finished goods into components. 2.
Standard Recipe Verification : Validation of recipes or production formulas to ensure compliance 
with standard procedures.3.•
Actions Performed with This Change:
Objective : The programs in ZParm control data flow, transportation, and material withdrawals, and any 
disruption in these programs would require immediate detection due to their importance to operations.•
What Was Done :
Changes were made to each program within the ZParm set to ensure they function correctly with 
no disruptions.•
CS16 Query Using Wildcard for BOM :
CS16 is a SAP transaction used to analyze the Bill of Materials (BOM) structure.•
In this case, a wildcard search was applied within the text field of the BOM to broaden the 
scope and ensure all relevant BOMs were captured.•
URL Path Verification : Every program in ZParm was analyzed to ensure the corresponding 
URLs referenced within the system paths were accurate and up -to-date.••
Results :
Verified Results :
The review confirmed there were no disruptions to operations during or after the 
change.•
Data transportation, material withdrawals, and other key functionalities continued to 
function smoothly.••
Note on Scope of Change :
The change was applied in SAP, not the BOM Portal : This distinction ensures the 
modifications were limited to the backend system (SAP) rather than the user -facing 
BOM Portal, suggesting a focus on backend configuration without altering the user 
interface or portal functionalities.••
This ensures the transportation process, material management, and operational consistency 
remain unaffected while confirming the accuracy and relevance of the modifications. Let me know 
if any further details are required!•Zparm Document Creation

   Manager One on One Page 89    
Overview of the Code Snippet:
updateMaterialAsync Method :
A function that takes plantMaterialModel and plantCode as arguments. •
Uses proxies['plantConfigHub'].invoke.apply() to call UpdateMaterialAsync on 
the server via SignalR.•
AngularJS Context ( $scope.updateMaterialAsync ):
$scope.updateMaterialAsync is a method that calls the server -side 
updateMaterialAsync using the jQuery 
$.connection.plantConfigHub.server.updateMaterialAsync method.•
Uses .done() to handle the promise, updating the material and triggering $scope.
$apply() to update the AngularJS data binding.•
Logs a success message using window.addEventLog() when synchronization completes. •
AngularJS $apply() :
A core AngularJS method that evaluates expressions and triggers a $digest() cycle 
to update the view with any data changes.•
This ensures that changes made within the function are reflected in the UI.•••
Execution Flow Analysis:
Client -Side Initialization :
The updateMaterialAsync function is triggered from the client (e.g., via a UI event). •
The method makes a call using proxies['plantConfigHub'].invoke() to execute 
UpdateMaterialAsync on the server -side plantConfigHub .•
Server -Side Request :
The request generated by invoke() is sent to the server through the SignalR 
connection.•
The server -side SignalR hub ( plantConfigHub ) receives the request and processes 
it, executing the UpdateMaterialAsync method.•
Server Response Handling :
Once the server completes the execution of UpdateMaterialAsync , it returns 
a response.•
The .done() callback on the client -side $scope.updateMaterialAsync
function processes this response. The materialModel returned is used to 
update the material , and $scope.$apply() ensures the AngularJS UI 
reflects this change.•
A log entry is added to indicate that the synchronization is complete.•
Data Binding and UI Updates :
$scope.$apply() triggers a $digest() cycle, which updates the UI by 
checking for changes in the material object and applying them to the 
bound view.•1.1.1.1.•11/6/24

   Weekly Meeting Page 97    
New URL Migration and User Monitoring:
31 unique users are using the new URL in production (excluding developers and analysts).•
The migration process is underway with plans to fully switch users to the new URL.•
Monitoring will continue to ensure smooth transition before decommissioning the old URL.•
A report from Okta logs provided insights on user roles and access to plants, with 17% having 
access to multiple plants.•
Performance Issues & Adjustments:
SignalR connection timeout was initially set to 5 minutes, now adjusted to 110 seconds.•
Testing in the lower environment confirmed that reducing the timeout resolves most issues.•
Deployment to production is pending resolution of minor deployment issues.•
Risk Assessment & Mitigation:
Adjusting the timeout setting is considered low risk.•
There is a process to revert to the 5 -minute timeout if needed. •
Monitoring the change will continue in QA and lower environments to ensure stability.•
Communication & User Transition Strategy:
Regular communication will inform users to switch to the new URL.•
A ticket will be created for the security team to track old vs. new URL usage.•
Follow -ups will target users who haven’t switched to the new URL, with direct 
communication to address issues.•
Hypercare Plan:
Proposal to implement a two -week Hypercare period post -cutover. •
Final decision on timing (two weeks vs. one month) remains open for 
discussion.•
Regular monitoring during Hypercare to ensure all users are on the new 
URL.•
Decommissioning the Old URL:
The old URL will remain active until most users transition to the new 
one.•
The transition will only be finalized when users are comfortable, 
following communication reminders.•
Monitoring usage patterns to determine the optimal shutdown date 
for the old URL.•1.1.

List all new components added in phase 2.•
Include screenshots or wireframes of the new components.•

Describe the purpose and functionality of each component.•
Detail user interactions (e.g., button clicks, form submissions).•
Provide design specifications (e.g., dimensions, color schemes, fonts).•
Document is Accessible on SharePoint:
Upload the updated functional spec document to the designated SharePoint folder.•

Title: Phase 2 –Implementation Process: BOM Structure Reconciliation for Recipe -Component
Description
As a BOM Portal Product Owner, I need to implement a detailed reconciliation process for BOM 
components at a granular level, ensuring that the structure of BOMs is accurately reflected and 
reconciled with the recipe components.
Acceptance Criteria
Implementation must be documented and agreed upon with the Product Manager:
Create a detailed implementation plan.•
Obtain Product Manager approval.•
Data components must be reconciled with the current set on BOM Portal:
Compare and reconcile BOM components with SAP components.•
Address and resolve all discrepancies.•
Ensure all components of recipes are accurately defined and reconciled:
Define and reconcile input/packaging components and their attributes.•
Include values relevant to cost accounting.•
Business Statement / Value
Accurate reconciliation of BOM components ensures data integrity and supports cost accounting and 
inventory management.
Technical Details
Develop and run scripts to reconcile data across tables (e.g., RECIPE_FORMULATION_XREF, 
FORMULATION_STEP, CORP_FORMULATION_STEP).•
Validate the reconciled data to ensure accuracy.•
(1 Point)
Title: Monitor PowerBI Report Health and Data Integrity
Description
As an analyst, I want to actively monitor the PowerBI report for data load success, data accuracy, and

Respond to Issues:
Document and escalate any discrepancies or failures to the appropriate team.•
Technical Details
Utilize PowerBI Service refresh history and dataset health dashboards.•
Work with DICE and GCP ingestion teams as needed for backend data integrity.•
Maintain a monitoring log accessible to the team on SharePoint.•

Card 2
Title: Define Query Logic for Component -Level Reconciliation
Description:
Develop and document reusable SQL logic that supports comparison of component -level recipe data 
across SAP and the BOM Portal. This includes ensuring accurate calculation and alignment of fields such 
as usage %, scrap %, material numbers, and cost -relevant attributes.
Acceptance Criteria:
List of reconciliation data points (e.g., usage %, scrap %, material description, UOM) is defined.•
SQL logic is created and validated for comparing BOM Portal and SAP data.•
Query results are reviewed for alignment on key component -level values. •
Logic is reusable across multiple material/plant/version scenarios.•
Card 3
Title: Business Requirements Definition for Component Reconciliation
Description:

between SAP and BOM Portal. Define which values need to align, the business impact of mismatches, 
and thresholds for acceptable variances.
Acceptance Criteria:

Cost -relevant fields and key attributes are identified and prioritized.•
Document includes a clear business statement and rationale.•

   What I'm Doing Page 122    
Phase 1: Recipe Header Analysis
Investigate throughput and crewing issues•
Examine system discrepancies and validate if they're tied to how data is being pulled or 
structured.
Review join conditions•
Ensure recipe headers, resources, and operations are correctly linked with no orphaned or 
duplicated joins.
Validate control key filtering logic•
Confirm that only relevant operations (e.g., PI05) are being evaluated for throughput and 
crewing.
Determine logic for most recent records•
Standardize approach for pulling only the latest valid entry for each record (e.g., max valid -from 
date).
Run initial header query•
Execute a standalone query to validate the logic and return recipe metadata.
Phase 2: Operations Data Extraction
Build operation -level query •
Query operation steps tied to recipes, including resources and control keys.
Verify costing relevance•
Ensure costing indicators (e.g., flags or checkboxes) are available and interpretable.
Validate throughput and crewing pull•
Confirm the right operation types are being evaluated for performance metrics.
Cross -check operation scope•
Filter to include only active and relevant operation steps for the target production recipes.
Phase 3: Material Information
Extract BOM structure data•
Retrieve BOMs, components, and how they relate to primary materials and recipes.
Include operational context•
Connect BOM components to their operation steps for traceability and usage tracking.
Validate item -specific metadata •
Ensure values like scrap percentage, category, and phantom indicators are accessible and 
accurate.
Request ingestion for missing BOM Portal tables•
Identify BOM Portal material/component tables not yet ingested and submit formal requests 
(e.g., for items such as item category, change number, phantom status, etc.).
Phase 4: Reconciliation & Modeling
Compare extracted data to BOM Portal UI•
Manually or programmatically align data from queries with what's shown in the BOM Portal.
Identify and document discrepancies•
Track mismatches in metrics, missing data, or out -of-sync records.
Prepare data for Power BI•
Shape and clean the dataset for modeling, applying standard naming and formatting rules.
Collaborate on dashboard development•
Support the visualization team (e.g., Ayesha) with field mappings and layout feedback.
Log ingestion dependencies•
Track BOM Portal or SAP tables that were required for reconciliation but not initially available.
Phase 5: Documentation Updates
Revise reconciliation documentation•
Update logic, steps, and findings in the reconciliation procedure document.
Maintain updated data dictionary•
Ensure every table and column is defined, with sources and transformation logic.
Track table ingestion requests and approvals•
Maintain a running list of all pending and completed table ingestions, who requested them, their 
status, and why each was needed for reconciliation.Phase 4: Reconciliation & Modeling

TITLE: Ingestion Process of BOM Component Data 
Description :

data into GCP so that all fields required for SAP reconciliation audit reporting are consistently available, 
validated, and mapped for downstream use in analytics and reconciliation reporting.
This includes validating the source data structure, coordinating ingestion with data engineering, and ensuring 
BOM_Portal and SAP datasets are aligned to support 1:1 component -level reconciliation.
Acceptance Criteria: 
Identify and document all BOM component -related tables required for reconciliation
Validate that each required field (e.g., material, usage %, scrap %, step reference) is available in the 
source MySQL tables•6/24/2025

3 Points -AUDIT –BOM & SAP Component Material Reconciliation
Description
As a BOM Portal IT Product Owner, I will develop a query that reconciles component -level materials 
between BOM Portal and SAP, identifying mismatches. The goal is to flag discrepancies where a 
component exists in one system but not the other, under a matched header context. This query will also 
explicitly handle cases where component material numbers differ while headers match, using a 
composite key for precision.
Acceptance Criteria
Discrepancy report includes:•
"Not on SAP" when a component is present in BOM Portal but missing in SAP. •
"Not on BOM Portal" when a component is present in SAP but missing in BOM Portal. •
Logic ensures headers match first before identifying component -level mismatches to minimize 
noise.•
Query uses composite key (Plant + Header Material + BOM Version + BOM Type + Step Code) for 
accurate reconciliation.•
Validated with Phuong for SAP accuracy and business logic alignment.•

Time spent putting this document together: 3:00PM
1. Acknowledge and Reframe
Thank you for taking the time to share your perspective and outline next steps for me. I value clear 
communication and this opportunity to ensure we’re aligned on expectations, responsibilities, and how I 
can best meet them. 
2. Address the “Disconnect” Directly
From my perspective, a key challenge has been a fundamental disconnect between how my role was 
defined at the outset, how responsibilities were communicated, and the ongoing support provided. 
Many of my daily activities were based on my original onboarding, early direction from leadership, and 
the team workflows I was integrated into. If those expectations have since evolved, I believe we haven’t 
yet had a structured conversation that fully realigned them.
3. Respond to Specific Concerns
I recognize the importance of timely task completion, backlog management, and serving as a central 
contact for developers and stakeholders. I also acknowledge that there are areas where I can improve 
execution and efficiency. At the same time, I believe additional clarity on prioritization, ownership 
boundaries, and the process for receiving task requirements would help me perform these duties at the 
expected level.
4. Highlight Efforts and Additional Responsibilities
Over the past several months, I have also taken on responsibilities outside the core Product Owner 
scope, such as [list 3 –4 key examples]. These have taken considerable time and effort, and while I have 
been committed to delivering on them, they may have reduced the bandwidth available for other 
duties. Including these contributions in our assessment will give us a more complete picture of my work.
Answer the “What do you need…” question within your rebuttal instead of leaving it only for the 
assessment form. This shows proactiveness.
Example:
. Outline What You Need for Success
To be successful in this role, I believe I need:
Role clarity –A clear, agreed -upon list of core responsibilities and priorities. •

communications.•

Support & feedback –Timely feedback on deliverables so I can adjust in real -time. •
6. Close with Commitment
End by reaffirming your commitment to improving and aligning with expectations.
Example:
I remain fully committed to improving in all areas discussed and meeting the expectations of the 
Product Owner role. I look forward to continuing the conversation, aligning on the action plan, and 
demonstrating consistent progress in the weeks ahead.
