Tyson Confidential
FY25 Material Requirements Planning
and Bills of Material Audit
Detailed Audit Report Report Date: December 20, 2024
Observation Area H M I Total
SAP MRP Procedures -1 1 2
Materials Management Procedures -1 -1
BOM Data Integrity 1 1 -2
Specification Deviations -1 -1
Inventory Management --1 1
Total 1 4 2 7
Audit Services (AS) conducted an audit of material 
management processes for both the Poultry and Beef and Pork 
business units. Prepared Foods was excluded due to the
upcoming SAP Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
implementation. As part of the audit the following scope areas 
were reviewed:
• Poultry SAP MRP and Related Processes 
• Beef and Pork Materials Management 
• Bill of Material (BOM) Management 
• Select Plant Inventory Management Processes 
AS analyzed key performance metrics, conducted process 
walkthroughs and performed detailed testing to identify 
potential risks and possible opportunities.
Background and Overview: 
Having the right materials in the right place at the right time in the right quantities is key to production 
schedule adherence and meeting customer 
demand. The Procurement team is leading the implementation of SAP MRP to improve materials 
planning processes. 
MRP helps to plan material needs and recommends purchasing and production activities with the goal 
of meeting demand and improving overall 
productivity. Safety stock levels, on hand inventory, lead times, BOMs and production schedules drive 
automated material requisitions and suggested 
production schedule changes. MRP has been rolled out to nearly all Poultry plants, with Prepared Foods 
slated to begin in January 2025. In addition 
to the SAP implementation, Regional Supply Managers have been added to oversee material 
management processes and provide a broader lens
across plants to solution material management issues.
While Beef and Pork is not using SAP MRP the business unit does manage materials using data on 
historical usage, lead times, BOMs and production 
forecasts to understand material needs. However, these processes are extremely manual with 
significant system limitations, and the process is
different across plants due to lack of standard operating procedures (SOPs) and disparate warehousing 
systems. 
Poultry MRP and Beef and Pork materials management processes are generally working as intended, but 
BOM management should be improved to 
ensure BOM inputs are accurate and plants are using the most up -to-date BOMs. Additionally, 
specification deviation processes should be reviewed 
to ensure they remain temporary in nature and are tracked and visible. While Prepared Foods was out 
of scope for the audit, this business unit is 
impacted by the BOM, specification deviation and MRP SOP issues identified and these opportunities 
will be included in the remediation efforts. 
Communication Priority: Red –High Level Finding; Yellow –Medium Level Finding; Green –Opportunity 
for Process/Control Enhancement
Tyson 
Audit Services
Tyson Confidential 2
Observation
Risk: If BOM data is not aligned between the BOM Portal and SAP the plants may run process orders that 
do not have an accurate, up -to-date and
approved BOM. Inaccurate BOM data in SAP impacts the effectiveness of MRP processes and could 
cause operational disruptions as plants and 
Corporate teams such as R&D, Finance and IT manage issues/errors. Additionally, forecasting processes 
are less accurate due to BOM discrepancies. 
Observation: 
BOMs are created, reviewed and managed in BOM Portal. A Finance BOM is created first and then 
cloned to a production BOM. Complete and 
approved BOMs are then fed to SAP via a process initiated through BOM Portal. The original production 
BOM for each material at each plant is a 3031 
BOM and alternate BOMs can be created for different production lines or formulations that should have 
the same specs as the 3031. When plants run 
a process order for a material using the BOM in SAP each component they scan to the process order will Include known problems to defend your case with audit 
OM Portal project -Data streaming policies

YesBOMs are created, reviewed and managed in BOM Portal. A Finance BOM is created first and then 
cloned to a production BOM. Complete and 
approved BOMs are then fed to SAP via a process initiated through BOM Portal. The original production 
BOM for each material at each plant is a 3031 
BOM and alternate BOMs can be created for different production lines or formulations that should have 
the same specs as the 3031. When plants run 
a process order for a material using the BOM in SAP each component they scan to the process order will 
be checked against the 3031 BOM in BOM 
Portal. If the component is not on the 3031 BOM or the 3031 BOM does not exist in BOM Portal the 
plant will receive a “not on BOM” message and 
should not complete the process order. 
There are known issues with BOMs being deleted from BOM Portal and not SAP. AS selected eight plants 
and compared the available and active 
BOMs in SAP to the corresponding BOMs in BOM Portal. The following inconsistencies were identified:
• There were 87 active BOMs found in SAP that were not in BOM Portal. Of these BOMs, 13 were 
recently used for process orders. 
• Of the 13 BOMs recently used for process orders, 11 BOMs had a 3031 BOM in BOM Portal. Two BOMs 
did not have a 3031 BOM in BOM 
Portal. AS noted the primary ingredient in these BOMs was a disassembly WIP that is a valid exception 
to the control.
Other known gaps:
• BOM Portal does not require phantom BOMs to be sent to SAP when transmitting production BOM 
data. 
• Certain spec changes to BOMs will overwrite existing valid data points in the BOMs. 
• When BOM changes are dated in the future the component will sometimes be deleted in BOM Portal 
but not in SAP.
There is currently no process to reconcile the data between BOM Portal and SAP, as BOM Portal 
reporting is limited and inaccurate. A BOM Portal 
Master Formulations project is underway to resolve this as well as the spec change overwrite issue 
noted above. Discrepancies between the BOMs in 
BOM Portal versus SAP may result in the plants not having needed materials or ordering materials they 
are unable to use. The teams who are 
managing or using BOMs spend a great deal of time addressing and correcting data issues. BOMs that 
could not be resolved by a team member 
resulted in ~900 IT tickets during FY24.
Recommendation:
A reconciliation control should be developed between BOM Portal and SAP which will require the 
development of BOM Portal reporting/data extraction. 
Additionally, functionality should be added to BOM Portal to clarify for the user that “Delete” will not 
remove a BOM from SAP, but rather is used when a 
BOM may need to be rebuilt to match SAP if the BOM Portal BOM is corrupted.
Approved Action Plan
The BOM Portal Master Formulations project underway will result in BOM Portal data being available in 
GCP. The project is scheduled to be completed 
9/30/2025. Completion of this process will allow for the creation of an automated, near real -time 
detailed reconciliation process between SAP and the 
BOM Portal. In the interim, the creation of a semi -automated/manual reconciliation mechanism at the 
BOM level will be explored to mitigate this risk 
sooner than the December 31, 2025 due date for the fully automated, near real -time reconciliation.
H #1 –BOM Data Integrity (Poultry)
Tyson Confidential 3
Action Plan Owners: Ken McCreary, Director Cost Accounting COE; Allen Shanks, Sr. Director Corporate 
Accounting; John Tran, Sr. Manager 
Applications; Jerrett Ableitner, Sr Director Applications; Chelsey Marr, Managing Director Information 
Systems 
Due Date: December 31, 2025
Tyson Confidential 4
Observation
Risk: If BOM data is not aligned between the mainframe and the Pack database the plants may run 
process orders that do not have an accurate, up to -date and approved BOM. This can lead to 
inefficiencies with material planning processes, potential operational disruptions and inaccurate 
forecasting.
Observation: 
Beef and Pork packaging BOMs are created, reviewed and updated in the Pack database and transferred 
to New Product Development (NPD) at the 
Corporate level. Updated BOMs are available to plants in PDF format and plants manually update the 
BOM data in the mainframe. 
AS selected 10 plant -level BOMs in the mainframe, compared their key data fields to the packaging 
specifications in NPD and the unit costing in Stores
and noted the following:
•  our of the 10 plant -level BOMs in the mainframe contained inactive or incorrect part numbers 
compared to the packaging specifications NPD 
document. Pack database uses a nine -character alphanumeric part number, but the mainframe system, 
managed by Plant Accountants, uses a 
seven -digit numeric code. Plant Accountants manually convert part numbers from Pack database, often 
leading to errors and mismatches. 
Two of the 10 plant -level BOMs had variances between the unit cost of part numbers in the mainframe 
compared to the unit cost per the Stores 
system. This was due to unit prices not being updated in the mainframe.
• Overall, AS noted a misalignment in the units of measure used in BOMs for standard usage. The unit of 
measure plants order against from 
suppliers and the unit of measure plants use to manage their inventory does not match, resulting in 
discrepancies when compared to the Pack 
database system.
Recommendation:
To help reduce errors and improve efficiency in the BOM updating process, Beef and Pork should 
consider implementing a process to eliminate the 
manual nature of BOM updates or, at minimum, a reconciliation control.
Approved Action Plan
• The SpecRight application is currently being implemented, which will replace Pack database. As part of 
this implementation packaging
specifications are being reviewed and migrated to SpecRight. 
• Once this is complete, a PowerBI dashboard will be created to house the BOM data. Reporting will be 
run out of PowerBI and the mainframe in 
parallel for a period of time to ensure the process is functioning as designed. With the implementation 
of the PowerBI dashboard, reviews of 
units of measure and costing will occur in partnership with the Finance team to ensure the data is 
accurate and aligned to the mainframe for 
inventory purposes. From that point all BOM data will be housed in SpecRight and PowerBI, eliminating 
the need for the plants to update 
separate BOMs in the mainframe. This effort should be completed by 9/30/2025.
Action Plan Owners: Johnathon Tran, Sr. Manager Packaging; Tendai Dete, Director, Beef and Pork 
Accounting; Carl West, Beef and Pork 
Accounting; Stephanie Teague, Director, Beef and Pork Control 
Due Date: September 30, 2025
M #2 –BOM Data Integrity (Beef and Pork)
Tyson Confidential 5
Observation
Risk: If a product is continuously produced using a product specification deviation rather than the 
approved BOM, it impairs data accuracy for costing, 
material ordering and sourcing strategies. Additionally, if specification deviations are not visibly tracked 
with end dates plants may use the deviation 
longer than necessary.
Observation: 
Specification deviations are requested when a plant must use a component (e.g., ingredient, packaging, 
WIP) that is not listed on the BOM, either 
because they do not have a required component, a required component is unable be used due to plant 
equipment limitations or they have excess 
components they would like to use. These specification deviations must be approved by FSQA or other 
relevant corporate team members (e.g., 
Product Managers, R&D, Packaging). A plant can only use the component for the process order once the 
specification deviation is approved.
For Poultry, AS noted from review of specification deviation reports and inquiry with FSQA that 
specification deviations are often requested for 90 days
which is typically the maximum number of days a deviation can be approved. If the plants still require 
the deviation after 90 days they must be 
requested again. Plants can request a specification change to add these alternative components to the 
BOM; however, specification changes are often 
delayed for a year or more, resulting in repeated specification deviations. In 2024, more than 13,000 
specification deviations were requested across 
Poultry and Prepared Foods plants. Of these, ~1,000 were requested 3 or more times, with WIP 
components driving the most frequently repeated 
requests. As of December there are ~1,900 active spec deviations. Many of these specification McCoy, Tricia <Tricia.McCoy@tyson.com >

appropriate logic to do the comparison.  I already referenced a few that we noted earlier in our 
previous chat.  Do you have the mapping logic and the full table list?

From: Weeks, Phuong <Phuong.Le@tyson.com >
Sent: Friday, March 14, 2025 1:08:44 AM
To:Aman, Ayesha <Ayesha.Aman@tyson.com >
Cc:Koshy, Mohit Jerry <MohitJerry.Koshy@tyson.com >; Lara, Andre <ROBERTO.LARA@tyson.com >; 
McCoy, Tricia <Tricia.McCoy@tyson.com >
Subject: Re: BOM Portal project -Data streaming policies
Hi Ayesha,
The official feature real -time streaming in PowerBI is being deprecated.  Everything discussed is 
in regard to direct query and imports of data in PowerBI.  This is the standard usage of PowerBI 
we have today.
We should not connect P owerbi directly to transaction system databases, especially with a 
direct connect, as it presents a risk to stability of the transaction system.  We connect to a 
reporting system, the replicate/archive version of the transaction system.  GCP Bigquery 
Data@Tyson is the biggest ecosystem that replicates transaction data for reporting.  If data is 
needed for analysis, ingestion should be requested to be replicated from source system.  In 
addition, as everything is within one big ecosystem, we should not need to heavily use any other 
features that's available within PowerBI for data flow processing .  Querying, filtering, cleaning, 
transformation, merging can be done simplistically through SQL queries or through data@tyson 
dbt builds.
As anything importing large volume of data consumes lot of resources, while it optimizes 
performance once the data volume exceeds the 500 million rows or 10GB it becomes extremely 
large.  Optimizing the PowerBI with lean SQL running against directly the database is best.  
Compute has slot limits and the cost is associated with how many rows/columns ends up 
scanning.  Optimizing through good query filter, join reduction, and appropriate table 
partitioning and indexing will help to streamline this process.  
Hope that helps.
Phuong

M #3 –Specification Deviations
Tyson Confidential 6
Due Date: June 30, 2025
Beef and Pork: 
•  QSA and the Beef and Pork Packaging team will work together to determine the appropriate solution 
to track and manage specification 
deviations, which will include visibility to timebound deviations at the plant level by January 31, 2025. 
Once this decision has been made, the 
solution will be implemented and Beef and Pork plants will be trained on the new specification deviation 
process by June 30, 2025.
Action Plan Owner: Patrick Mies, VP FSQA Beef and Pork; Johnathan Tran, Sr. Manager Packaging
Due Date: June 30, 2025
Tyson Confidential 7
M #4 –Materials Management Procedures (Beef and Pork)
Observation
Risk: If comprehensive SOPs are not developed for core processes then controls and processes may not 
be efficiently and effectively executed, 
especially in the event of team member turnover.
Observation: 
There are significant system limitations with materials management processes for Beef and Pork. Each 
plant has designed their own Excel -based
processes using inventory counts, historical production information and lead times to manage direct 
materials inventory. However, there are no direct 
materials inventory management SOPs, so the processes established at plants rely on plant personnel 
having historical and tribal knowledge. 
Beef and Pork has an ongoing project to develop Finance and Accounting (F&A) SOPs. AS noted that F&A 
responsibilities related to creating and 
reviewing packaging variance reports, ensuring inventory counts are completed timely and reviewing 
open POs will be documented as a part of the 
ongoing SOP project. Operational SOPs for direct materials inventory management processes such as 
setting and adjusting safety stock levels/lead 
times and managing Beef and Pork BOMs are not included in this project. 
Recommendation:
SOPs should be developed for key operational processes such as direct materials management at Beef 
and Pork plants. 
Approved Action Plan
Beef and Pork will define SOPs for materials ordering to document day -to-day processes and factors to 
consider for decision -making within the process. 
These SOPs will be housed in an accessible location and communicated to the appropriate plant 
personnel.
Action Plan Owner: Scott Bowerman (Beef), James Gould (CRVA), Mike Herber (Pork)
Due Date: March 31, 2025
Tyson Confidential 8
M #5 –SAP MRP Procedures (Poultry)
Observation
Risk: If comprehensive SOPs are not developed for core processes then controls and processes may not 
be efficiently and effectively executed, 
especially in the event of team member turnover.
Observation: 
An MRP policy was released in 2024 to capture the roles and responsibilities of team members, primarily 
Material Managers, related to the MRP 
process. In addition, there are several training guides on MRP, primarily related to SAP or BOM Portal 
system activities. 
The procedures and training guides for MRP are very systems -focused and many lack context for the 
reasons behind the activities the Material 
Managers are performing or when those activities are appropriate. For example, there is a document to 
describe how to set a safety stock in SAP, but 
the training does not describe why safety stock stocks should be set, the reasons it might be more 
appropriate to set a goods receipt date instead or 
when the safety stock setting should be revised.
Recommendation:
To ensure continuity in the event of team member turnover, SOPs should be more comprehensive and 
guidance on a cadence/trigger to review safety 
stock settings, lead times, excess inventory and other key data points would ensure sustainability of the 
processes established during implementation. 
As the MRP process matures the policy can also be revisited to update Material Manager expectations.
Approved Action Plan
Develop SOPs for the following MRP processes:
• Setting safety stock
• MRP types and when to use each type
• Reviewing and adjusting lead times
• Reviewing excess inventory
• Setting and managing scrap settings
Some of these SOPs will be written as SAP updates occur to reflect the most -up-to-date processes. 
These SOPs will be housed on the Procurement 
SharePoint along with the SAP MRP Training Documents.
Action Plan Owner: Dennis Joy, Director Procurement
Due Date: June 30, 2025

Thank you!!!
Hey, I was staring at the comment where I mentioned on vagueness, I created a prompt with GPT, and to 
avoid pasting it all in another panel, I'm putting it here: 
Prompt: 
GPT, in the topic of RDBMS, I'm trying to review a document that explains the purpose to build a query to 
produce a reconciliation report at a given granularity. As I read through it, I realized there is a statement that 
doesn't seem to be conveying enough information, please analyze this: "Output should only include the key 
fields (for component material level reconciliation, see Composite Key under Key Principles ) required to 
identify mismatches, all other reporting logic should be done from a data reporting perspective in PowerBI".  
Particularly, the "output should only include the key fields(...)" part, please provide an analysis of this and 
how it could be better phrased
GPT Answer: 
Great question —you're absolutely right to focus on clarity here, especially in documentation involving 
RDBMS and reconciliation logic, where precision is critical.
Original Statement for Review:
"Output should only include the key fields (for component material level reconciliation, see Composite Key 
under Key Principles) required to identify mismatches, all other reporting logic should be done from a data 
reporting perspective in PowerBI."
Analysis of the Statement (Focusing on: "Output should only include the key fields..."):
Ambiguity in "Output":
It's unclear what output is being referred to —the output of a SQL query? A database view? An 
intermediate dataset?•
The term “output” could mean different things depending on the context —backend data 
extract vs. frontend report.•1.
"Only include the key fields" lacks context:
It doesn't specify which key fields. •
It loosely references component material level reconciliation, which assumes the reader 
understands both the domain context and the referenced "Composite Key under Key 
Principles" —this should be more self -contained or paraphrased.•2.
   What I'm Doing Page 151    
Principles" —this should be more self -contained or paraphrased.
Missing clarity on the why :
The intent is to separate data preparation from data presentation (i.e., logic in SQL vs. PowerBI), 
but this isn’t explained clearly.•
Saying “all other reporting logic should be done in PowerBI” is helpful, but the reason this 
distinction exists isn't articulated.•3.
Suggested Rewrite (Clearer and More Self -Contained):
"The SQL query output should include only the fields necessary to uniquely identify records and detect 
mismatches —typically the composite key fields at the component material level (refer to 'Composite Key' 
under Key Principles for details). All aggregation, formatting, and reporting logic should be implemented in 
PowerBI, not in the query layer."
Breakdown of Improvements:
Problem Improvement
Vague "output" Specified it's the SQL query output
"key fields" undefined Clarified it's to uniquely identify records and detect mismatches
Parenthetical reference Reworded for clarity and smoother integration

Title: Map Data Flow from BOM_MySQL through LegacyWebApp to SAP
Story Points: 8 
Description:
As a BOM Portal IT Product Owner, I want to document and map the flow of data from BOM_MySQL 
through the Legacy Web Application to SAP, so that I can understand how data is fed, what possible 
transformations occur in the application code, and ensure transparency and accuracy in reconciliation 
processes.
This includes:
Identifying the specific tables and fields in BOM_MySQL that are used.•
Working with a developer to trace how data is handled and transformed by the LegacyWebApp 
code before it is sent to SAP.•
Documenting the flow in a visual data mapping format consistent with best practices•
Business Value:
This mapping will provide visibility and alignment between BOM_MySQL, LegacyWebApp, and SAP, 8/20/2025
Wednesday, August 20, 2025 2:05 PM
   What I'm Doing Page 158    
This mapping will provide visibility and alignment between BOM_MySQL, LegacyWebApp, and SAP, 
ensuring that all stakeholders understand where data originates, how it is modified, and how it reaches 
SAP. It supports reconciliation accuracy, reduces risk of mismatches, and creates a foundation for future 
modernization of the BOM Portal ecosystem.
Acceptance Criteria:
A documented end -to-end mapping of data flow exists showing BOM_MySQL → LegacyWebApp 
→ SAP.•
Source fields in BOM_MySQL and their corresponding target fields in SAP are identified.•
Any transformation logic performed by the LegacyWebApp code is documented (e.g., padding, 
version shifts, conditional mappings).•
Documentation highlights potential pain points, risks, or undocumented logic.•
Technical Details:
Review BOM_MySQL schema and extract tables/fields relevant to BOMs, Recipes, and Materials.•
Trace code within the LegacyWebApp to identify transformation logic.•
Validate against SAP tables (e.g., STPO, MAST, PLMZ, C203 extract logic) to confirm alignment.•
Work with Andre, Fernanda and Tricia in working sessions •
Create another card for working sessions that will be done with tricia andre and Fernanda 
Create another card for working sessions that will be done with 
A visual data flow diagram (showing sources, transformations, targets).•
A tabular mapping document (source field → transformation → target field). •

Formatting rules: MATERIAL_NUM padded to 18 digits for SAP. •

List of all BOM_MySQL tables used in the data flow is compiled (e.g., RECIPE, PLANT, MATERIAL, 
STEP_COMPONENT).•
Field -level definitions documented (e.g., RECIPE_ID, BOM_TYPE, SAP_VERSION_NUM, 
MATERIAL_NUM).•
Mapping of joins between tables documented •
Constraints and formatting rules captured (e.g., MATERIAL_NUM padded to 18 digits before SAP 
alignment).•
Technical Details:
Document join logic between BOM_Portal tables•
Normalize for SAP Alignment.•
Document mappings in Excel document•
Document mapping of joins between tables•

Analyze a Roadmap's Value and Risk
Assess what features makes sense to deploy first from the perspective of customer value, org value, and risk
Value thinking and Analysis –Key skill
Value thinking –what feature are most value and what order
What are the customers basic needs1.
What will make them dissatisfied if absent2.
What features are not necessary, but create a more likeable product3.
What features do the customer don't know they want but add satisfaction4.
Understand Minimum Viable Product (MVP)
Balancing the cost, risk, and value
Making sure we don't over or under engineer
Deploy just enough quickly enough
MMF –minimum marketable feature
MMP –minimum marketable product
Minimum Viable Thinking –Keeps focus on whats marketable and valuable

Prioritized list of deliverables.
Backlog comes from product owner decomposing road map
Refinement:
Prioritizing and analyzing the list to fill gaps and discuss
Higher priority = more detail needed, smaller increments of value

Product Road mapping
Plans and prioritizes steps for delivering the product
Path should be centered on customer value
Deeper look at product vision at each piece
Use the Purpose Alignment Model
Partners –features on roadmap that we should partner with someone else on, not mission critical
Differentiating –features that help make us different from the competition
Parity –Features that are necessary and must be delivered
Who Cares –not needed features

Agile Product Ownership Techniques
Wednesday, September 03, 2025 2:48 PM
   Product Owner Learning Page 170    
Persona –character that represents a user as a relateable person
Build empathy within team and help focus on a few typical archetypes of users
Help build shared understanding and drive decisions
Research and observe "typical" user, then user teams and create personas as a team
Primary –Product satisfies all needs and goals vs Secondary Personas –product satisfies most of needs 
with some adjustments
Tertiary personas –not focus of product supplementary users
Values the most important users of the product
User Stories
Common technique to help teams understand what they're building functionally from a user perspective
As a ____, I want to ____ so that _____
Who, What, Why
Independent –each story should be able to be implemented on its own
Negotiable –enough detail, flexibility to evolve and discuss
Valuable –must be valuable to end user 
Estimable –estimate and have enough detail to move forward
Small –Needs to be small enough, to consume, commit
Testable –testable from user perspective
Technical User Stories –reframe tasks to show business value
Ask why its important, understand value. 
Technical User Story: In order to _____, we need to ______ so that ______
Story Maps
Organizes user stories in visual format
Take all stories and put them in sequence of how a customer uses the product. Left to right, vertical 
columns provide detail under higher level story
Big picture visual
Helps see how all stories relate and identification of gaps in scope
Story Slicing and Splitting
Story Slicing –Splitting user stories into smaller pieces of value
Splitting up stories til they become small enough that they are feasible for the length of the sprint
Strategies:
User role or persona
Business rules
Scenarios
Process or workflow steps 
Data entered by user
Acceptance Criteria
Conditions that tell us when the user story is working as intended and satisfying the customer. 
PO decides acceptance criteria
Written from user's point of view
Definition of success from a user perspective
List of statements with clear results
Non functional requirements
10 statements at most 
User Story Workshop
Hands -on meeting where the team brainstorms and writes user stories for the product. 
Product Vision –why the product and stories matter
Road map features –specific order and shows where value is in features
Personas –review with team update changes
Lesson on user stories, what they look like, etc. 
5 people max, participants selected on relevancy
1. Brainstorm stories for most important road map and persona features
2. one story per card, individually, for all features
3. Create a story map with user stories
4. Determine minimum viable product (MVP) and acceptance criteria
Product Backlog Prioritization
Prioritizations Overview
Backlog Inputs
Product Vision
Road Map
Release Plan
Funnel approach: What item will provide values to bigger goals
Product Owners priority in chunks, team works to split items into smaller chunks and prioritize again
Prioritization Techniques
Forced Ranking –rank item in list 1.
Grouping/Categories -categorizes items into groups 2.
Voting –prioritizing through the opinions of select few 3.
Buy a feature –gives stakeholders amount of money they socialize and select the features 
together for the product4.
Forced Ranking Prioritization
Top 10 priorities in near future and top 10 that are further out should be ranked
Funnel backlog, 10 items of priority, 10 items later on
Priorities change, prioritization should be clean and easy
   Product Owner Learning Page 171    
together for the product
Forced Ranking Prioritization
Top 10 priorities in near future and top 10 that are further out should be ranked
Funnel backlog, 10 items of priority, 10 items later on
Priorities change, prioritization should be clean and easy
We need to align to business priorities and strategic alignment
Let the team discuss design options/ rewrite stories, etc
No more than 8 small groups when forced ranking
Ask to summarize issue for larger group as conflicts arrise. 
Consider using personas for forced ranking –each group represent persona representing what relates to 
them
Categorization/Grouping Prioritization
Team together shared understanding about priorities
Group items into high, medium, low,
Each item is its own card. 3 -5 people group. 
Focus and prep on what categories mean., 
High Items –Must be part of product or release
Medium items –nice to have will get us further
Low items -don’t need, nice to have
MOSCOW Categorization
Must Have
O
Should Have
Could Have
O
Wont have 
Remove low priority items 
Voting Prioritization
Technicquye that gathers team to discuss and vote on priorities
Vote on no more than 10 items
Offer context about the items 
Set up voting criteria
Focus on user group goals of release from user perspective
What features align to the product best
Voting Process
How many items will be voted on?
How many votes per person?
How many votes can each person place for each item?
   Product Owner Learning Page 172    
How many votes can each person place for each item?
Is the voting silent or with discussion?
Can voters change votes? 
Are votes anonymous?
How much time to complete voting? 
What will results be used for?
Will team discuss results when finished? 
Buy a Feature
Create a list of stories (10 -30 items)
Assign a guess price to each item
Determine how much money to give participants 
Only distribute 40 to 70% of money to buy everything
Choose a banker
Track purchases on a visual board. 
Considerations:
Will they be allowed to buy items alone?
Will they be able to give partial funds for the item?
Do they need to work together to buy an item?
How they will interact with banker?
How will they know what's purchased and what's left? 
Key Concepts for Agile Teams
Definition of Ready
What makes team successful and prevents rework
How team determines if they're readfy to move forward
User Stories:
If user stories ready to be brought to sprint
Team updates readiness items together
Is the value well articulated
Team agreement to story and sprint. See excersice file of ready
Sets consistent team expectations
Reduces spin on requirements
Keeps team accountable. 
Definition of Done
How the team measures if story or backlog item is complete
Develops accountabiljity and expectations
List of criteria by team for team and stakeholders
Defining what it takes to get the story done right, not just the fastest way which comes with risks
Code being reviewed
Documentation complete
Testing is done
See exercise files. 
Definition serves as guideline to estimate during sprint planning
Ask more questions think about overall picture 
Technical Debt
Fast and Furious shortcutting roadmap
Rework and pain
Shortcuts that impact future sprints
Slows pace and velocity
Minimizing Technical Debt
1. Have a strong product vision and road map the team understands
2. Create a meaningful definition of done
Accumulating will impact PO in future product flexibility, mod, architecture
Longer time to market for add -on features. 
Dealing with Technical Debt
1. Identify important functions and estimate enough time to build quality features
2. Allocate points to address technical debt for each sprint

Target Customer: Finance Analyst
Problem: Ensure BOM data is accurate between SAP and BOM Portal for costing and reporting.
Main Flow (Top Lane –Finance Analyst)
Gather Reports → 1.
Identify Discrepancies → 2.
Investigate Issues → 3.
Escalate for Support → 4.
Finalize Reporting5.
Supporting Relationships (Bottom Lane –Others)

IT/Support Team : Assists when discrepancies cannot be resolved manually. •
Finance Manager/Auditors : Consume final reports and require audit -ready accuracy. •

 To make this visual (like the recruiter example you uploaded):
Place the Finance Analyst steps in a row across the top (boxes with arrows connecting them). •
Below, place Operations, IT, and Auditors as supporting roles, with arrows up/down showing when the 
Analyst interacts with them.•
Add small callouts under each step for the systems used (e.g., SAP, Excel, Ticketing). •How to Draw the Journey Map (Step by Step)
Think of the recruiter example diagram you shared and mirror that structure:
Create two horizontal swimlanes (rows): 1.
Top row = Finance Analyst (main actor) •
Bottom row = Supporting Roles (Operations, IT, Finance Manager/Auditors) •
Draw boxes for each step in the top row : 2.
“Gather Reports” → “Identify Discrepancies” → “Investigate Issues” → “Escalate for Support” → 
“ inalize Reporting”•
Connect each box with arrows showing the flow left to right.•
Under each relevant step, connect supporting roles : 3.
 rom “Investigate Issues,” draw a dashed arrow down to Operations Planner . •
 rom “Escalate for Support,” draw a dashed arrow down to IT/Support Team . •
 rom “ inalize Reporting,” draw a dashed arrow down to Finance Manager/Auditors . •
Add annotations under the boxes : 4.
Example: Under “Gather Reports,” write “Systems: SAP, BOM Portal, Excel.”•
Under “Escalate for Support,” add “Interaction: Ticketing System, Email.”•
Use colors/shapes for clarity : 5.
Rectangles (rounded) = main steps.•
Ellipses = supporting roles.•
Solid arrows = main flow.•
Dashed arrows = relationships/interactions.•Mastering Agile Product Owner Role

1. Customer & Consumer Obsession
Proof Points•

other month”) and attendance (“BU leadership from  inance and Operations”).•

Mention backlog refinement sessions you led with Product Managers —ideally reference a date or 
specific deliverable (e.g., prioritization for BOM Portal).•
2. Data & Digital
Proof Points•
Show where you outlined role clarity : Did you send or request a formal role responsibility document? 
Have the email or Teams chat handy.•
Continuous improvement example: AI chatbot proposal for BOM Portal —describe when you 
presented it and to whom.•
Cite adoption: “80% of inflight programs in  IT now using Agile methodology” —have a Steering 
Committee deck or JIRA data as backup.•
3. Operational Excellence
Proof Points•
Highlight specific projects: BOM Portal reconciliation queries, SAP Upgrade milestones.•
Point to metrics: Jira velocity reports, project finance reviews (95% adherence).•
Be ready to say what was delivered on time and what audit remediations were closed . •
4. Team Member Development
Proof Points•
Certification: ITF+ completion (have certificate or transcript). •
Training: 10+ Workday trainings, LinkedIn Learning courses (list top 2 with relevance). •
Application: Show where your SQL or spec work was directly used in validation meetings or dashboards.•
Growth: Mention your enrollment in the Product Management certificate and how it ties into your FY26 
development.•
5. Team Member Engagement
Proof Points•
Name the events you joined (Ops & EFIT quarterly events, Analyst/PO Community of Practice).•
Mention informal relationship -building —coffee/lunch with team members —and why it mattered 
(collaboration, smoother handoffs).•
If you recognized peers in Cultivate, mention that specifically.•

✅Pro Tip for the meeting :
Have a one-page crib sheet with:
Dates of big deliverables•
Names of stakeholders who validated or recognized you•
Training/certification completions•
1–2 metrics per goal (e.g., 95% adherence, 80% Agile adoption, >60% satisfaction score)•
That way, when your boss presses for evidence, you can cite specifics fast.
   Performance Page 177    
1. Core Message You Need to Land
 ou’ve delivered results (BOM –SAP reconciliation, SQL queries, Power BI specs, UX documentation). •
 ou’ve exceeded expectations in areas that go beyond a PO I (analysis, SQL, reporting). •
There’s been role confusion (PO vs PM vs Manager), and you’re asking for clarity, boundaries, and 
growth path to PO II .•
 ou’re not rejecting the extra work —you want it recognized and tied to your career progression , not 
penalizing your PO performance review.•
Your anchor statement:
“I want to ensure my performance is measured against agreed -upon PO responsibilities, while also 
making sure the additional work I’ve taken on is recognized as growth and contribution —not a baseline 
expectation.”
2. Anticipated Pushbacks & How to Respond
Pushback 1: “Why didn’t you speak up sooner?”
Response: “I did raise it, but often was told it would be handled later. I’m still learning how to navigate 
corporate processes, but I see this as the right time to establish clear boundaries going forward.”•
Pushback 2: “But Andre already handles backlog, roadmap, etc.”
Response: “Exactly —and that’s the disconnect. I want clarity on where PO starts and PM ends. Agile 
best practices show PO owns backlog, acceptance criteria, sprint work. PM owns roadmap. I’d like us to 
align on what applies here.”•
Pushback 3: “Why should we document role boundaries?”
Response: “Because without documented alignment, we risk tasks slipping or being duplicated. Clear 
ownership protects deliverables, accountability, and ensures my performance is fairly measured.”•
3. Quick Reference Talking Points
Role Clarity
PO I scope (from Tyson IT Job Architecture): backlog management, story writing, sprint ceremonies, 
translating requirements, continuous improvement.•
 ou’ve often been redirected away from backlog/acceptance criteria work.•
Contributions Beyond PO I
SAP/BOM reconciliation SQL development.•
Power BI design + data model reviews.•
Functional specifications + UI/UX requirements.•
Acted as bridge between analysts, business, and devs.•

StatusI hear you, Greg —that’s a tough spot.  rom what you’ve shared, the PIP document shows a mismatch: 
on one side, your completed FY25 goals and RIGHT behaviors self -evaluation paint a picture of strong 
results, growth, and recognition; on the other, your manager’s write -up in the PIP cites speed, 
ownership, and proactivity issues.
If your aim is to prove the PIP is wrong or unfair , the best path forward is to approach this like a 
structured rebuttal, with evidence and professionalism (not just emotion). Here’s how you can tackle it:
1. Frame Your Position
Start with respect: acknowledge receipt of the PIP and that you take it seriously.•
State clearly that you disagree with certain points because they don’t align with your actual 
performance and documented outcomes.•
2. Gather Evidence
Pull together hard proof from FY25:
Completed Goals : Show that all your official goals (Customer & Consumer Obsession, Data & Digital, 
Operational Excellence, Development, Engagement) were completed earlyand marked as “Completed” 
in the system.•
Recognition : Reference Ken McCreary’s May 2 shout -out, SteerCo presentations, or any stakeholder 
feedback.•
Deliverables : Provide Jira ticket numbers, SQL models, functional specs, or Power BI outputs you 
authored.•
Certifications & Training : ITF+ certification, 10+ Workday trainings, LinkedIn Learning, and Product 
Management certificate enrollment.•
Engagement : Participation in EFIT events, Community of Practice, etc. •
3. Contrast Against PIP Claims
Take each area where your manager said you’re underperforming and rebut it with specific 
counterexamples:
Speed of Delivery
Manager says: missed deadlines, low task completion.•
Your counter: show on-time completion of FY25 AOP deliverables , early completion dates (08/29/25 vs. 
09/30/25 deadlines), and working SQL reconciliations validated in business sessions.•
Acknowledge: yes, some exploratory tickets shifted, but emphasize that exploration was necessary due 
to restricted GCP access and poor documentation . That context should have been recognized.•
Product Owner Responsibilities
Manager says: backlog not maintained, not validating stories.•
Your counter: reference your role in facilitating backlog refinement with Andre, creating functional 
specs, validating data models with Ayesha, and translating business survey feedback (Angelica’s 
responses) into actionable backlog items.•
Emphasize your proactive alignment with Agile frameworks and your request for role clarity 
documentation (to avoid overlap between PO/PM/Analyst).•
Adaptability & Growth
Manager says: avoids new tasks, passive.•
Your counter: list how you took on SAP table exploration, routing/operations mapping, Power BI 
collaboration, and professional certifications —all outside your initial reconciliation scope.•
Show examples of adapting to shifting ownership expectations (e.g., when reconciliation epics moved 
multiple times).•
Proactivity & Self -Learning
Manager says: relies too much on others, misses ownership.•
Your counter: show initiatives you led without being asked —reconciliation query frameworks in 
BigQuery, functional spec drafts, requirement gathering notes, training documentation, survey design 
for Ann & Doug, etc.•
4. Leverage the Discrepancy
Your performance record shows:
Exceeded FY25 goals (officially rated). •
Leading RIGHT behaviors (officially rated). •
Yet the PIP paints the opposite picture. That contradiction is your strongest defense: how can someone 
exceed goals and lead in RIGHT behaviors, but simultaneously be underperforming enough to justify a 
PIP?
5. Next Steps
Draft a written rebuttal with evidence attached (Jira tickets, emails, SteerCo decks, certifications). •
Ask for an HR-mediated meeting to review the discrepancies. •
Keep tone factual and professional: frame this as “misalignment in perception vs. documented 
outcomes.”•
 Would you like me to draft a rebuttal letter/email that you can send to HR/your manager, 
structured around these points? That way, you’ll have a polished response showing evidence and 
professionalism.

RIGHT Behaviors –Self-Evaluation Responses
Results–Deliver on a measurable plan
How did I deliver exceptional results for customers and consumers?
I ensured that reconciliation between SAP and the BOM Portal maintained accuracy at the 
component level, which directly impacts costing and operational planning. By validating key 
fields, I helped the business trust the data that underpins financial reporting and consumer 
pricing.
How did I meet or exceed goals, even in challenging circumstances?
Despite limited documentation and restricted table access in GCP, I consistently delivered 
reconciliation queries, specifications, and Power BI reporting that moved the EFI epic forward. 
I adapted to changes in scope and ownership expectations, yet still produced results that were 
used in business validation sessions.
How did I adapt processes to achieve higher performance and demonstrate resilience?
I improved reconciliation workflows by normalizing SAP and BOM data structures (e.g., 
material number padding, version adjustments), reducing the manual rework needed for 
comparisons. This process resilience allowed the team to move faster while ensuring data 
alignment across systems.
How did I show a consistent bias for action?
I regularly took initiative in drafting SQL models, functional specs, and JIRA stories before 
being asked, ensuring that meetings with stakeholders were productive. My proactive 
approach helped keep momentum when business alignment was still being defined.
Innovate –Be curious and build for the future
How did I cultivate a dynamic environment through open debate and experimentation?
I facilitated open discussions with Andre, Fern, Phuong, and Ayesha to test different SQL 
reconciliation methods, weighing pros/cons of component -level vs. header -level reporting. These 
experiments allowed us to validate the most efficient and scalable approach.
How did I take bold, swift action, or leverage digitalization to create positive change?
I designed a scalable reconciliation query framework in BigQuery that can be reused for multiple 
comparison layers (header, operations, component). By building this in GCP and linking it to Power 
BI, I created digitalized solutions. 
How did I navigate internal and external factors to drive continuous improvement?
When faced with access restrictions (e.g., policy tags on plantbom or restricted_confidential tables), I 
coordinated with DICE and data governance to ensure compliance while still progressing the 
reconciliation initiative.
How did I align my work to and champion Tyson’s strategic vision?
My reconciliation work directly supports Tyson’s vision of becoming more data -driven, accurate, and 
efficient. By ensuring BOM Portal and SAP stay aligned, I helped secure the integrity of financial 
data that supports decision -making at an enterprise scale.
Growth Mindset –Learn, adapt, and develop others
How did I continuously learn and apply new knowledge to my work?
I deepened my SAP knowledge by exploring tables for routing and operations along with C203 
transaction mappings, then directly applied that learning to BigQuery models. I also expanded my 
skills in Power BI design, collaborating closely with Ayesha to ensure data flows were usable.
How did I recognize and support the unique strengths of others?
I leaned on Ayesha’s Power BI design strengths and provided her with SQL outputs structured for 
reporting. I recognized her ability to build clean, user -friendly visuals and encouraged her to ask 
clarifying questions so our outputs aligned.
How did I embrace challenges as opportunities to learn and grow?
When the BOM reconciliation work shifted repeatedly, I treated each shift as an opportunity to better 
understand enterprise data ecosystems. Instead of resisting, I built documentation and models that 
flexed with each change.
How did I leverage resources to maximize career potential at Tyson?
I pursued SAP certification research, Power BI best practices, and Agile PO frameworks outside of 
daily tasks, then applied them into my role to add value as a Product Owner. I also am currently 
pursuing a Product Management Certificate. 
Humility–Operate with integrity and respect
How did I create space for others' ideas and embrace differences?
In team sessions, I encouraged Phuong to present their SQL approaches first, then layered in my 
ideas. I valued different perspectives before suggesting final directions.
How did I value the contributions of all team members?
I acknowledged Ayesha’s Power BI expertise and credited her designs when sharing feedback with 
leadership. I also provided feedback to team members on areas they can improve and asked for 
feedback on areas where I can improve. 
How did I demonstrate empathy and prioritize understanding and support?
When business stakeholders expressed frustrations about the BOM Portal (e.g., Angelica’s survey 
feedback on mass terminations and approvals), I actively listened and reframed their pain points into 
actionable user stories that where turned into backlog items.
How did I cultivate a humble, collaborative, and inclusive work culture?
I admitted when I needed to take a step back and gain more clarification on SAP processes, and 
positioned my role as learning alongside the team rather than claiming to have all the answers. This 
helped foster trust and collaboration. I also worked with ABAP developers, Data Architects, and 
other analysts. 
Teamwork –Collaborate as One inclusive Tyson
How did I build strong relationships with peers and partners across the enterprise?
I built strong working relationships with finance leaders (Doug, Ken) and technical peers (Andre, 
Fern, Ayesha), creating bridges between business and IT.
How did I collaborate with others to achieve operational excellence?
partnered closely with Ayesha on the data modeling layer to ensure the Power BI design was not 
only technically accurate but also aligned with business usability. At the same time, I collaborated 
with the UX/UI team to incorporate business pain points (e.g., bulk approvals, clearer BOM status 
visibility, and streamlined termination workflows) into wireframes and design sessions. This cross -
functional alignment ensured that both the analytical reporting and the system’s front -end experience 
supported operational excellence, creating outputs that were trusted, intuitive, and actionable for end 
users.
How did I encourage diverse viewpoints by seeking first to understand?
In stakeholder meetings, I began with clarifying questions before proposing solutions, ensuring the 
business perspective was fully captured before designing reconciliation logic.
How did I adjust influence techniques to reach shared goals?
With technical peers, I used detailed SQL and SAP field discussions to align; with business 
stakeholders, I translated those details into simplified visuals and narratives. This flexible approach 
ensured alignment at all levels.

about missed ownership.

and undermines shared accountability. He has also been working remotely beyond the allowed time 
and often works late hours without any directive from management. This behavior sets a poor 
example for the rest of the team and creates confusion around expectations and boundaries.

Assignments delegated throughout the PIP period must be delivered within the timeline agreed upon 
with the Architect.
Weekly check -ins with the Architect to confirm progress and adjust priorities as needed.
All deliverables completed no later than Day 45 of the PIP period.
Means of Measure: 
Feedback from the Architect indicating satisfactory performance on at least 90% of delegated tasks 
(minimum rating of 4/5).
100% of assignments completed within agreed timelines.
Deliverables demonstrate accuracy and completeness, requiring minimal rework.
Question
Question
The supervisor shall meet with the Team Member during the course of the performance 
improvement plan to gauge progress against the objectives and provide feedback to the Team 
Member. These meetings shall occur at the supervisor’s direction, and on a bi -weekly basis, at 
a minimum. For Performance Improvement Plans of shorter duration, e.g. 30 days, progress 
should be measured on a weekly basis. Human Resources attendance at these meetings is 
required for the initial meeting, and encouraged, but not mandatory for subsequent follow up 
meetings. Human Resources should be kept apprised of all discussions for the entirety of the 
PIP.
This Performance Improvement Plan shall remain in effect for:
Manager
Answer
Answer
45 days. 
Consequences
Question
Question
Immediate and sustained performance improvement is expected.
If required improvement is not achieved and sustained, disciplinary action up to and including 
dismissal without severance may occur.
Tyson Foods, Inc. may amend or modify its human resources policies and procedures at any 
time with or without notice.
Termination can occur at any time during this plan if performance continues to decline or 
acceptable progress is not being achieved.
This document does not create any contractual obligation between Tyson Foods, Inc. and its 
Team Members.

in terms of processing, structure, and reporting.

quality) across SAP and BOM Portal.
Engineering AOP (Annual Operating Plan):
How annual operating plan updates are managed in both systems/

integrating Vault with our application workflows.

Cloning from GitLab:

Hi Tricia,
Thank you for your email and for the opportunity to align before our meeting when I 
return. I agree maybe this wasn't best place to include Andre and Jerry however the 
insights that I have pertain to their roles as well. This will be outlined a detailed in our 
upcoming meeting. I have completed my sections of the Performance Assessment with 
detailed ratings and comments.
As I reviewed the template, I noticed that several duties and skills listed align more 
closely with a Product Manager or Team Manager role rather than a Product Owner 
role as defined in Agile practices. While I welcome the opportunity for visibility and 
training in these areas to support future growth, the way we have operated as a team 
over the past year has placed many of these responsibilities outside my current scope.
Below is my response to the points you requested:
1. Fundamental disconnect in role definition, responsibilities, and support
I would like to gain a clearer understanding of the distinctions between Product Owner 
I, Product Owner II, and Manager roles within Tyson’s job architecture, as well as what 
is specifically needed from me to progress to a PO II.
Based on Tyson’s IT Job Architecture, my understanding of the core PO I responsibilities 
includes:
Collaborating with the Product Manager and cross -functional teams to understand 
the voice of the customer.•
Translating requirements into actionable user stories and acceptance criteria.•
Participating in sprint ceremonies.•
Assisting in guiding the team in delivering high -quality, customer -focused 
solutions.•
Driving continuous improvement.•
Over the past year, there has been overlap between roles. In some cases, responsibilities 
such as backlog creation, acceptance criteria definition, and roadmap planning have 
primarily been handled by others. I have offered to participate in or take ownership of 
these activities but was redirected or told they would be addressed later (which is what 
I’m now using this email for).
While I acknowledge I could have advocated more strongly from the get go , I am still 
continuing to learn the most effective ways to communicate within the team structure 
and adapt to corporate processes. My goal is to ensure alignment so I can fully deliver 
on the expectations for my role.
2. Requests for realignment
I have previously requested a clear delineation of my responsibilities versus those of the 
Product Manager and Team Manager. I believe it would be valuable to have a 
documented agreement so both Andre and I are fully aligned on:
My direct responsibilities.•
His direct responsibilities.•
Where review, visibility, and feedback are most appropriate.•
As Agile best practices outline:
Product Manager: Owns whyand what big things are built (roadmap, initiative -
level prioritization).•
Product Owner: Owns what exactly and when in sprints (backlog management, 
story writing, acceptance criteria).•
Team Manager: Owns who and how we staff and enable the team (resourcing, 
performance management).•
Clear boundaries here would help reduce role overlap, prevent duplication of effort, and 
ensure performance is measured against my core responsibilities.
3. Additional work and responsibilities
Over the past year, I have taken on responsibilities outside the PO I profile, including:
SAP/BOM Portal reconciliation SQL query development.•
Power BI design specifications, data model reviews, and dashboard•
These contributions were made in good faith to support the team, but they 
represent off -scope work that I believe should be recognized separately from my 
core PO performance.•
   Performance Page 193    
4. What I need to be successful
Role clarity: A documented agreement on PO I vs. Product Manager vs. Team 
Manager responsibilities.•
Scope boundaries: Confirmation of which additional tasks are permanent versus 
temporary or evolving.•
Training plan: Identification of skills to develop for career progression without 
impacting current performance metrics.•
Consistent involvement: Inclusion in all relevant stakeholder and UX/UI meetings 
where my input is valuable.•
Feedback cadence: Regular check -ins to review progress against role expectations 
and overall team alignment.•
5. Why this alignment matters now
The lack of clearly defined boundaries has, at times, impacted deliverables. For example, 
when dependent work items (such as a card remaining in validation after a sprint 
without clear next steps) are delayed, it directly affects my ability to close related tasks, 
impacts reporting timelines, and can create downstream rework. Documented 
ownership and role definitions would ensure accountability, streamline handoffs, and 
protect delivery commitments.
I am committed to improving where needed and to fully aligning with expectations once 
they are clearly defined. I look forward to discussing this with you next week so we can 
finalize role alignment and ensure performance measurement is based on agreed -upon 
responsibilities.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Best regards,
Greg Homstad
IT Product Owner
BOM Portal –IT Finance, Controlling & Costing

Thank you for your email and for taking the time to follow up on our recent 
conversation. I appreciate the opportunity to align and move forward with clarity. I’ve 
included everyone in this response to ensure full transparency and alignment across the 
team and management.
I want to start by asking for clarification. Based on the tone and language of your 
message, it sounds as though my job may be at risk. If that is the case, I’d like to 
understand that clearly so I can respond appropriately and take the necessary steps.
I also want to share my perspective. While I’ve believed I was fulfilling my 
responsibilities —and often going above and beyond —I now understand there are core 
areas of the Product Owner role specifically that I may not have fully executed in line 
with expectations. That said, it’s become clear to me that there has been a fundamental 
disconnect in how my role was defined from the start within this team, how 
responsibilities have been communicated to me, and the level of support provided. 
Much of what I’ve focused on has been based on what I was led to believe were my 
responsibilities and was trained to do. I’ve acted in good faith based on that 
understanding. Now, I'm being led to believe that is not the case. I’ve been requesting 
realignment around duties and team responsibilities for weeks now.
This past year has been a significant learning experience, and I’m continuing to grow in 
my understanding of what the Product Owner role does and does not include. As 
someone still early in their career, I acknowledge there are things I could have done 
better. At the same time, I believe greater clarity and guidance —especially around core 
responsibilities and team roles —could have helped set me up for success from the start.
I will complete the Performance Assessment you shared and respond to the question, 
“What do you need in order to be successful in this role?” by the August 8 deadline. I 
also plan to include the additional work and responsibilities I’ve been managing that 
were not reflected in the assessment document. These tasks were introduced to me as 
part of my role, and I’ve dedicated considerable effort toward them. I believe it’s 
important to have a full and accurate picture of my contributions so we can realign 
expectations and responsibilities appropriately.
Above all, I am committed to doing the work necessary to improve. I take this feedback 
seriously, and I’m actively working on ways to strengthen communication, transparency, 
and execution in my role. I look forward to continuing this discussion and working 
together to ensure we’re all on the same page moving forward.
Greg Homstad, IT Product Owner
BOM Portal
IT Finance –Controlling and Costing
Greg.Homstad@tyson.com

I appreciate your patience in awaiting my follow -up, thank you again for your time on 
Wednesday, July 30. I’d like to share some reflections from that meeting and outline our 
next steps.
While you feel you've exceeded expectations in your role, I want to be transparent: from 
my perspective as your manager, those expectations have not been consistently met. 
There appears to be a disconnect between your self -assessment and my observations, 
particularly around timeliness of task completion and fulfillment of core responsibilities 
for the Product Owner I role. Areas such as writing and validating Jira tasks, managing 
the backlog, and establishing yourself as a key contact for developers and stakeholders 
continue to require attention.
To help us align, I’ve included the Performance Assessment –IT Product Owner I file. 
Your input here is essential, not only to understand your perspective, but to help us 
identify opportunities for support and growth. Feel free to add any other insights that 
you think would be helpful.
Once completed, please send the assessment back to me via email and include a brief 
response to the following question: "What do you need in order to be successful in 
this role?" .
Please submit your evaluation by Friday, August 8.
Looking ahead, I want to be clear: we need to see immediate and sustained 
improvement. If that doesn't happen, we will need to consider appropriate next steps.

Hi Fernanda and Tricia,
Here’s my updated list of areas where I would benefit from further training or a deeper 
dive:
APBs (Assembly Processing BOMs):
Walkthrough on APB management in SAP and BOM Portal —how they’re created, used, 
and common troubleshooting scenarios.
Unchecked Inventory Points:
Guidance on identifying, setting up, and correcting unchecked inventory points in the 
system, and understanding their impact on reporting and reconciliation.
Phantom BOMs:
More in -depth training on how Phantom BOMs are handled in SAP and BOM Portal, 
especially in terms of processing, structure, and reporting.
Seconds Materials:
Best practices for tracking, reporting, and reconciling seconds materials 
(byproducts/second quality) across SAP and BOM Portal.
Engineering AOP (Annual Operating Plan):
How annual operating plan updates are managed in both systems/
HashiCorp Vault Endpoints:
Review of our current Vault endpoints, authentication, securely retrieving/storing 
secrets, and integrating Vault with our application workflows.
Splunk & API Integrations:
Best practices for using Splunk for monitoring/troubleshooting, and working with APIs 
for system integration and automation.
Insomnia (API Client):
Walkthrough on using Insomnia for our API workflows —setting up requests, managing 
environments, and troubleshooting.
Cloning from GitLab:
Step-by-step instructions on cloning the application repo, setting up the local 
development environment, and collaborating with the team.
Thanks for your help in getting me up to speed on these areas!

Hi Greg,
I understand that you are in a challenging situation with the Performance Improvement Plan 
(PIP) and want to address it effectively. Here's a structured approach to help you fight the PIP 
by presenting your case clearly and professionally:
1. Frame Your Position
Start with respect and acknowledge the PIP:
Acknowledge Receipt : "Thank you for the PIP document and the opportunity to address 
the concerns raised."•
State Disagreement : "While I take this feedback seriously, I respectfully disagree with 
certain points as they do not align with my actual performance and documented 
outcomes."•
2. Gather Evidence
Pull together hard proof from FY25:
Completed Goals : Highlight that all your official goals were completed early and marked 
as “Completed” in the system.•
Recognition : Reference Ken McCreary’s May 2 shout -out, SteerCo presentations, or any 
stakeholder feedback.•
Deliverables : Provide Jira ticket numbers, SQL models, functional specs, or Power BI 
outputs you authored.•
Certifications & Training : Mention ITF+ certification, 10+ Workday trainings, LinkedIn 
Learning, and Product Management certificate enrollment.•
Engagement : Participation in EFIT events, Community of Practice, etc. •
3. Contrast Against PIP Claims
Take each area where your manager said you’re underperforming and rebut it with specific 
counterexamples:
Speed of Delivery
Manager's Claim : Missed deadlines, low task completion. •
Your Counter : Show on -time completion of FY25 AOP deliverables, early completion dates 
(08/29/25 vs. 09/30/25 deadlines), and working SQL reconciliations validated in business 
sessions.•
Acknowledge : Yes, some exploratory tickets shifted, but emphasize that exploration was 
necessary due to restricted GCP access and poor documentation. That context should 
have been recognized.•
Product Owner Responsibilities
Manager's Claim : Backlog not maintained, not validating stories. •
Your Counter : Reference your role in facilitating backlog refinement with Andre, creating 
functional specs, validating data models with Ayesha, and translating business survey 
feedback (Angelica’s responses) into actionable backlog items.•
Emphasize : Your proactive alignment with Agile frameworks and your request for role 
clarity documentation (to avoid overlap between PO/PM/Analyst).•
Adaptability & Growth
Manager's Claim : Avoids new tasks, passive.Performance Improvement Plan 
Monday, September 15, 2025 2:33 PM
   Performance Page 201    
Manager's Claim : Avoids new tasks, passive. •
Your Counter : List how you took on SAP table exploration, routing/operations mapping, 
Power BI collaboration, and professional certifications —all outside your initial 
reconciliation scope.•
Show Examples : Adapting to shifting ownership expectations (e.g., when reconciliation 
epics moved multiple times).•
Proactivity & Self -Learning
Manager's Claim : Relies too much on others, misses ownership. •
Your Counter : Show initiatives you led without being asked —reconciliation query 
frameworks in BigQuery, functional spec drafts, requirement gathering notes, training 
documentation, survey design for Ann & Doug, etc.•
4. Leverage the Discrepancy
Performance Record : Show that you exceeded FY25 goals and led in RIGHT behaviors. •
Contradiction : Highlight the contradiction between exceeding goals and being 
underperforming enough to justify a PIP.•
5. Next Steps
Draft a Written Rebuttal : Include evidence attached (Jira tickets, emails, SteerCo decks, 
certifications).•
Request HR -Mediated Meeting : To review the discrepancies. •
Keep Tone Factual and Professional :  rame this as “misalignment in perception vs. 
documented outcomes.”•
Draft Rebuttal Letter/Email
Here’s a draft you can use to structure your rebuttal:
Subject: Response to Performance Improvement Plan
Dear [Manager's Name],
Thank you for providing the Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) and the opportunity to 
address the concerns raised. While I take this feedback seriously, I respectfully disagree with 
certain points as they do not align with my actual performance and documented outcomes.
Completed Goals
All my official goals (Customer & Consumer Obsession, Data & Digital, Operational 
Excellence, Development, Engagement) were completed early and marked as 
“Completed” in the system.•

Jira ticket numbers: [List of Jira tickets]•

Product Management certificate course.•
Engagement

specifications.

Maintain a centralized repository for requirements and process documentation.
Target Dates: 
Update every 1:1
Survey/Feedback to be completed by Oct 10 and review results by Oct 17.
Means of Measure: 
At least 90% of requirements are documented within agreed timelines.
Stakeholder survey/feedback with a minimum satisfaction score of 4/5 regarding clarity and 
completeness of documentation.
Stakeholder Collaboration & Communication (20%)
Schedule and lead regular meetings with stakeholders to review progress and confirm alignment on 
requirements
Simplify complex technical requirements into clear business terms for non -technical audiences.
Provide concise written and verbal updates to stakeholders.
Target Dates: 
Conduct stakeholder meetings at least weekly during requirements gathering.
Meeting notes shared within 24 hours after each session.
Means of Measure: 
Attendance, punctuality, and participation in 100% of assigned stakeholder meetings .
Stakeholder survey feedback showing improvement in communication clarity (minimum 4/5 
rating).
Documented meeting notes/action items shared within 24 hours of each session.

gathering                                                           .

Execute assigned test cases and provide outcome documentation by Day 40.

Question
Question
The supervisor shall meet with the Team Member during the course of the performance 
improvement plan to gauge progress against the objectives and provide feedback to the Team 
Member. These meetings shall occur at the supervisor’s direction, and on a bi -weekly basis, at 
a minimum. For Performance Improvement Plans of shorter duration, e.g. 30 days, progress 
should be measured on a weekly basis. Human Resources attendance at these meetings is 
required for the initial meeting, and encouraged, but not mandatory for subsequent follow up 
meetings. Human Resources should be kept apprised of all discussions for the entirety of the 
PIP.

 Core Product / Agile Certifications (Very Strong for Product Owners)
Certification Why It's Good
Certified Scrum Product 
Owner (CSPO)The classic Product Owner cert. Deepens your skill running agile teams, 
backlog grooming, user stories, working with stakeholders. Highly 
respected.
SAFe Product 
Owner/Product Manager 
(SAFe POPM)If you're in an enterprise company (like Tyson), scaling agile is huge. SAFe 
teaches enterprise agile frameworks beyond just Scrum.
Professional Scrum 
Product Owner I (PSPO I)Focused more tightly on the Product Owner role than the general Scrum 
Master certs. Great if you want proof you know how to drive value in an 
agile environment.
 Business / IT Alignment Certifications (For Strategy + Analysis Skills)
Certification Why It's Good
ECBA (Entry Certificate in 
Business Analysis)If you want to grow your business analysis skills (requirements gathering, 
stakeholder management, process improvement). Lightweight, but very 
good for a PO.
CBAP (Certified Business 
Analysis Professional) (later 
on)Once you have more years' experience —CBAP is heavyweight and 
earns respect for senior BA/Product roles.
PMI-ACP (Agile Certified 
Practitioner)Broader agile certification covering Scrum, Kanban, Lean. Shows you're 
flexible across methodologies.
 Enterprise Systems (SAP/Integration Side) —[Since you already deal with SAP BOM Portal!]
Certification Why It's Good
SAP Certified Application Associate –
Business Process Integration with SAP 
S/4HANAWould strengthen your technical understanding of SAP 
systems and integrations, making you a killer bridge between 
tech and business.
SAP Certified Associate –SAP Activate 
Project ManagerFocused on SAP project implementation. Great if you 
manage SAP -related projects as a Product Owner.
 Top 3 Recommendations for You Specifically:
Certified Scrum Product Owner (CSPO) —the single best "stamp" for a Product Owner. 1.
SAFe POPM —if you want to stand out at large enterprises (Tyson, etc.) 2.
SAP Business Process Integration (if you want to double -down on SAP expertise) 3.

Role of the business analyst
Change agent –helps organization makes change
Enable change within organization
Come in understand the vision, look at where there at today
Gap analysis –what steps to take to get from where at today to where they want to go
Objectives and goals of business as well as stakeholders ie customers, users, etc
Help translate into terms into whoever is creating the solution
Translating needs into functional Ideas
Role is broad, gap analysis, design, implementation, testing, rolling out, training, support after
Example: Company Vision Wants all Work From Home
Analyst understands vision, needs of users; what they need access to, etc
Adjusting processes
Understand what the system is what it does and what they want that future system to be and then 
helping them get there
Types of business analysts roles
Business Process Analyst –models business processes, process models uses workflow and process 
engines or what -if scenarios, future process flows, business process engineer, workflow engineer, 
business process modeler
Requirements Analyst –works with stakeholders and end users to illicit understand identify and 
document needs and wants of organizations of users, bridges role of business and IT, use IT solutions to 
solve problems, requirements engineer, functional architect
Systems Analyst –create functional specifications define how a system will do what, works with techs to 
create a solution 
Data Analyst –mines business data to find patterns/correlations between systems, design create reports 
to help business execs in decision making, data modelers, bi analyst, data wharehouse analyst
User Experience Analyst –designs interface, focuses on efficiency and ease of use, understands the end -
user's behavior  uI design web designer, usability professional. 
6 key skills
Communication –oral and written, business analyst is a bridge 
Negotiation –involved in projects, resolution is agreeable by both parties
Problem solving –glorified problem solver, recommend solutions, break down into manageable 
components
Facilitation –meetings, direct, present, and achieve meeting goal
Organization –different projects, different people, good notes to reference during and after project
Critical thinking –analyze findings and come up with solutionsSection 2: Basics
Thursday, September 12, 2024 9:27 AM
   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 209    
Overview of Change
Change is a transformation in response to an identified need. 
It typically follows this pattern:
Problem identification.1.
Change implementation.2.
Solution creation to address the problem and identified needs.3.
Changes can involve:
New technology.•
Process optimization.•
New products and services.•
Risks and Investments:
Change comes with risks, time, resources, and monetary investments.•
Predictive vs. Adaptive Approaches
Predictive Approach
Goal: Attempt to foresee the future and work in a procedural manner.•
Process:
Define the problem.1.
Design the solution.2.
Create the solution.3.
Test the solution.4.
Roll out the solution.5.•
Stages:
Analysis, Design, Creation, Testing, Implementation.••
Benefits:
Not all team members need to be engaged in every phase.•
Provides natural checkpoints for sponsors to adjust plans before the next phase.••
Constraints:
The solution development takes time, delaying value production.•
Difficulty updating requirements if business needs change.••
Adaptive Approach
Goal: Embrace change and remain flexible to changing circumstances.•
Characteristics:
Allows new requirements to emerge during the process.•
Focuses on deploying small, incremental pieces of value.•
Provides hands -on user experience and feedback to shape solutions. ••
Constraints:
Difficult to budget.•
Uncertainty about when the solution is complete.••
Common Predictive Frameworks
Waterfall Method
Stages:
Analysis: Requirements elicitation, analysis, and documentation.1.
Design: Outlining how the solution will work and function.2.
Creation: Building the solution.3.
Testing: Resolving defects and issues to ensure readiness.4.•Section 3: Approaching Change
Thursday, September 12, 2024 9:28 AM
   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 210    
Testing: Resolving defects and issues to ensure readiness.4.
Implementation: Deploying the finalized solution.5.
Advantages:
Linear progress provides clarity on project progression.•
Defined checkpoints for validating outputs.•
Predictable timeline for organizational preparedness.••
Disadvantages:
Rigid, with limited flexibility for changing requirements.••
Industries Using Waterfall:
Construction, Regulatory Compliance, Manufacturing, Infrastructure Development, 
Government Projects.••
V Model (Verification and Validation Model)
Stages:
Define Requirements.1.
Design Solution.2.
Develop Solution.3.
Test Modules.4.
Test Solution.5.
User Acceptance.6.•
Characteristics:
Emphasizes a relationship between development, creation, and testing.•
Suitable for industries with strict compliance or safety standards.••
Industries Using V Model:
Medical Device Development, Aerospace & Defense, Automotive Software, Government 
Projects, Process Automation, Critical Infrastructure Projects.••
Extended V Model:
Includes an analysis phase at the beginning and a benefits phase at the end to ensure 
business objectives are met.••
Common Adaptive Frameworks
Scrum
Purpose: Optimized for delivering, developing, and sustaining solutions to complex problems. •
Pillars:
Transparency: Clear understanding and communication of the process. 1.
Inspection: Regularly reviewing progress towards goals. 2.
Adaptation: Adjusting based on learnings to shape the solution and team. 3.•
Iterations: Conducted in sprints with prioritized backlogs and daily meetings. •
Kanban
Purpose: Focuses on providing early value to the organization. •
Features:
Kanban board with columns to show the status of tasks.•
Team members self -assign tasks and move them as progress is made. •
Focus on completing work in progress before starting new work.••
Scrumban
Combination: Merges Scrum and Kanban features. •
Characteristics:
Self-assignment of tasks.•
Adaptable to fit company needs.••
Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe)
Purpose: Used for larger organizations to handle extensive changes, requiring communication, 
coordination, and team alignment.•
Advantages: Good for highly complex solutions but may involve excessive hierarchy. •
DevOps
Focus: Practices, principles, and cultural values fostering collaboration and enhancing efforts to •
   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 211    
Focus: Practices, principles, and cultural values fostering collaboration and enhancing efforts to 
deliver high -quality software quickly and reliably.•
Stages:
Planning: Requirements gathering and definition.1.
Coding: Implementing features.2.
Building: Compiling and creating deployable artifacts.3.
Testing: Integration and acceptance testing.4.
Deploying: Releasing to production.5.
Monitoring: Identifying and resolving issues.6.•
Culture: Continuous improvement and automation for efficiency. •
