Understanding the Business Objective
Understand what the goal of the project is, why is this project here
What is the purpose of the project?1.
What are the goals and objectives of this project?2.
In the eyes of the project, what is success and how will it be measured?3.
Writing and Presenting a Business Case
Business Case
A decision making tool used to determine the effects a particular deciosn will have on profitability 
( a document presented to decision makers giving them options and based on the actions heres 
the cost benefit or ROI by making a decision one way or another•
Driver of projects•
Identify and highlight opportunities a business has and the different options and recommended 
solution•
Used to convince decision makers about the course of action
Selling the business in investing money to solve this problem○•
Not always writtenc•
Created prior to begin of project, roi prior to taking action•
Analysts, managers, executives can crease business cases•
Creating a Business Case
Phase 1 –Initial Analysis
Understand problem or opportunity•
Determine high -level requirements •
Identify data needed to suppor the business to take action (ROI)•
Validate with decision makers the high level return is worth the potential investment•
Analyze the likelihood project will be approved and if you should continue•
Phase 2 –Determine Potential Solutions
Identify all possible solutions to the problem
Benefits ○
Costs○
Timetable of project○
Time before a return on investment is realized○
Risks ○•
*One of the solutions should be to do nothing, no action taking
Phase 3 –Write the Business Case
Executive Summary –high level summary about the document •
Problem statement•
Analysis•
Solution options•
Cost -benefit analysis•
Recommendation•
Phase 4 –Review Business Case
Validate problem statement justifies call to action•
Ensure all valid solutions are given•
Double check cost -benefit analysis calculations •
Objectively dissect your recommendation•
Correct any spelling or grammatical mistakes•
Ask another person to review the document•
Get project buy –in of two key stakeholders •
Phase 5 –Present the Business Case
Remind yourself, they havent seen this before•
Clearly define the problem and business need to act •
Give your recommendation•
Explain ROI•
Touch on each risk, but unless asked, don't dive deep•
Mention stakeholder backers•
Close the presentation summarizing the benefits and ROI•
Project Stake Holders
Anyone that has a stake in the project
Internal or external○•
Identify stake holder
Increase the chances for success○
More ideas○
More perspectives○
Increased credibility○•
Get ideas from them as you identify them•
Assigning Responsibilities to stakeholders using a RACI Matrix
Raci Matrix –identify responsibilities early on in the project 
Alleviates power struggles
Reduces lack of ownership
Sets clear expectations
Responsible –who is/will be doing this task? Who's assigned to the task?
Accountable –who has the authority to sign off the work, who's head will roll?
Consulted –Who can tell me more about this task? Subject matter experts
Informed –Who's work depends on this task? Who has to be kept updated about progress

Requirements 101
Guides the desing of the eventual solution
Without correct requirements, you cannot design or build the correct product
60% of project failures originate with the requirements
Categories of Requirements
Functional Requirements
Things the product must do•
Action the product must take•
Non -Functional Requirements
Properties or qualities the product must have •
How the product will behave•
Constraints
Global Requirements
Purpose of the project○
Users of a product○•
Product Constraints 
Purpose of the product –reason for building the product •
Client, Customer, and Stakeholders –people that interact with the product •
Users of the Product –intended end -users and how they affect product usability •
Requirements Constraints –limitation of the project and restrictions on design •
Naming conventions and definitions –vocabulary of the product •
Relevant facts –outside influences that make a difference to this product •
Assumptions –assumptions developers are making •
Functional Requirements
Scope of the product –defines boundaries and connection to other products •
Functional and data requirements –things the product must do and data manipulated by the 
functions•

Tips for Producing Valid Requirements
Should use the word shall•
Only one shall per requirement•
Written in short, simple sentences•
Consistent terminology•
Stated positively•
Accompanied by notes and comments to support and clarify •
Stated positively•
Accompanied by notes and comments to support and clarify•
Stated imperatively•
Don't use will and should •
Translations for Requirement Verbiage
OR-select one of these options •
Can, should -express desire or suggestion instead of requirements •
Must -100% reliability •
Are, is, will -Descriptive part to lead into the requirement •
Support, and/or -confusing •
But not limited to, etc -incomplete requirement/thought •
Shall -dictates specification and functional capability •
Terms to Avoid
Adequate•
Approximately•
Better than•
Comparison•
Easy•
Maintainable•
Maximize•
Minimize•
Normally•
Optimize •
Quality product•
Quick•
Rapid•
Substantial•
Sufficient•
Timely•

Business Rules
Business Rules –A rule that defines or constrains some aspect of business and always resolves to either 
true or false. 
Purpose: Business rules are intended to assert business structure or to control or influence the behavior 
of the business
   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 216    
of the business
Entered email addresses must appear valid (contain @ and .)•
Each class must have at least one instructor•
Customers must have a valid driver's license to rent a vehicle•
A quote must be completed prior to an invoice being generated•
Business Rules vs Business Requirements
Rule: entered email addresses must appear valid
Possible Requirements
Capability to enter email address
Alert agent when email doesn't appear to be valid
Allow for correction of email if invalid email format is entered
Rule: Each course must have at least one instructor
Possible Requirements:
Rule –each course must have at least one instructor
Capability for dean to assign instructor to course○
Course registration cannot be opened until an instructor is assigned○•
Rule –Customers must have a valid driver's license to rent a vehicle
Employee to inspect driver's license○
Ability for employee to validate driver's license○•
Rule –A quote must be completed prior to an invoice being generated
Capability to enter a quote○
Details from quote must automatically flow to the invoice○
Ability to tie the quote and invoices together for reporting○•
Business Rules Best Practices
When documenting rules, keep it simple•
Business requirements are used to comply with business rules•
Each business rule may need multiple requirements•
Business rules should not be changed
Changes can cause major constraints down the road○•
   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 217    
Requirement Elicitation Basics
Getting business requirements through various activiteis thorugh users and stake holders and be 
able to understand their system constraints and their expecations
Elicitation Technique: Brainstorming 
Brainstorming –solving problems, generate ideas, create consensus
Brainstorming Types
Individual
Project team member creates list of ideas
Open
Participants call out ideas that are captured by scribe
Structured
Participants write down their ideas
Facilitator goes particpant to participant to have them share on idea each
Coninuing sharing process unil all the idea
Brainstorming Pros and Cons
Advantages Disadvantages
Generates multiple ideas quickly Ideas are not discussed/explored
Involves multiple perspectives True meaning may be ambiguous or misunderstood
Promotes equal participation
Brainstorming: Best Practices
Determine type of brainstorming meeting ahead of time•
Publish an agenda prior to the brainstorming session•
Clearly state the objective of the meeting•
Create environment to encourage participation•
Groundrules
Don't discuss ideas only as questions to clarify○
Don't dismiss or discount an idea or person○
Do not build on others' suggestions and Ideas○•
Do build on other's suggestions and ideas•
Establish Roles
Timekeeper○
Scribe○
Facilitator○•
Create process for combining, categorizing, and summarizing like ideas•
If complex, create multiple meetings to keep meeting fatigue low•
Schedule follow -up meetings •
Prioritize final idea to plan further analysis•
Elicitation Technique: Requirement Workshops
Structured meeting that involves:
End users
Subject matter experts
Project manager
Business
It reps
Generally is used for projects with multiple business units
Works to  define, clarify, and complete requirements
Starts at broad level and dives into function and processes as the workshop moves forward 
Advantages Disadvantages
Effective at getting real requirements instead of 
perceived requirementsDifficult to get appropriate stakeholders 
in one room at same time
Greater chance of obtaining consensus because issues 
and questions are asked in real -timeIncreased number of global projects 
poses logistic difficulties and adds 
complexity
Confirmation of requirement accuracy is immediate Success of the session is highly dependent 
on the expertise of the facilitator
Successfully gather requirements from a large group 
in a short period of timeCan be expensive
Documentation is completed within hours of the 
session and provided quickly back to participants for 
review
Best Practices
Determine type of requirement workshop ahead of time•
Be clear on what sessions will deliver•
Utilize modeling tools to visualize the processes and requirements•
Facilitator should be experienced•
Limit the meeting to the key project participants
Include end -users, subject matter experts, developers, and senior management ○•
Remember that you are an analyst, not just a scribe/facilitator•
Elicitation Technique: Interviewing
Systematic discussion to drive out accurate requirements quickly•
Gain understanding of high -level needs, constraints, and assumptions •
Reduces misunderstanding de to cultural differences, lack of openness, and acronyms/vocabulary•
One on one or with a small group•
Can be formal or informal•
See the process or requirements from interviewee's perspective•
Interviewing: Types
Personal Interviews
Scripted questions –interviewee's answers documented ○
Exploratory questions to clarify and validate requirements, while removing assumptions○•
Job shadowing
Walk through a work day with a user or user group observing them○•Activity: Utilize the elicitation skills you've learned
This activity is all about you working through how you would handle this real -world scenario.  
SCENARIO:   You are working as a Business Analyst for ABC Company.  You have been tasked 
with playing the BA role for an oncoming project.  The Project Manager has fully defined the 
project and created a Project Charter that lays out the specific objective the project is looking 
to achieve. The project is pertaining to the Customer Support team and more specifically how 
they are handling incoming support calls.  The project was funded by the VP of Customer 
Service because the incoming support call volume has continually risen quarter over quarter 
and customer cases are taking a significantly long time to close (average of 4.5 days to close 
now in comparison to 2.1 days a year ago), thus causing a decrease in sales.  It is assumed 
(based on feedback from Customer Service Managers) that the increase in time to close a case 
is directly correlated to the number of cases their teams are handling.  It is also assumed (based 
on feedback from the Customer Retention Managers) that the customer dissatisfaction with the 
case close time is decreasing the sales volume for ABC Company.  
You, as the Business Analyst, are charged with completing an Analysis and understanding what 
the cause of the issues really are and some potential solutions that could be put in place to fix 
the found issues.
For this activity, you are to plan for who you would need to elicit requirements from, what you 
would be addressing with them (or hoping to gain from them), and the elicitation technique or 
techniques you feel would be the best to apply.  Once you are done, share this with a family 
member or a friend, and get their feedback on your plan.  There is no right or wrong answers 
here.  You simply are applying the pros and cons of each elicitation technique to a real -world 
scenario.
Enjoy!!Section 6: Requirement Elicitation

Interviewing: Best Practices
Determine the best interview type to accomplish your goals•
Appropriately prepare for the interview•
Schedule interviews ahead of time•
Respect the person by being on time and display interest in the subject•
Match the pace of the interviewee
If they are cautious, talk slow. If they are in a hurry, talk quickly○•
Check understanding often•
Let interviewee know what will be one with the information•
One person conducts interview while other documents answers, or record interview•
Ask for ezamples of their issues and document screen shot •
Interview two to three users for each user category targeting•
Interview end -users, not just upper management who thinks they know how thehow the 
process/system is used•
Create a thank -you email appreciating their time and how the information will help create quality 
requirements -(sent with interview invite)•
Create and follow -up email telling the person how the information will be used and the next steps 
for the project•
Allow time in schedule to debrief and finish documentation after each interview•
Interviewing: Interview Questions
Make questions open -ended
If they could answer the question with a yes or no, reward it○•
Avoid questions that may present judgement or a conclusion •
Allow the questions to flow naturally so they can be put into conversation rather than a survey•
Interview Document
Name of interviewee •
Role of person and their primary responsibilities•
Open -ended questions•
Space for answers•
Space for interviewers' insights•
Action item box for flagging key pieces of information
Requirement, new requirement risk, assumption, or constraint○•
Sample Interview Questions
What are other ways you accomplish this goal?•
Tell me about your frustrations with this process•
What would the process look like if it was perfect•
What standards or regulations should we be aware of?•
What purpose is accomplished by using the product or process•
What equipment do people need to use•
How long should tasks take•
What people do you share information with•
What failures cause the orgainzation the most pain•
What didn't I ask that I should have?•
If we could only change one aspect of the process what would it be•
Elicitation Technique: Surveys
Open -ended Questions
Gives respondents an opportunity to answer in their own words○
Useful, time consuming to interpret and catalogue ○•
Closed -ended questions
Finite set of answers for each question○
Lends itself to statistical analysis○
Tough to create questions that are not leading or need an "other" answer○
Questions can vary
Not important to extremently important▪
Disagree to agree▪
Rank order a list of items▪
Multiple choice▪○•
Advantages Disadvantages
Require limited stakeholder's time Low response rate
Effective at reaching dispersed stakeholders Poorly worded questions may provide inaccurate 
information
Scalable for larger audiences Use of open -ended questions requirements more 
analysis by the business analyst
Relatively fast and inexpensive to administer Require both instrument training and problem or 
business domain experience
Supplement more subjective info, such as 
opinions gained through interviewIncentives for responding might be expensive
   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 219    
opinions gained through interview
Surveys: Best Practices
Focus on high -priority identified risks •
Set a baseline by identifying user satisfaction with existing systems•
Direct and unambiguous •
Complex questions later in survey•
Demographic info last•
Create rewards for participating•
Create survey with inexpensive online tools•
Notify the participants when the survey is available and continue to remind them to participate•
Elicitation Technique: Documentation Review
Review existing documentation
User guides○
Prior system implementation documentation, including lessons learned○
Technical documentation○
Lessons learned after completion of latest project○•
Formulates context for understanding the scope of the project •
Advantages Disadvantages
Current process documentation provides a 
starting pointExisting documentation may be old and out -of-date
The reviewer needs domain and technical expertise to 
determine if existing
Can be time consuming, and may not provide the desired 
payback
Documentation Review: Best Practices
Know the purpose for reviewing•
Set self -imposed time limits •
Create a flossary of terms from former project documentation•
Elicitation Technique: Analyzing Interfaces
Revie system, people, and process linkages•
Determine needs for input, output, and the medium•
Describe manual and automated processes•
Advantages Disadvantages
Discovers missed interfaces and their purposes Not useful as standalone elicitation activity
Determines regulations or interface standards Can begin to focus on many technical details
Provides missed requirements Can be redundant with modeling activities
Uncovers areas of project risk

Introduction to Requirement Analysis
1. all stakeholders to understand requirement1.
2. business customer must understand the need and what it will do so they can prioritize it2.
3. design and implement a solution to meet requirements3.
Visual Modeling Concepts
Graphical representation using a modeling language that takes something complex and makes it 
easier to understand
Benefits of Visual Modeling
Easily understand complex information•
Gets all stakeholders involved•
Receive requirements efficiently•
Identify the underlying problem•
Analyze 'what if' scenarios•
Allows removal of irrelevant information•
What Gets Modeled?
Current state "as -is" •
Future state, "to -be" •
Requirements fill the gap•
Business Models
Organizational Chart
Breaks down the hierarchy of the org making it easier to identify stakeholders○•
Stakeholder Map
Visual model of the system and all stakeholders by department○•
Use Case Diagram•
Process Flow Diagram•
User interface wireframe
Design of a system○•
Technical Models
System context diagram –the system and what people/systems are interacting with the main 
system and how•
Data Flow Diagram –helps to show where the data is flowing or how it's working •
CRUD Matrix
Roles are across the top and specific info on left hand column○
Create○
Read○
Update○
Delete Information○•
State Diagram•
Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD)•
BPMN vs UML
Business Process Modeling Notation vs Unified Modeling Language 
modeling languages for creating visual models•
Activity -activity within a process, triggered by an event •
Event -manual or automated action, or delay in time that triggers an action •
Gateway -Split of pathways, where multiple paths can be taken or decision on a path must be 
made per a condition•
Flow -Direction of the sequence or order of events and actions •
Swimlanes -visual distinction of who is doing what within a process •

Categorizing Requirements
Breaking requirements down into functional, non -functional, supplemental, and constraint
Aids in documentation •
Helps to prioritize •
Assists in estimating the system cost•
Identifies areas that requqire further investigation•
Functional Requirements
Things the product must do•
Action the product must take•
Nonfunctional requirements 
Properties or qualities the product must have•
How the product will behave•
Product Restraints
Global Requirements
Purpose of the project○
Users of a product○•
Deriving Requirements
Parsing Requirements
Breaking down requirements that are too broada.
Original Requirement
User Completed fields on tax forms shall be converted to electronic text documentsi.b.
Parsed Requirements
System shall be able to convert handwriting to texti.
System shall be able to convert machine print to textii.
System shall be able to electronically correct user -completed fields iii.c.
Remove "And" From Requirements
Risk is high that only one of the conditions will be testedi.
Hard to trace the requirement bug/failureii.d.1.
Interpreting Requirements
Reduce generalness and ambiguity of state requirementsa.
Original Requirement
"Each PC shall have state -of-the-art software installed" i.b.
Interpreted Requirement
Each pc shall mace microsoft office 2013 and windows 10 installedi.c.
Parsed Requirements:
Each pc shall have microsoft office 2013 installedi.
Each PC shall have windows 10 installedii.d.2.
Focusing Requirements
Combine overlapping requirements into one focused requirementa.
Original Requirement
Each pc must have a standard spreadsheet tool installed that runs in windowsi.b.
Focused requirement:
Each PC on the LAN shall have Microsoft Office Excel 2013i.c.3.
Qualifying Requirements
Add a requirement to provide a method of verification or compliance to make it testablea.
Original Requirement
The XXX command must preform the following actions i.b.
Qualified Requirement
Each command shall be executed during system testing to demonstrate its 
functionalityi.c.4.
Assigning Requirement Attributes
Clarification •
Filtering •
Validation•
Typical Attributes
Unique Identifier –req001, identifier unique to specific requirement •
Acceptance criteria –what is the criteria used to validate that the requirement has been met •
Author •
Complexity•
Ownership•
Performance•
Urgency•
Business value•
Status •
Priority•
Source•
Prioritizing Requirements
Generally there are too many functions and feature to implement within the project schedule and 
budget
Prioritization Factors
Value to business•
Value to customer•
Minimize cost to develop•
Time to implement•
Ease of technical implementation•
Ease of business implementation•
Obligation to some external authority•
3 Step Prioritization Process
Step 1 –define usefulness to business (critical, important, nice to have) •
Step 2 –Estimate cost (1 -5 scale) •
Step 3 –Determine Timeframe (1 -5 Scale) •
Best Practices
Keep it simple•
Business Value reigns supreme•
Remove prioritization away from politics•
Prioritize (and re -prioritize) after each project iteration •
Validating Requirements
Going through and making requirements are valid
Specific, measurable, attainable, reasonable, traceable○•Prioritizing Requirements Example
You are hired by a brick -and-mortar business to help them create and implement an e -
commerce store. Below are two requirements and you are asked which one should be 
completed first.
What do you think?
(1) A website visitor can save products to their wishlist.
Usefulness: Nice to have --They can easily find and purchase those products later. Not 
necessary for the e -commerce store to function•
Estimate: 4 --lots of hidden requirements as they will also need the ability to review the list, 
remove from the list, add to cart from list, etc.•
Timeframe: 4 --will take additional requirement discussions, designing, etc. •
(2) A website visitor can change the number (quantity) of products in their cart
Usefulness: Important --being able to change the number of items from the cart is important 
to a good user experience•
Estimate: 2 --small scope, only affects the cart and (maybe) the checkout process •
Timeframe: 2 --implementing should be fairly straightforward after requirements are fully 
identified.•
======================================
SCROLL DOWN FOR ANSWER
======================================
As you look at these two requirements, you can see that the ability to change the quantity (2) 
should be prioritized above the wishlist item; it is more useful and it will take less time to 
develop and implement
(1) A website visitor can save products to their wishlist.
Usefulness: Nice to have --They can easily find and purchase those products later. Not 
necessary for the e -commerce store to function•
Estimate: 4 --lots of hidden requirements as they will also need the ability to review the list, 
remove from the list, add to cart from list, etc.•
Timeframe: 4 --will take additional requirement discussions, designing, etc. •
(2) A website visitor can change the number (quantity) of products in their cart
Usefulness: Important --being able to change the number of items from the cart is important 
to a good user experience•
Estimate: 2 --small scope, only affects the cart and (maybe) the checkout process •
Timeframe: 2 --implementing should be fairly straightforward after requirements are fully 
identified.•Section  8: Requirement Specification

Houses all requirements, use cases, stake holders, everything illiciting are documented here•
Business analyst is responsible for completeness and acuract•
Houses all requirements for this specific project•
Lists and houses stake holders in project•
Stakeholders sign off on BRD •
Help design solution, capturing what business needs, design shows, solution implemented•

Overview
Business Team Approval•
Technical Team Approval•
Project Sponsor / Committee Approval•
Gaining Business Approval
Schedule
Multiple Review Sessions•
Separate Business Units•
Never exceed four hours per session•
Involve subject matter experts (SME)•
Keep it relevant to the audience•
Create meeting agenda•
Conduct
Explain the purpose of the meeting and the agenda•
Review project and objective•
Go over each requirement•
Address questions and concerns immediately•
Change and update requirements•
Table all new requirements unless deemed critical•
*if critical requirements are determined, reschedule the approval meeting
Gaining Technical Approval
Session 1 –schedule initial high level review •
Session 2 –schedule in -depth follow up review •
Include technical subject matter experts (SMEs)•
Include technical lead / architect•
Create agendas for both meetings•
Session 1: Conduct
Explain purpose of meeting and agenda1.
Review project and objective2.
Touch on each section of requirements3.
Identify any major technical concerns4.
Answer questions5.
Session 2: Conduct
Explain the purpose of the meeting and agenda1.
Review project objective2.
Go through each requirement to validate technical feasibility3.
Identify any troublesome requirements4.
Verify enough detail for design phase5.
Make or buy decision
Make –create high level design architecture a.
Buy –determine options (competitive comparison matrix b.6.
Update project estimated cost7.
Gaining Sponsor or Committee Approval
Schedule:
Create presentation to talk high level about project

   Business Analysis Fundamentals -ECBA, CCBA, CBAP Page 225    
Conducting a Project Review
Project Review
Brings project team and other stakeholders together to discuss what went well and what didn't•
Can be multiple meeting depending on size of project•
Send a survey ahead of time to collect feedback•
Prepare and send an agenda prior to the meeting•
Feedback Survey
Sent at least one week before the meeting asking about thoughts on project
Overall, how successful do you think this project was? What went right (3 things)a.
What obstacles where faced (3 things)b.
What needs improvement/should have been done differently (3 things)c.
Do you have any other comments?d.•
Review Meeting
Tend to focus on negative so keep it non -confrontational and be sure to ask about what went right •
Work through each section of the feedback survey and facilitate positive and meaningful 
discussion•
Take notes both on positive and constructive feedback•
Post Review Meeting
Analyze the results from review meeting and recommend process or procedural changes that 
should be made for future projects•
Verifying Project Completion
Validate project objectives have been met and analyzes if the project adhered to budget, 
schedule, and quality requirements.
Completed by project manager○
Format and level of detail varies per project○
States any open issues or risks○
Outlines high level customer satisfaction on project○•Section 10: After the Project

Episode 1: Software Usage Requirements
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -2-1-1-software -usage -requirements •
Description : Don and Ronnie discuss the various requirements for using software, including costs, 
licensing agreements, and platform compatibility.•
Keywords : Single -platform software, Cross -platform software, Licensing, Installation •
Content :
General Application Concepts :
Single -Platform Software : Runs on a single OS or CPU type (e.g., Windows -only 
applications, x86/ARM -specific software).•
Cross -Platform Software : Runs on multiple platforms (e.g., Linux and macOS). •
Compatibility Concerns : Software may be "ported" to other OSes, but performance 
may vary.••
Licensing Types :
Single -use, group/site licenses, volume licensing, concurrent licensing. •
Open Source vs. Proprietary :
Open Source: MIT License, Apache 2.0, GNU GPLv3.•
Proprietary: Closed -source with specific user conditions. •••
Costs :
Subscription vs. One -time Purchase : E.g., Office 365 (subscription) vs. Office 2016 
(one -time purchase).•
Product Keys/Serial Numbers : Acquired through various methods (OEM, email). ••
Software Installation Best Practices :
Reading instructions and agreements (EULA).•
Understanding installation processes for different OSes (e.g., Linux, Windows).•••

Episode 1: Programming Concepts
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -3-1-1-programming_concepts •
Description : Don and Ronnie introduce application development, covering fundamental 
programming concepts and demonstrating basic application design.•
Content :
What is "Code"?
Code is written as source code , which consists of collections of instructions. •
Source code follows specific styles/formats based on a programming language . ••
Types of Programming Languages :
Interpreted Languages : Examples include JavaScript, Perl, Python, and PHP. •
Compiled Languages : Examples include C, C++, and Rust. •
Query Languages : SQL, Transact -SQL, XQuery. •
Assembly Language : Directly corresponds to hardware. •
Machine Code : Lowest -level code understood by hardware. ••
Choosing a Language :
The decision is subjective , depending on needs and goals. ••
How Languages Work :
Programming is like a flow -chart , where actions follow a series of decisions. •
Example: MIT Scratch for beginners. ••
Key Concepts :
Variables : Data containers. •
Arrays : Collections of variables. •
Functions : Blocks of code performing specific tasks. •
Objects : Contain properties (attributes). •
Loops : Execute code repeatedly until a condition is met.•••

python
Copy code
def hello(name):
    if(name == "Ronnie"):

    else:
        print(name + " is super boring.")
hello("Ronnie")
hello("Don")•
Looping a Function :
Example of a while loop: •
python
Copy code
def hello(name):
    if(name == "Ronnie"):
        print(name + " is my favorite!")
    else:
        print(name + " is super boring.")
loop = 0
while(loop < 6):
    hello("Ronnie")
    hello("Don")
    loop = loop + 1•

Episode 1: Central Processing Unit (CPU)
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -4-1-0-central -processing -unit-cpu •
Description : Don and Ronnie introduce the CPU and discuss its role in different types of 
computing devices, from general -purpose PCs to IoT devices.•
Keywords : Workstations, Laptops, Servers, Mobile, Tablets, IoT, CPU, Processing Speed. •
Content :
CPU Overview : Central Processing Unit as the brain of a computer. •
Device Types :
General -purpose: PCs, workstations, laptops, servers. •
Specialized: Mobile phones, tablets, gaming consoles, IoT devices.••
CPU Architectures :
ARM : Energy -efficient, used in mobile devices. •
32-bit vs. 64 -bit:
32-bit: Limited to 4GB RAM, handles smaller data chunks.•
64-bit: Accesses more than 4GB RAM (up to 18 billion GB), handles larger data 
sets.•••
Processing Speeds :
Measured in MHz (1 million Hertz) or GHz (1 billion Hertz). •••

Content :
Internet Service Types :
Fiber Optic : Fastest, but limited availability. •
Cable : Uses TV cables, good bandwidth, shared connection.•
DSL: Faster than dial -up, speed depends on proximity to provider. •
Wireless : Relies on towers, requires external antenna. •
Satellite : Large coverage area, slower due to signal travel distance, limited data. •
Cellular LTE (4G) : Mobile or fixed connectivity, dependent on tower proximity, subject 
to data limits.•••

Episode 1: Database Concepts
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -6-1-1-database_concepts •
Description : Ronnie and Don introduce the fundamentals of modern databases, explaining their 
structure, advantages, and alternatives to databases.•
Content :
What is a Database?
A repository of information structured for quick access . •
Supports data -driven decisions , capturing, collecting, and correlating data for 
meaningful reporting .•
Records and storage enable data organization and access. ••
Database Capabilities :
Input Data : Data entry. •
Output Data : Querying and reporting. •
Data Persistence : Archiving data for long -term access. ••
Why Use a Database Over Flat Files (e.g., Excel)?
Multiple Concurrent Users . •
Scalability and speed . •
Handles a variety of data types . ••
Types of Databases :
Structured , semi -structured , and non-structured data models. •
Relational Databases vs. Non -relational Databases . •
Examples include key/value databases and document databases . ••
Example SQL Queries :
Generating a list of employees with their hire dates.•
Ranking employees by bonus amount.•••

Ways to Access a Database :
Direct/Manual Access : Risky due to potential data corruption. •

User Interface/Utility Access : Useful for research and development. •
Query/Report Builders : Efficient for repetitive queries. ••
SQL Command Types :

Data Definition Language (DDL) : Used for defining and structuring databases. ••

Episode 3: Table Design
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -6-3-1-table_design •
Description : Ronnie and Don explain how data is structured and stored in a database, focusing on 
relational design and data types.•
Content :
Elements of a Table :
Schema : Blueprint of database structure. •
Tables : Organize data. •
Rows/Records : Individual entries in a table. •
Fields/Columns : Define data attributes. •
Constraints : Rules for data integrity. ••
Relational Database Design :
Primary Key : Unique identifier for records. •
Foreign Key : Links records across tables. •
Indexes : Speed up data retrieval. ••
Example Table Design Tool :
DB Designer : Useful for visualizing database design. ••
Data Types :
Char/Strings : Text data. •
Numbers : Integers, floats. •
Boolean : True/False values. •••

Episode 1: Troubleshooting Methodology
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -8-1-1-troubleshooting -methodology •
Description : Don and Ronnie introduce the first half of the CompTIA 8 -step troubleshooting 
model, using a practical example to illustrate each step.•
Content :
Explain the Troubleshooting Methodology :
Step 1: Identify the Problem :
Gather Information : Collect data from the user and system logs. •
Duplicate the Problem : Reproduce the issue if possible. •
Question Users : Ask about recent changes or symptoms. •
Identify Symptoms : Determine specific signs of the issue. •
Determine Changes : Identify any recent changes in the environment. •
Approach Multiple Problems Individually . ••
Step 2: Research Knowledge Base/Internet :
Use resources to find similar issues and potential solutions.••
Step 3: Establish a Theory of Probable Cause :
Question the Obvious : Consider simple causes first. •
Consider Multiple Approaches : E.g., Divide and Conquer . ••
Step 4: Test the Validity of the Theory :
Perform tests to confirm or rule out the theory.••••
Episode 2: Troubleshooting Methodology Pt. 2
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -8-1-2-troubleshooting -methodology -pt2 •
Description : Don and Ronnie continue the 8 -step troubleshooting model, covering the remaining 
procedures and solving the problem introduced in the first part.•
Content :
Step 5: Establish a Plan of Action :
Resolve the Problem : Identify the steps to implement the solution. •
Identify Potential Effects : Consider any impact on other systems. ••
Step 6: Implement the Solution :
Apply the fix or escalate as necessary . ••
Step 7: Verify Full System Functionality :
Confirm that the problem is resolved and the system is working as expected.•
Implement Preventive Measures : Take steps to avoid recurrence. ••
Step 8: Document Findings :
Record Lessons Learned : Detail the issue, actions taken, and outcomes for future 
reference.•••
Episode 3: Troubleshooting Example
Filename : comptia -itfunfc0u61 -8-2-1-troubleshooting_example •
Description : Don and Ronnie apply the 8 -step troubleshooting model to a real -life hardware issue, 
demonstrating the process from start to finish.•
Content :
Walkthrough of the Troubleshooting Steps :
Identify the Problem : Gather initial details and duplicate the hardware issue. •
Research and Establish a Theory : Look for possible causes. •
Test the Theory : Validate the suspected cause through testing. •
Establish a Plan : Create a step -by-step plan to address the problem. •
Implement the Solution : Apply the fix to resolve the hardware issue. •
Verify Functionality : Ensure the computer is working correctly after the fix. •
Document Findings : Record what was learned during the troubleshooting process. •••8. Troubleshooting

Question #7 of 75 Question ID: 1257445
Which kind of application architecture includes making a purchase on the Internet?
¥ A) n -tier
x © B) Two -tier
% © C) Three -tier
x D) One -tier Explanation
Making a purchase on the Intemet is an example of n -tier application architecture. There might be 
several layers beyond three -tier:
e The web browser is the Presentation layer.
e The checkout cart is another layer.
« Customer information is controlled by another layer. e Credit card entry and verification is another 
layer.
e Shipping is another layer.
Tiers are separated into layers. The presentation layer (also called the client layer) is how the application 
appears to the user and how the user interacts with the application. The logic or programming layer 
uses what is generated in the presentation layer to create data. Data is the information that is used by 
the application.
With a one -tier application, the presentation, logic, and data layers are all in the same location. 
Examples include a locally installed application or a game on a smartphone that is played without a 
cellular or Wi -Fi connection.
The two -tier application architecture model can be represented by a local network -hosted application. 
Two -tier means two devices are needed. such as a local computer and a network server, to associate the 
user interface with the application’s programming and data.
Three -tier applications have each tier on a separate device. An example of this is Google Docs, where 
the presentation layer is on a user device such as a smartphone or computer, the logic layer is the 
application running from the Google Docs site, and the data layer contains the documents created and 
stored in Google Drive.
Objective: Applications and Software
Sub-Objective: Explain methods of application architecture and delivery models.
References:
CompTIA IT Fundamentals+ FC0 -U61 Cert Guide Premium Edition, Chapter 19: Getting Apps From Source 
to Your Device. Application Architecture Models
v © A)  T S x © B) Ext x © C)  AT32 v —D) HFS+ Explanation
NTFS and HFS+ file systems support compression. Neither Ext4 nor FAT32 support compression. With 
Ext4 and FAT32, 3 separate application would need to be used, such 35 gzip or pkzip, respectively, to 
compress files.
Compression is 3 way to reduce the amount of space necessary to store a file, allowing 3 drive to store 
more data. One way to look at compression is that it removes excess space in the file, much like vacuum 
suction reduces the amount of storage needed for 3 bag. Text files and documents are greatly reduced 
through compression, whereas mukimedia files, such as music, video, and images, do not benefit as 
much.
There are many file features. Not all features are available in all file systems. Some features include:
e Encryption scrambles the content of a file, folder, or drive so that it is unreadable to an unauthorized 
user. FAT32 uses 3 separate application, but encryption is supported by Ext4, NTFS, and HFS+. Encrypted 
files use a recovery key, similar to a password, to allow a user to open the file.
« Permissions are assigned to folders and files, and groups and users are granted those permissions. In 
the NTFS file system, the permissions are Full Control, Modify, Read and Execute, List Folder Contents, 
Read, and Write. When 3 permission is assigned to 3 folder and subsequently 3 subfolder is added to 
that folder, the subfolder inhents the permissions from the parent folder. A file inherits the permissions 
from the folder it is in. A subfolder, however, can be configured to NOT inherit permissions from its 
parent folder, shown for Sub Folder 1 in the graphic.
e Journaling is a feature that monitors changes that are occurring or about to occur to a drive. If a 
system crash occurs or the power goes out during a major update, journaling would make ®@ much 
easier to recover the drive. NTFS, HFS+, and Ext4 support journaling.
Every file system has limitations. They include the size of the volume, the largest file size. number of files 
per volume, and number of files per folder. The following exhibit shows the file size limits of several file 
systems.
Operating System File -size Limit Win22 wi FATIFAT32 2GB/4GB
Win22 wi NTFS 278 (possibly larger)
Linux 2.2 -Intel 32 -bit 2GB (LFS 4GB)
Linux 2 4+ (using ex43 file system) 4T8 Solaris 9/10
MacOS X wi HES+ 278
NetiVare wWINSS fle system 38718
File systems have naming rules, often called naming conventions. Files have 3 name (preferably 
describing the contents), followed by 3 dot, followed by an extension. The extension tells the operating 
system what type of file it is and uses the application specified (either by default or by user settings) to 
open it.Friday, October 11, 2024 12:22 PM
   Comptia ITF+ Page 246    
NetiVare wWINSS fle system 38718
File systems have naming rules, often called naming conventions. Files have 3 name (preferably 
describing the contents), followed by 3 dot, followed by an extension. The extension tells the operating 
system what type of file it is and uses the application specified (either by default or by user settings) to 
open it.
WUeSTION F1U OT /D Question ID: 1257376
Which of the following represents one bit? (Choose all that apply.)
A) anibble B) PB
Cc) 0
D) 1
E) 01000001
x X A © XK X&
F) F3
Explanation
A bit is represented as an individual 0 or 1, and is the level at which the processor operates. All 
commands must be translated to a 0 or a 1 So that the processor can use it. A bit is the smallest 
measurement of storage on a computer.
The values of 0 and 1 can also be used to represent no or yes, as well as false or true.
01000001 would represent a byte. A byte is a collection of eight bits (Os and 1s), such as 01000001. A 
single character in a plaintext file requires a byte to store it.
A nibble is a measurement of four bits, or half a byte.
F3 would be a hexadecimal number. Hexadecimal (or “hex”) numbers are based on values from 0 -9 
followed by A -F, with A -F representing the decimal numbers 10 -15.
eee eee ee
Pecma OB ee fo fees
Hexadecimal numbers are often represented as pairs, such as E& and 2F, but 32 can also be a 
hexadecimal number.
APB is a petabyte, roughly one quadrillion (1,125,899,906.842,620) bytes, with each byte representing 
eight bits. To put it in perspective, a PB would be enough storage to hold approximately 20,000 -45,000 
full-length HD movies.
Objective: IT Concepts and Terminology

Question 17 Incorrect

Correct answer
26
Your answer is incorrect
16
10
31
Overall explanation
A) The hexadecimal number 1A is equivalent to 26 in decimal. In hexadecimal, A represents 10, 
so 1A is calculated as (1 * 16) + 10 = 26.
Incorrect Answers:
B) Incorrect conversion of the hexadecimal number 1A to decimal.
C) Incorrect conversion; A in hexadecimal represents 10, not the entire number.
D) Incorrect conversion; 1A in hexadecimal is not equal to 31 in decimal.

A) INSERT is used to add new records to a table.
B) DELETE is used to remove records from a table.
C) UPDATE is used to modify existing records in a table.

Assigning subnet addresses
Overall explanation
B) DHCP is used for dynamically assigning IP addresses to devices in a network, eliminating the need for

Incorrect Answers:

by DHCP.
C) DNS addresses are resolved by DNS servers, not by DHCP.
D) Subnet addresses are typically configured manually or through network design, not dynamically by 
DHCP.

system.

period of time.

Incorrect Answers:
A) Debuggers find and fix errors but are not involved in the compilation process.
C) IDEs provide a development environment but are not directly responsible for code compilation.
D) VCS is used for version control and collaboration, not for compiling code into executable forms.

C) XSS attacks involve injecting malicious scripts into web pages but are not related to capturing and

1 MB = 1000 KB (in decimal)
1 MB = 1024 KB (in binary)
Incorrect Answers:

D) D) Administrator accounts have elevated privileges and are not designed to restrict access; 
rather, they have broader control.

Question 70 Correct

Your answer is correct
Records changes in a log for recovery after system crashes
Enforces file naming rules and restrictions
Facilitates real -time encryption of file data
Overall explanation
B) Journaling records changes in a log for recovery after system crashes. It helps maintain data 
integrity and allows the system to recover to a consistent state in case of failures.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Journaling is not designed for providing compression but for tracking changes.
C) Enforcing file naming rules is a different aspect of file management, not related to journaling.
D) Journaling focuses on tracking changes and recovery, not real -time encryption.

Your answer is correct

Float
   Comptia ITF+ Page 366    
Float
Overall explanation

Incorrect Answers:
A) String could technically represent this state with values like "on" or "off," but it's less 
efficient and would require unnecessary string comparison.
B) Integer could be used (e.g., 0 for off, 1 for on), but it's less clear than a boolean and allows 
for a broader range of values than needed.
D) Float is designed to hold decimal numbers and is not suitable for binary states.
Question 5 Correct

devices like hard drives or SSDs, not the CPU.
Incorrect Answers:

(Arithmetic Logic Unit).
D) Decoding instruction sets to understand what actions to perform is another crucial role of 
the CPU.

trademarks in multiple jurisdictions, and famous trademarks may receive broader protection.

Question 8 Correct
After troubleshooting a printer that wasn’t printing for multiple users, you discover that the 
issue was due to an IP conflict between the printer and another device on the network. After 
resolving the IP conflict, what should you do next?
Your answer is correct
Document the resolution process and outcomes.
Send an email to all staff explaining how to avoid such conflicts.
Monitor the printer usage for the next few days.
Conduct a training session on network management for the users.
Overall explanation
A) Documentation is crucial in troubleshooting to ensure that the solution and the problem are 
recorded for future reference. This can help prevent future occurrences and aid others who 
might encounter similar issues.
Incorrect Answers:
B) While educating staff might help prevent future issues, it is not the immediate next step 
after resolving an IP conflict. The priority is to ensure that the solution is effective and well -
documented before addressing broader preventative measures.
C) Monitoring the printer could help confirm the solution's effectiveness, but it is secondary to 
documenting the resolution. Continuous monitoring isn't necessary unless the initial solution 
fails or the problem recurs, making it less immediate than documenting the fix.
D) This option, while potentially beneficial in a broad educational context, is not a direct or 
necessary follow -up to resolving a specific technical issue like an IP conflict. Training sessions 
are resource -intensive and generally planned as part of broader IT strategy, not as an 
immediate response to individual troubleshooting outcomes.

Question 13 Correct
After a sudden power outage, the IT manager at a graphic design company needs to assess

type of storage would most likely retain data despite the power outage?
RAM
Your answer is correct
SSD
CPU cache
   Comptia ITF+ Page 371    
CPU cache
GPU memory
Overall explanation
B) SSDs are a type of non -volatile storage, meaning they retain data even when the power is 
turned off, unlike RAM or cache memory, which are volatile and lose their content without 
power.
Incorrect Answers:
A) RAM is volatile storage and loses its data without power.
C) CPU cache is a type of volatile memory designed for speed, not data permanence, and loses 
its content when power is lost.
D) GPU memory, similar to RAM, is volatile and loses its information without power.

controls.

Your answer is incorrect

Correct answer
IMAP

Incorrect Answers:
A & B) HTTP and HTTPS are protocols used for web browsing, not email access.
C) POP3 (Post Office Protocol) downloads emails to a single device, not ideal for accessing 
emails from multiple devices.

Question 19 Correct

routers support to best meet these requirements?

802.11b
802.11g
Your answer is correct
802.11ac
Overall explanation
D) The 802.11ac standard supports higher data rates, improved capacity, and better 
performance in dense environments compared to older standards. It is also backward 
compatible with 802.11b/g/n, ensuring that older devices can still connect.
Incorrect Answers:
A) 802.11a provides faster speeds than 802.11b/g but operates only on the 5GHz band, which 
might not be supported by all older devices.
B) 802.11b is widely compatible but offers the slowest speeds, which could lead to a poor user 
experience.
C) 802.11g is better than 802.11b and supports speeds up to 54 Mbps, but it is not as efficient 
as 802.11ac in handling multiple connections.

Question 21 Correct

To install more applications on the same hardware
Your answer is correct
To allow each application to use its own operating system
To improve graphical performance for user interfaces
To decrease the physical footprint of the server hardware
Overall explanation
B) A Type 1 hypervisor provides the capability to run multiple virtual machines each with its

environment requirements without additional hardware expense.
Incorrect Answers:

separation of environments, not merely the quantity of applications.

D) While virtualization can lead to reduced physical footprint, the direct benefit here is 
operational flexibility and environment isolation.

Question 31 Incorrect
A company's server experiences frequent unexpected shutdowns due to power failures. To 
minimize data loss and ensure the integrity of file operations, the IT department is considering 
upgrading the file system. Which feature should the IT department look for in a new file system 
to meet their needs?
Your answer is incorrect
Compression
Encryption
Correct answer
Journaling
Archiving
Overall explanation
C) Journaling is a feature in file systems designed to keep track of changes not yet committed to 
the main file system. In case of a system crash or power failure, journaling helps in recovering 
corrupt files by replaying these changes.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Compression: While useful for saving space, it doesn’t help in recovering data after crashes.
B) Encryption: Important for security, but does not aid in data recovery post -crash.
D) Archiving: Useful for data storage and backups but doesn't help with active file system 
recovery.

Question 33 Incorrect
You are assisting a user who is experiencing performance issues with their computer. The user 
mentions that the computer takes a long time to boot up and frequently freezes when multiple 
applications are open. Which of the following actions should you recommend to improve the 
computer's performance?
Your answer is incorrect
Install additional RAM
Upgrade the CPU
Correct answer
Replace the hard drive with an SSD
Reinstall the operating system
Overall explanation
C) Replacing the traditional hard drive with a solid -state drive (SSD) is one of the most effective 
ways to improve a computer's performance. SSDs offer faster read and write speeds compared 
to traditional hard disk drives (HDDs), resulting in quicker boot times and improved overall 
system responsiveness.
Incorrect Answers:
A) While adding more RAM can improve multitasking performance, it may not address the 
underlying issue of slow disk access times.
B) Upgrading the CPU may improve processing performance but is unlikely to address the 
specific issues related to slow boot times and application freezes.
D) Reinstalling the operating system should be considered as a last resort and may not 
necessarily improve performance if the underlying hardware is the bottleneck. Additionally, it 
can be time -consuming and may result in data loss if not done correctly.

Question 36 Correct
You're at the help desk and a user complains their computer monitor flickers constantly, 
making it difficult to work. They haven't downloaded any new software recently and haven't 
noticed any other unusual behavior with the computer. What would be your FIRST 
troubleshooting step to diagnose the issue?
Recommend a software update to fix potential bugs.
Your answer is correct
Check for loose connections with the monitor cable.
Run a virus scan to detect any malware.
Restart the computer to see if the flickering resolves.
Overall explanation
B) While outdated software or viruses could potentially cause visual problems, a loose 
connection with the monitor cable is a more likely cause of constant flickering.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Recommend a software update: Software updates can sometimes resolve display issues, but 
flickering is a less common symptom. Checking for physical problems is a more efficient first 
step.
C) Run a virus scan: Viruses can impact performance, but flickering isn't a typical symptom. A 
virus scan is a good practice, but not the most relevant initial step for this issue.
D) Restart the computer: A restart can sometimes resolve temporary glitches, but it's not as 
likely to fix a hardware issue like a loose cable.

D) Load balancing of network traffic is a feature often implemented by routers or specialized

Question 38 Correct
During a business conference call using VoIP technology, participants experience a delay 
between when someone speaks and when others hear it. What term describes this issue?
Data transfer rate.
Signal strength.
Your answer is correct
Latency.
Security protocol.
Overall explanation
C) Latency in VoIP communication refers to the delay between a speaker's message and the 
time it is heard by the listener. High latency can disrupt the flow of conversation, making it 
difficult for participants to interact naturally and promptly.
Incorrect Answers:
A) While related to overall network performance, data transfer rate does not specifically 
describe the delay in hearing speech.
B) Signal strength affects the quality of connection but is distinct from the temporal delay 
described by latency.
D) Security protocols secure data transmission but do not address delays in the delivery of voice 
packets.

Your answer is correct

access control.

Question 45 Correct
John, a junior programmer, is writing code for a project and needs to declare variables to store 
different types of data. He wants to ensure that his variable names are valid and adhere to 
naming conventions. Which programming concept should John consider when choosing names 
for his variables?
Your answer is correct
Identifiers
Constants
Data types
Keywords
Overall explanation
A) Identifiers in programming are names given to variables, functions, classes, or other entities 
in a program. When choosing names for variables, John should consider identifiers and adhere 
to naming conventions, such as using descriptive names, starting with a letter or underscore, 
and avoiding reserved keywords, to ensure that his variable names are valid and meaningful 
within the context of his code.
Incorrect Answers:
B) Constants are variables whose values cannot be changed during program execution and are 
not directly related to choosing names for variables like identifiers.
C) Data types define the type of data that variables can store but are not directly related to 
choosing names for variables like identifiers.
D) Keywords are reserved words in a programming language that have special meanings and 
cannot be used as identifiers but are not directly related to choosing names for variables like 
identifiers.

   Comptia ITF+ Page 389

match the performance optimization capabilities of assembly language due to their higher level

C) JavaScript is primarily used for web applications and lacks the capability for deep hardware 
optimization.
D) Java is designed to be platform -independent, which typically prevents it from achieving the 
level of performance optimization possible with assembly language.
Question 47 Correct
Lisa is developing a banking application that allows users to perform various transactions, such 
as deposits, withdrawals, and transfers. What programming concept should Lisa use to define 
actions like depositing money, withdrawing money, and transferring funds within her code?
Properties
Attributes
Your answer is correct
Methods
Objects
Overall explanation
C) Lisa should use methods to define actions like depositing money, withdrawing money, and 
transferring funds within her banking application. Methods are functions associated with 
objects that perform specific actions or behaviors. By defining methods for each transaction 
type, Lisa can implement the logic for performing the respective actions within her application.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Properties are variables associated with objects that represent characteristics or attributes 
and are not used to define actions or behaviors like depositing, withdrawing, or transferring 
funds.
B) Attributes are synonymous with properties in object -oriented programming and represent 
characteristics or features of objects, not actions or behaviors.
D) Objects represent instances of classes or templates that encapsulate data and behavior, 
including properties, attributes, and methods, but are not specifically used to define actions or 
behaviors like depositing, withdrawing, or transferring funds.
Question 48 Incorrect
   Comptia ITF+ Page 390    
Sarah is setting up a new network for her office and needs to assign unique identifiers to each 
device on the network. Which protocol should Sarah use for assigning IP addresses to devices?
UDP
Your answer is incorrect
TCP
Correct answer
DHCP
DNS
Overall explanation
C) Sarah should use the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) for assigning IP addresses 
to devices on her network. DHCP allows devices to obtain IP addresses automatically from a 
DHCP server, simplifying network management and ensuring that each device has a unique 
identifier.
Incorrect Answers:
A) UDP (User Datagram Protocol) and B) TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) are transport layer 
protocols used for data transmission but do not assign IP addresses to devices.
D) DNS (Domain Name System) is a protocol used for translating domain names to IP addresses 
and vice versa but is not used for assigning IP addresses to devices.

Upload the document to a public cloud storage service and share the link.
Your answer is correct
Use a password -protected file and email it.

Overall explanation

document, making it unreadable without the password.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Attaching directly to email offers no encryption and anyone with access to the email could 
potentially view the document.
B) Public cloud storage can be insecure, and anyone with the link could access the document.
D) While some instant messaging platforms offer encryption, it's important to verify the specific 
service and encryption strength used. Email is a more common and accepted method for 
document sharing.
Question 51 Incorrect
Mark is a system administrator responsible for managing file access permissions on a shared 
drive. He receives a request from Sarah, a team leader, to grant read -only access to a 
confidential project folder for her team members. Which type of access control mechanism 
should Mark implement to fulfill Sarah's request?
Mandatory access control (MAC)
Correct answer
Discretionary access control (DAC)
Role -based access control (RBAC)
Your answer is incorrect
Attribute -based access control (ABAC)
Overall explanation
B) Mark should implement discretionary access control (DAC) to fulfill Sarah's request. DAC 
allows owners of resources, such as files or folders, to determine who has access and what type 
of access they have. In this scenario, Sarah, as the owner of the project folder, can grant read -
only access to her team members based on her discretion.
   Comptia ITF+ Page 392    
only access to her team members based on her discretion.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Mandatory access control (MAC) is a more restrictive access control mechanism typically 
used in high -security environments where access decisions are based on system -wide policies 
rather than the discretion of resource owners.
C) Role -based access control (RBAC) assigns permissions based on predefined roles within an 
organization and may not give Sarah the flexibility to grant access to specific individuals based 
on her discretion.
D) Attribute -based access control (ABAC) evaluates a set of attributes associated with the user, 
resource, and environment to make access decisions, but it may not provide the fine -grained 
control needed in this scenario.
Question 52 Correct
Mark is configuring a firewall to restrict access to a company's internal network. He wants to 
allow access to specific services from trusted IP addresses only. Which access control 
mechanism should Mark use to implement these restrictions?
Discretionary access control (DAC)
Role -based access control (RBAC)
Attribute -based access control (ABAC)
Your answer is correct
Rule -based access control (RuBAC)
Overall explanation
D) Mark should use rule -based access control (RuBAC) to configure the firewall rules. RuBAC 
allows administrators to define specific rules based on conditions such as source IP addresses, 
destination ports, and protocols to control access to network resources.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Discretionary access control (DAC) allows resource owners to determine access permissions, 
but it is not typically used for configuring firewall rules.
B) Role -based access control (RBAC) assigns permissions based on predefined roles within an 
organization and is not typically used for configuring network firewall rules.
C) Attribute -based access control (ABAC) evaluates a set of attributes associated with the user, 
resource, and environment to make access decisions, but it is not typically used for configuring 
network firewall rules.
Question 53 Incorrect
Following a critical database corruption, an IT team needs to restore the database from the 
most recent dump. What is the first action they should take?
Your answer is incorrect
Analyze the log files to identify the cause of the corruption.
   Comptia ITF+ Page 393    
Analyze the log files to identify the cause of the corruption.
Immediately start the database dump import to minimize downtime
Correct answer
Verify the integrity of the database dump file before restoring.
Rebuild the database structure manually before importing the dump.
Overall explanation
C) Verifying the integrity of the database dump file before restoration is a critical step to ensure 
that the data is intact and the file is not corrupted. By performing integrity checks, such as 
checksum validation or using database management system tools to verify the dump file, the IT 
team can prevent potential issues during the restoration process. This verification step helps 
maintain data integrity and minimizes the risk of further complications.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Analyzing log files is an essential step in understanding the root cause of the database 
corruption. However, in the immediate aftermath of a critical database corruption, the top 
priority is to restore the database to minimize downtime and ensure business continuity.
B) While minimizing downtime is crucial, blindly starting the database dump import without 
verifying the integrity of the dump file can lead to further complications. If the dump file is 
corrupted or incomplete, it could result in data loss or database inconsistencies. Therefore, it's 
essential to ensure the reliability of the dump file before proceeding with the restoration 
process.
D) Rebuilding the database structure manually is unnecessary if the dump includes the full 
database structure, which is typically the case. It would be an inefficient use of resources and 
could introduce human error. Additionally, it may prolong the downtime unnecessarily.

Question 58 Incorrect
You are tasked with making structural changes to a table in your organization's database. Which 
SQL command should you use to modify the structure of the table by adding or removing 
columns?
   Comptia ITF+ Page 396    
columns?
Correct answer
ALTER TABLE
Your answer is incorrect
UPDATE TABLE
MODIFY TABLE
CHANGE TABLE
Overall explanation
A) The ALTER TABLE command in SQL is used to modify the structure of a table, such as adding, 
removing, or modifying columns. It allows you to make structural changes to the table schema.
Incorrect Answers:
B) The UPDATE command in SQL is indeed used to modify existing records within a table, not 
the structure of the table itself. It allows you to change the values of one or more columns in 
existing rows based on specified conditions.
C) While the idea of "modifying" a table is similar to what we want to achieve, there's no direct 
SQL command named MODIFY TABLE for altering the structure of a table. ALTER TABLE is the 
standard command for such operations.
D) There's no SQL command named CHANGE TABLE for altering the structure of a table. This 
option might sound plausible due to its resemblance to the concept of changing the table 
structure, but ALTER TABLE is the correct command for this task in SQL.
Question 59 Incorrect
You are concerned about malware infections and unauthorized access to your computer's files 
and resources. Which type of firewall should you configure to prevent malicious software from 
accessing your computer's network services?
Your answer is incorrect
Network -based firewall
Hardware firewall
Correct answer
Host -based firewall
Application firewall
Overall explanation
C) Host -based firewalls operate at the individual computer level, providing a layer of defense 
directly on the device itself. By configuring a host -based firewall, you're essentially setting up a 
barrier around your computer's network services, allowing you to control which incoming and 
outgoing connections are permitted based on predefined rules.
Incorrect Answers:
   Comptia ITF+ Page 397    
A) network -based firewalls are positioned at the network perimeter, often within routers or 
switches, and are responsible for monitoring and filtering traffic as it enters or exits the 
network. While these firewalls are crucial for protecting against external threats, they operate 
at a higher level than individual computers, focusing on traffic flowing across the entire 
network rather than specific devices.
B) Hardware firewall is a dedicated firewall device installed between the internal network and 
the internet to filter network traffic, but it is not installed on individual computers.
D) Application firewall is a type of firewall that monitors and controls network traffic specific to 
individual applications or services, not at the host level.

does not directly address compatibility with hardware devices.

Question 63 Correct

created. To prevent others from using your algorithm without permission, what type of

Trademark
Copyright
Your answer is correct
Patent
Trade secret
Overall explanation
C) Patents protect inventions, including algorithms and functional aspects of technology. By 
obtaining a patent for your unique algorithm, you can prevent others from using or 
implementing it without your permission, providing legal protection for your intellectual 
property.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Trademarks protect brand names, logos, and other distinctive marks, but they do not cover 
algorithms or functional aspects of technology.
B) Copyright protects creative works like software code, but it does not directly cover the 
functional aspects of algorithms or inventions.
D) Trade secrets protect confidential information, but once the algorithm is incorporated into a 
publicly available software application, it can no longer be considered a trade secret.

Question 65 Correct

Enable full disk encryption on all company computers
Your answer is correct
Use a secure file transfer protocol like SFTP or HTTPS
Implement access controls on the internal network
Encrypt the data at rest on the company's servers
Overall explanation
B) To protect data in transit during transmission over a network like the internet, it is essential 
to use secure protocols such as SFTP (Secure File Transfer Protocol) or HTTPS (Hypertext 
Transfer Protocol Secure). These protocols encrypt the data in transit, preventing unauthorized 
access and ensuring the confidentiality and integrity of the transmitted data.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Full disk encryption protects data at rest on the computer's storage devices but does not 
directly secure data in transit during transmission.
C) Access controls on the internal network help restrict unauthorized access within the 
organization but do not provide security for data in transit over external networks like the 
internet.
D) Encrypting data at rest on the company's servers protects the stored data but does not 
secure the data during transmission to the partner company.

Overall explanation

corruption. Enabling journaling is a recommended practice when working with critical

contribute to data integrity or prevent accidental data loss.
D) Password protection can help restrict access to sensitive documents, but it does not provide 
a mechanism for tracking changes or recovering from data loss.

Question 75 Correct
A system administrator receives multiple service tickets reporting various technical issues, 
including network connectivity issues, software crashes, and printer malfunctions. Which issue 
should the administrator prioritize for immediate resolution?

Software crashes occurring on individual computers
Printer malfunctions affecting a single department
Your answer is correct

resolution as they have the potential to disrupt business operations for a larger number of 
users compared to individual software crashes or printer malfunctions.
Incorrect Answers:
A) Software updates for non -critical applications can be scheduled for later installation and do 
not require immediate resolution compared to network connectivity issues affecting multiple 
users.
B) Software crashes occurring on individual computers may impact productivity, but they 
typically affect fewer users and can be addressed after resolving network connectivity issues.
C) Printer malfunctions affecting a single department may be important, but they are localized 
issues that can be addressed after resolving network connectivity issues affecting multiple 
users. 
   Comptia ITF+ Page 407    
1. Computer Operating Systems
Definition : A computer OS provides a common environment for software execution, linking users 
with hardware.•
Types :
Workstation, Server, Mobile, Embedded (Firmware), Hypervisor.••
Key Functions :
HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer), Drivers, Logging/Auditing, Permissions/Access Control, 
Multitasking, Network Services.••
Examples :
Microsoft Windows: Uses NTFS for file management, Task Manager for process 
management.•
Apple macOS: Uses HFS+/APFS file systems, Activity Monitor for process management.•
GNU Linux: Uses EXT file systems, System Monitor for processes.••
2. Applications and Software
General Concepts :
Single -Platform Software : Runs on a single OS (e.g., Windows -only apps). •
Cross -Platform Software : Runs on multiple platforms (e.g., Linux, macOS). •
Licensing Types : Open Source (e.g., MIT, GNU GPL), Proprietary. ••
Business Software :
Project Management (e.g., SmartSheet), Accounting Software (e.g., QuickBooks).•
Application Delivery Models : Locally Installed, Cloud Hosted. ••
Programming Concepts :
Languages : Interpreted (e.g., Python), Compiled (e.g., C++), Query Languages (e.g., SQL). •
Key Elements : Variables, Functions, Loops, Arrays. ••
3. Database Fundamentals
What is a Database? : A structured repository of information for quick access. •
Database Capabilities : Input data, output data (querying), data persistence. •
Relational Database Design :
Primary Key : Uniquely identifies records in a table. •
Foreign Key : Links records between tables. ••
SQL Command Examples :
Create a Table : CREATE TABLE USStates (state_name NVARCHAR(50), 
state_abr NCHAR(2));•
Query Data : SELECT * FROM USStates ORDER BY state_name; ••
4. Networking Concepts
OSI Model :
Layers 1 to 7: Physical, Data -Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. •
Local Devices : Switches, access points. •
Remote Devices : Routers, firewalls. ••
Network Types :
Wired : Ethernet (RJ -45). •
Wireless : Wi-Fi standards (802.11a/b/g/n/ac), Bluetooth, NFC. ••
Security Practices :
Wireless Security : WPA2 for highest encryption. •
Captive Portals : Used in public Wi -Fi for user authentication. ••
5. Security Concepts
Encryption :
Plain Text vs. Cipher Text : Plain text is human -readable; cipher text is encrypted. ••Study Guide
Friday, October 11, 2024 2:25 PM
   Comptia ITF+ Page 408    
Plain Text vs. Cipher Text : Plain text is human -readable; cipher text is encrypted. •
Encryption Methods : Use keys/algorithms to scramble and unscramble messages. •
Behavioral Security :
Privacy Concerns : Important when using social networking, email, file -sharing, and instant 
messaging.•
Password Management : Importance of password expiration, password history, and using 
password managers.••
Wireless Network Security :
WPA2 : The most secure Wi -Fi encryption standard. •
Captive Portals : Ensures users agree to terms before accessing a network. ••
6. Hardware and Infrastructure
CPU : The brain of the computer, available in 32 -bit and 64 -bit architectures. •
Storage Devices : SSDs (Solid State Drives) vs. HDDs (Hard Disk Drives), volatile (e.g., RAM) and 
non-volatile memory (e.g., SSDs).•
Peripheral Devices : USB, FireWire, Thunderbolt for connectivity; drivers for installation and 
management.•
Display Technologies : VGA, HDMI, DisplayPort for video output. •
7. Software Development Concepts
Types of Programming Languages :
Interpreted Languages : JavaScript, Python. •
Compiled Languages : C++, Rust. •
Query Languages : SQL for database queries. ••
Organizational Techniques :
Flowcharts : Useful for visualizing logic before coding. •
Pseudocode : Simplifies the logic of the code before translating to a specific programming 
language.••

Question #1 of 75 QuestionId #1257377
Which of the following is the fastest throughput unit?
A)Mbps
B)Kbps
C)bps
D)Gbps
Explanation
The fastest throughput listed is gigabits per second (Gbps). bps represents the number of bits (0 or 1) that are 
transmitted per second. One gigabit is 1,073.741,821 (or one billion) bits.
Throughput is commonly abbreviated as follows, from slowest to fastest:
bits per second bps
bytes per second Bps
kilobits per second Kbps
kilobytes per second KBps
megabits per second Mbps
megabytes per second MBps
gigabits per second Gbps
One Mbps would represent 1,048,576 (or one million) bits transmitted per second.
One Kbps would represent 1,024 (or one thousand) bits transmitted per second.
The following table illustrates the relationship between the numbers in bytes (for example, 1,024 bytes is 1 
kilobyte):
1,024 Kilo Thousand
1,048,576 Mega Million 1,024 Kilos
1,073,741,824 Giga Billion 1,024 Megas
1,099,511,627,776 Tera Trillion 1,024 Gigas
1,125,899.906.842.620 Peta Quadrillion 1,024 Teras
Objective:
IT Concepts and Terminology
Sub-Objective:
Compare and contrast common units of measure.
References:
CompTIA IT Fundamentals+ FC0 -U61 Cert Guide Premium Edition, Chapter 6: Common Units of Measure: 
Storage, Throughput, and Speed:

Explanation
A foreign key allows you to find associated data in a master or parent table.
Foreign keys are not required; they are optional. Foreign keys do not need to be unique.

Question #3 of 75 QuestionId #1577514
When using the Internet, which of the following areas should concern you about your expectation of privacy? 
(Choose all that apply.)
A)When using social networking sites
B)When using email
C)When using file sharing applications
D)While instant messaging
Explanation
When using the Internet, you should be concerned about your expectation of privacy when using social 
networking sites, when using email, when using file sharing applications, and while instant messaging.
Social networking sites own your data. Even when you have an account, anything you post on a social 
networking site is their property. Even when you delete your post, that post remains on the social networking 
site’s server.
Email privacy can be divided into two areas: the routing information and the content. The routing 
information (the “From” address, the outgoing mail server, the “To” address, and the incoming mail server) 
must be public in order to deliver the message. The content (or body of the email and attachments) should 
have a reasonable expectation of privacy until it is delivered to the recipient. The sender loses all control over 
the content when it is delivered, and the recipient may share it with whomever they please. With that said, 
users of corporate networks forfeit their right to privacy when they have to agree to an Acceptable Use Policy 
(AUP), often including a statement that the user’s email is monitored. Users of free public Wi -Fi at coffee 
shops, airports, and hotels must often first log onto a captive portal and agree to the location’s AUP before 
proceeding. The following exhibit shows a captive portal.
File sharing applications may be a convenient method to download software, music, and video, but there is a 
significant danger of malware infection and loss of confidential information. You should not have any 
expectation of privacy when using file sharing applications.
Instant messaging can have significant privacy issues. The instant messages are often stored on the provider’s 
server or on the local computer. Also, a recipient may decide to save a copy (through a screen shot) to use 
later, particularly if the content can embarrass the sender or if there is financial gain if the material is 
released to certain publishers.
Objective:
Security
Sub-Objective:
Summarize behavioral security concepts.
References:
CompTIA IT Fundamentals+ FC0 -U61 Cert Guide Premium Edition, Chapter 30: Summarize Behavioral Security 
Concepts
